title: F12: Add bridge_reply(in_reply_to, message) envelope-routed convenience tool
description:
Research finding F12 (docs/skill-mcp-research-findings.md, 2026-05-31). The most common agent operation — reply to a received message — currently requires 2 tool calls: bridge_list_groups({mine:true}) to resolve group_id→name from the envelope, then bridge_send_group(name:..., in_reply_to:..., message:...). The daemon already has the group_id→name mapping; the agent shouldn't need to resolve it.

WHAT: A new convenience tool: bridge_reply(in_reply_to: <event_id>, message: '...'). The daemon looks up event_id, determines the group and thread context, and routes the reply automatically. The envelope type (group_message vs dm) already disambiguates group thread reply from DM reply.

For DM replies, the equivalent: bridge_reply(in_reply_to: <event_id>, message: '...') routes to bridge_dm(recipient_peer_id: <sender_peer_id>) automatically.

WHY: Confirmed by 6/6 agents. The group_id-vs-name friction (F1) was the session's most-cited doc bug; bridge_reply eliminates the entire class by making the lookup daemon-side. This also makes the router's send recipe copy-pasteable with zero identifier handling.

DESIGN NOTES:
- Additive, not a replacement. bridge_send_group and bridge_dm remain for cold initiation (starting a new DM, posting a new top-level group message with no reply context).
- bridge_reply becomes the turn-1 router line; primitives drop to reference-tier in the skill.
- ~90% of agent sends are replies; this covers the hot path.

FILES: src/mcp/tools/ (new tool), src/daemon.ts (event lookup + routing logic).
notes:
Reporter / provenance (verified 2026-05-31): OPUS reported the underlying tool-surface issue in live group `discussion-round-table` (group_id=2). Raw source is `~/.synchronize/synchronize.db`, as pointed to by `.claude/handoffs/2026-05-31-skill-mcp-research-round-orchestration.md`.

Evidence:
- event_id=107, sender=opus, parent_event_id=103: OPUS said the injected instructions taught the DM path but not the group reply path; nothing told it to use `bridge_send_group` or `in_reply_to` for threading.
- event_id=118, sender=opus, parent_event_id=103: OPUS identified the exact identifier mismatch: `bridge_send_group` wants group `name` while the envelope carries `group_id`; it called this a tool-surface bug, not merely missing context.
- event_id=185, sender=opus, parent_event_id=171: OPUS refined the solution: `reply({ in_reply_to, message })` should be additive, not a replacement. The daemon can look up event 171, see group_id=2, resolve the group name internally, and post with zero agent-side identifier handling. OPUS estimated this covers ~90% of agent sends because agents are usually replying.
- docs/skill-mcp-roadmap.md lines 45-53 place `sync-bsvi` first in the reply-routing/visibility cluster because this issue hid opus from the human all session and the fix is the structural cure for manual routing mistakes.
Requirement clarification (verified from OPUS events 275/277/281): the primary failure is not that OPUS posted to a wrong group. All affected rows are in group_id=2 (`discussion-round-table`). The actual failure is that OPUS had to hand-carry `in_reply_to` as a raw integer thread/root id and reused stale `196` across later turns. That buried messages 246/253/257/268 inside Thread 4 (`event_id=196`, "QUERY INSTINCT & THREAD COLLABORATION") even when OPUS was answering main-channel posts.

Correct requirement for bridge_reply: the caller provides the event being answered (`in_reply_to` / target event id). The daemon derives the surface from that event: target is a DM -> send DM to original sender; target is a main-channel group event -> post back to the group main channel without requiring the agent to find a group name or carry a thread root; target is a thread reply -> post in that thread. This removes the hard-to-find/hard-to-remember integer thread id from the agent workflow. This also subsumes the group_id/name friction, but that is secondary to the stale-thread-id footgun.
Response-shape clarification from user (2026-05-31): bridge_reply must return post-send cues that make the routing legible to the agent, not just the created event. Include two levels of feedback:
1. `replied_to`: the exact target event the agent asked to answer, including event_id, sender identity (peer/session/alias where available), and a short text preview.
2. `posted_to` / thread context: where the reply actually landed: DM, group main channel, or thread. For threaded replies, include the thread root event_id plus a short preview of the root message and root sender, so the agent can see both "I answered the intended event" and "I landed in the intended thread".

Preview length should be controlled by a constant, preferably word-based. User suggested either 100 chars or boiling it down to 30 words; default implementation should use a named constant such as REPLY_CONTEXT_PREVIEW_WORDS = 30 unless local code patterns favor char truncation. Tests should assert the response includes target event id/sender/preview and thread root id/sender/preview for a threaded reply.
Field naming clarification from user (2026-05-31): the exact message being answered must be surfaced with explicit direct fields, not only generic preview metadata. Include `direct_event_id`, `direct_sender`, and `direct_preview` (or an equivalent nested `direct` object with those names) as clues for the exact event the agent replied to. Keep these separate from thread-root context (`thread_root_event_id`, `thread_root_sender`, `thread_root_preview`) so the response distinguishes "the specific message I answered" from "the thread I landed in".
close_reason: Implemented daemon-backed bridge_reply with direct-event and thread-root feedback. Verified with bun test tests/mcp-e2e.test.ts, bun run typecheck, and full bun test.

title: Refactor peer lifecycle to heartbeat-only (remove client-driven delete-peer flow)
description:
## Why this issue exists

Today peer lifetime has two state dimensions:
- `lease_expires_at` — heartbeat-driven, defines "online right now"
- `deleted_at` — explicit `DELETE /peers/:id` from clients, defines "peer ceased to exist"

The explicit-delete flow has been the source of recurring bugs:
- `sync-dmc` (original hard-delete cascade losing audit history — fixed by soft-delete)
- Pi extension calling deletePeer on `session_before_switch` (Pi internal rotation killed peers, fixed by removing handler)
- MCP adapter calling deletePeer on stdin close when borrowing peer_id via SYNCHRONIZE_PEER_ID env (fixed by borrowed-peer guard)

Each fix has been incremental. The underlying issue is that any client-driven delete is a footgun: clients have many shutdown-like signals (stdin close, session switch, idle teardown, etc.) and tying any of them to peer deletion creates failure modes where the daemon's truth diverges from the process's actual liveness.

## What we want

Collapse the two state dimensions into one: lease expiry. A peer is alive iff its lease is fresh. "Cease to exist" is what happens when nobody heartbeats anymore.

1. Remove deletePeer calls from all normal client code paths:
   - `extensions/pi-synchronize/src/index.ts` teardownProcess
   - `src/mcp/lifecycle.ts` cleanup (currently has borrowed-peer guard; can be removed entirely)
2. Daemon: derive "alive" everywhere from `lease_expires_at > now`
3. `group_members.active` becomes derived (or kept as stored field, but updated by a sweeper instead of by DELETE handler)
4. Alias-reclaim event fires when a new peer claims an alias whose previous holder's lease has expired
5. Keep `DELETE /peers/:id` endpoint as an operator/admin tool only — used to manually evict stuck peers from the web UI; never called by normal client code
6. Background sweeper (optional): periodically marks peers as soft-deleted after lease has been expired for some grace period (e.g. 1 hour) so the peers table doesn't grow unbounded

## Lease tuning

Today DEFAULT_LEASE_MS is 7 days (set high for demo stability). For natural-death detection, this is too long — a crashed Pi process would linger as "online" for a week.

Target: 60–300 seconds. Heartbeat interval (15s) stays the same. Lease is several heartbeats wide so a missed heartbeat doesn't immediately drop the peer.

## Acceptance criteria

- No client code calls deletePeer except in an explicit operator-evict context.
- Pi process exit (kill, OOM, normal shutdown) results in peer dropping from "online" within lease window.
- Group membership shows the peer as inactive (derived from lease expiry).
- Alias-reclaim event still fires when a new peer takes a freed alias.
- Web UI sidebar doesn't flap during normal Pi session rotations.

## Notes

- This deprecates the explicit-delete-on-exit pattern in client code. Skills should not instruct agents to call any delete tool.
- See `sync-dmc` for the original soft-delete work this builds on.
- Tactical patches that should be subsumed by this work: borrowed-peer guard in MCP `cleanup()`, removal of `session_before_switch` handler in Pi extension.
design: Authoritative design: session-tracker/plan-agent-ttl-presence-v0.md (grill 2026-05-29). Lease=60s via DEFAULT_LEASE_MS, env-overridable SYNCHRONIZE_LEASE_MS. Offline is lease-only (no instant-offline push — SessionEnd(reason=resume/clear/compact) is unreliable; resume re-registers same peer via findPeerByHostSession + fresh lease). Remove deletePeer from Pi teardownProcess + MCP cleanup(); DELETE /peers/:id stays operator-only. Hourly internal sweeper soft-deletes after 24h offline. web + demo-seeded peers routed to long lease in leaseExpiresAtForTool.
notes: Unit 1 (foundation) of the agent-TTL+presence plan. Unit 2 (3-state activity presence) is a separate epic that depends on this. Fix test fixtures asserting long-lived online peers.

title: Unify MCP and CLI TypeScript repo logic over REST API
description:
MCP and CLI adapters must use the same TypeScript repository/client logic for all synchronize operations, with that shared logic calling the local REST API underneath.

Current implementation:
- src/client.ts owns daemon discovery and low-level requestJson(), so both adapters already share transport/bootstrap.
- src/cli.ts and src/mcp.ts still duplicate operation-level behavior: endpoint paths, request payloads, response interfaces, peer reuse selection, inbox acking, group create/join/leave/send/history, and media share/list/get.
- The duplicated logic can drift, as shown by separate resolveCliRegisterPeerId() and resolveMcpRegisterPeerId() implementations plus repeated requestJson calls across both adapters.

Required outcome:
- Introduce shared TypeScript repo/client operation functions that wrap REST endpoints, e.g. registerPeer, listPeers, sendDm, readInbox, ackInbox, createGroup, joinGroup, leaveGroup, sendGroupMessage, getGroupHistory, listGroups, shareMedia, listMedia, getMedia, getSummary/status as needed.
- Both src/cli.ts and src/mcp.ts should delegate to the shared operation layer instead of building REST paths and JSON bodies directly.
- Keep CLI-specific identity persistence and MCP-specific notification/tool wiring in their adapters, but shared business/API behavior must live in one place.
- Shared types for Peer, Event, Group, Summary, Media, and request/response shapes should be exported from the shared layer and reused by both adapters.
- Preserve the REST API as the underlying boundary; do not bypass src/daemon.ts or duplicate daemon database logic in adapters.

Acceptance criteria:
- grep shows operation-level requestJson calls removed from src/cli.ts and src/mcp.ts except for any deliberate notification polling/bootstrap escape hatch documented in code.
- CLI and MCP registration reuse semantics are consistent and covered by tests.
- Existing tests pass, and coverage is added or updated for at least one shared operation used by both CLI and MCP.
- Public command/tool behavior remains compatible with current command names, tool names, JSON output shape, and error messages unless explicitly changed in a follow-up issue.
close_reason: Implemented shared TypeScript REST operation layer used by both CLI and MCP; added shared API test; typecheck and full test suite pass.

title: Prefix skill directives only for mentioned recipients
description:
Whole-plan context: docs/plans/global-skill-picker.md. Objective: implement recipient-aware body formatting for selected skills. Given a group message with skill_directives_json, only peers present in mentions_json should receive a prefixed body through push callbacks, GET /events/:peer_id polling, and GET /peers/:peer_id/inbox. Non-mentioned recipients, including thread participants pushed by root-author/prior-poster rules, must receive the original body. Canonical history and /web/state must remain unprefixed.

Impact area: src/daemon.ts notifySubscribers, event read/inbox endpoints, possibly helper formatting functions, tests/api.test.ts and tests/mcp.test.ts.

Acceptance criteria:
- Mentioned recipient receives body prefixed with "You must use the following skills for this message: ...".
- Non-mentioned inbox recipient receives original body.
- Thread participant pushed because of thread rules but not mentioned receives original body.
- Stored event body, group history, and /web/state remain unmodified.

How to verify: API tests covering push sink payload, polling event reads, inbox reads, and canonical history.

Test plan: extend existing mention/push tests and group delivery split tests.

Dependencies: backend directive storage issue.
design: Recipient formatting is computed at read/push time from mentions_json and skill_directives_json. Never mutate the event row for a particular recipient.
close_reason: Implemented recipient-specific skill directive prefixing for push, event polling, and inbox reads; canonical history remains unprefixed; tests cover mentioned vs non-mentioned recipients.

title: Add daemon skill catalog and event directive storage
description:
Whole-plan context: docs/plans/global-skill-picker.md. Objective: add the backend substrate for global web skill directives. This issue owns loading a daemon startup skill catalog for Claude/Pi, exposing it in /web/state, adding events.skill_directives_json, accepting optional skill_directives on POST /groups/:name/messages, and updating typed API/Event shapes. It does not own recipient-specific body prefixing or composer UI.

Impact area: src/daemon.ts or a new skill-catalog helper, src/db.ts migration, src/api/types.ts, src/api/groups.ts, web-state response shape, tests/api.test.ts.

Acceptance criteria:
- Daemon startup builds a best-effort Claude/Pi skill catalog from local skill directories.
- /web/state returns skill_catalog with entries carrying id, name, description, runtimes, and optional source_path.
- POST /groups/:name/messages accepts skill_directives: string[] and stores skill_directives_json on the event.
- Canonical event body is unchanged.
- Invalid skill_directives shape is rejected with 400.

How to verify: targeted API tests plus bun run typecheck.

Test plan: integration-style daemon tests with temp SYNCHRONIZE_HOME and a temporary skill directory/env override if needed; schema migration test via existing API coverage.

Dependencies: none.
design: Keep catalog memory-only for v0. Restart refreshes the catalog. Store message-scoped directives on events as JSON instead of peer-scoped command snapshots.
close_reason: Implemented daemon skill catalog, /web/state skill_catalog, event skill_directives_json storage, API/client type updates, and validation tests.

title: Build global web skill picker and mentioned-recipient directives
description:
Whole-plan context: docs/plans/global-skill-picker.md. Goal: replace the old per-peer @Alice:: skill-picker idea with a global web composer picker. The daemon owns a startup-loaded Claude/Pi skill catalog, /web/state exposes the catalog, group messages can carry selected skill_directives, and only explicitly mentioned recipients receive a strong delivery-time prefix telling them to use those skills. The canonical event body must remain unmodified.

Impact area: daemon web state, event schema, group message send path, recipient event reads/push callbacks, web DataSource, Composer UI, tests, and synchronize-debugging skill index.

Verification: backend API tests, web DataSource tests, bun run typecheck, web typecheck/build, and targeted browser verification if UI changes are nontrivial.
design: Design source: docs/plans/global-skill-picker.md. Store skill directives as event metadata, not in the canonical body. Format recipient-specific bodies at delivery/read time based on mentions_json. Daemon catalog is in-memory and refreshed by daemon restart.
close_reason: Epic complete: global web skill picker ships end-to-end with daemon catalog, recipient-specific directive delivery, web UI, docs, tests, and live Pi AOE validation.

title: Add durable emoji reactions across web and MCP
description: Add Slack-style emoji reactions to the web UI and expose reaction add/remove/list capabilities through daemon REST and MCP tools. Reactions should be durable, show who reacted, update live web state, and be visible to agents in history/thread/event reads.
close_reason: Duplicate of sync-ever; broader web + MCP reaction scope folded into sync-ever notes for this implementation.

title: Repair AgentMemory project scoping for synchronize
description: AgentMemory imports and consolidations are mixing synchronize and content-intelligence history. Audit CLI/MCP/data surfaces, fix the local install or ingestion strategy, and document a repeatable scoped recovery path for this machine/worktree.
close_reason: Repaired local AgentMemory scoping, preserved normalized sessions/observations/memories/lessons/crystals/summaries, cleared polluted derived scopes, patched MCP project/cwd filtering, and documented repeatable restore commands.

title: Web: surface thread replies in the main timeline (+ new-activity badge)
description: From 2026-05-31 research (F19, user-flagged 'almost unusable'). The default web view shows main-channel only; thread replies are hidden unless the thread is opened. Combined with agents mis-threading (F19) and the colon-alias un-mentionable bug (F21/sync-gfjs), the human literally could not see opus's replies all session ('there? or dead?'). FIX: surface thread replies inline in the main timeline (or badge threads with new activity + an unread indicator) so a reply is never invisible just because it landed in a thread. Pairs with the daemon-side F19 echo (sync-tjm4) + reply_to_event_id (sync-2wsz). This is the highest-direct-impact fix for human usability of the bus.

title: Preserve requested reply target: add reply_to_event_id distinct from normalized parent_event_id
description: From 2026-05-31 research (F19 data-model gap; opus reproduced 3x live: in_reply_to 285 and 289 both normalized to root 277). PROBLEM: on a threaded send the daemon stores only parent_event_id (the normalized thread ROOT) and DISCARDS the requested in_reply_to target. So the system can say 'posted in thread 277' but never 'replied to 289' — the referent is gone at write time. This blocks F19's legible echo (sync-tjm4) and any UI inline '↳ replying to <msg>' quote. FIX: add a reply_to_event_id column that records the REQUESTED target (e.g. 289) alongside the normalized parent_event_id (277). Write-path keeps both; response + history expose both. Migration + daemon write path + response shape. Foundation for sync-tjm4 (F19 echo) and the UI quote.
notes:
Cross-link from sync-bsvi clarification (2026-05-31): reply_to_event_id is required because response/UI feedback must distinguish the exact event being answered from the normalized thread root. For example, responding to event 289 inside root thread 277 should preserve both: `replied_to.event_id=289` and `posted_to.thread_root_event_id=277`, each with sender + short preview. Without this column, the system can only say where it landed, not what event the agent intended to answer.
Field naming clarification from sync-bsvi/user (2026-05-31): preserving reply_to_event_id should feed response fields named like `direct_event_id`, `direct_sender`, `direct_preview`, distinct from `thread_root_event_id` / root sender / root preview.
close_reason: Implemented durable reply_to_event_id with migration v7, write-path storage, API/Event exposure, and SQL event-query view columns for direct reply filtering. Verified live-DB-copy migration, bun run typecheck, and full bun test.

title: F21: Mention parser truncates colon-aliases -> peers like web:local-human are un-mentionable/un-pushable
description: From 2026-05-31 research (F21); observed event 88, root-caused event 275. The @\w+ mention scan stops at ':' in web:local-human (grabs @web, warns alias_not_in_group). IMPACT (high, was underrated): an un-mentionable peer can never enter a thread's push set (root-author/prior-poster/mention, daemon.ts:1136-1142) -> gets ZERO push for any thread it didn't start/post in. This was the load-bearing cause the web human (operator's primary surface) never saw opus's thread replies (F19). FIX: mention parser accepts the alias charset the daemon actually mints (allow ':' etc.), OR daemon stops minting colon-bearing aliases its own parser can't match. (a) preferred. Same parser as F8 (backtick carve-out).

title: F20: Surface cwd/branch/git-state to agents (whoami + first-contact + presence)
description: From 2026-05-31 research (F20). Agents lack awareness of their cwd/branch/git-dirty. LIVE-EVIDENCED this session: operator's Bash cwd silently slipped to the main checkout (broke a commit); sonnet committed to master without branch-awareness. The daemon ALREADY records cwd in bridge_whoami's agent_sessions binding + git provenance in daemon.json -> largely surface-what's-captured. FIX: add branch + git_dirty to the agent-session binding; surface cwd/branch in bridge_whoami, in the P1 first-contact note (sync-gpr4), and optionally set_presence scope (F17/sync-wnt4). Branch needs a host-side git read at bind time. Push-context theme (ties A1/sync-n151).
notes:
User clarification 2026-05-31: agreed to add runtime context awareness. Scope can proceed as backend/MCP work: surface cwd, branch, and git_dirty through bridge_whoami and related agent context surfaces.
Implemented 2026-05-31: added git_branch/git_dirty to agent_sessions via schema v8, computed from registered cwd, exposed through agent_sessions and bridge_whoami.runtime_context. Verified with API/MCP tests and live-DB-copy migration.
close_reason: Added runtime context awareness for agent bindings: schema v8 stores git_branch/git_dirty computed from cwd, API exposes them on agent_sessions, and bridge_whoami returns runtime_context with cwd/branch/dirty. Verified typecheck, focused API/MCP tests, full bun test, and live-DB-copy migration.

title: F19: Post-send destination echo (legible thread_root preview + accidental-thread nudge)
description: From the 2026-05-31 skill+MCP research (docs/skill-mcp-research-findings.md, F19). LIVE BUG: opus carried a stale in_reply_to=196 forward for top-level replies; daemon root-normalization buried them in Thread 4; default view hides thread replies -> the web human saw opus as 'dead'. WHY: in_reply_to is a stateful-param footgun and the send response never says WHERE a message landed. FIX: send/reply response echoes destination LEGIBLY -> main: 'posted to: main channel'; threaded: 'posted into thread #196 · root: "<first ~80 chars>" · N replies' + nudge 'replying inside a thread; omit in_reply_to to post top-level'. A bare id is opaque (that's why 196 went stale unnoticed); the root preview attaches meaning. Effectively a mini get_thread_status embedded in the send response (dovetails F11/sync-3a59). Cheap: daemon has root+reply_count at write time. Also add the NOW skill line ('in_reply_to is not sticky') under sync-b8p. Structural cure is F12/sync-bsvi (bridge_reply). Same 'snippet not bare count' principle as P2.
notes:
Cross-link from sync-bsvi clarification (2026-05-31): destination echo should expose two levels, not just "posted into thread #N". The response shape should include `replied_to` (requested target event_id, sender identity, short preview) and `posted_to` / thread context (surface plus thread root event_id, root sender, root preview, reply count/nudge if useful). This is the response-side pair to bridge_reply: agent verifies both "I answered the correct event" and "I landed in the correct thread/surface". Use the same preview policy as sync-bsvi, currently proposed as a named constant around 30 words.
Field naming clarification from sync-bsvi/user (2026-05-31): destination echo should expose the exact replied-to message using direct fields: `direct_event_id`, `direct_sender`, `direct_preview`, separate from thread-root fields. This prevents root-normalization from hiding the actual referent.
close_reason: Implemented backend destination echo on group send/reply responses with direct target and thread-root context. Verified through API/MCP tests, SQL query filters, typecheck, and full bun test.

title: F1-design: keep bridge_send_group name-only; surface group_name in event envelopes
description:
Research finding F1 design half (docs/skill-mcp-research-findings.md, 2026-05-31). The event envelope gives agents group_id, but bridge_send_group requires name. This forces a bridge_list_groups lookup on every reply — a friction tax paid by every agent on every turn.

The SESSION-FIX half (skill doc correction) is part of sync-b8p. This issue tracks the design decision: extend bridge_send_group to accept group_id as an alternative to name.

WHY: The daemon owns the group_id→name mapping. Making the agent resolve it is unnecessary friction, and it's the root cause of the 'group_id vs name' failure mode that hit 5/6 agents in the research session. With bridge_reply (sync-bsvi) handling the ~90% reply case, this matters most for new top-level posts and edge cases — but consistency in parameter semantics is worth tracking.

NOTE: bridge_reply (F12, sync-bsvi) eliminates the issue for the common case (replying to a received message). This issue covers the primitives (bridge_send_group for new posts). Low priority relative to bridge_reply.

FILES: daemon.ts, bridge_send_group MCP tool definition.
notes:
User clarification 2026-05-31: do NOT extend bridge_send_group to accept group_id. Consolidate around group names everywhere instead. The genuine issue is that MCP/event envelopes and skill guidance should surface the group name needed by bridge_send_group, so agents do not have to translate group_id -> name. Requirement: keep group names unique and rely on name as the stable MCP routing identifier. Current schema has groups.name UNIQUE and API tests cover case-insensitive create collisions; implementation should verify this remains enforced at API layer while fixing MCP/envelope surfaces.
Implemented 2026-05-31: kept bridge_send_group name-only, added group_name to Event API/MCP notification envelopes and history/inbox/event responses, updated MCP tool wording and roadmap. Verified group create already enforces unique names plus case-insensitive API collision checks.
close_reason: Kept bridge_send_group name-only and fixed the routing surface by adding group_name to event/API/MCP envelopes, notification metadata, group history/inbox/event reads, and the MCP tool guidance. Existing group-name uniqueness and case-insensitive API collision checks remain enforced. Verified typecheck, focused API/MCP tests, full bun test, and live-DB-copy migration.

title: F9: Add inline hint field to warnings payload in bridge_send_group response
description:
Research finding F9 (docs/skill-mcp-research-findings.md, 2026-05-31). When bridge_send_group returns a warnings array, the entries carry {token, reason} but no remedy. Agents encountering a warning cold must either infer the fix (which leads to wrong inferences, as happened in the research session with the @mention parser) or read source code to understand the cause.

WHAT: Add a hint field to each warning entry:
  {token: '@foo', reason: 'alias_not_in_group', hint: 'not a group member; wrap in single/triple backticks if literal'}

WHY: The daemon already knows the roster and the cause at the point it generates the warning. Adding hint collapses the dead-end array into the doc lookup — the fix travels with the error. This is strictly better than a severity field: severity tells you how alarmed to be; hint tells you what to do.

ALSO: The backtick-escape rule (wrap @literal in single or triple backticks to suppress mention parsing) must be documented in the skill/reference explicitly with the 'single or triple' qualifier. Double-backtick spans are NOT stripped by the current stripBacktickedRegions() parser — this is a real edge case that bit opus in the research session.

SMALL RESIDUAL BUG: Double-backtick spans (CommonMark usage when content contains a backtick) are not stripped. One-line fix: extend stripBacktickedRegions() regex to handle ... spans. Low priority but worth tracking.

FILES: daemon.ts (warnings generation near line 1728), skill mention reference.

title: F11: Thread-family MCP consolidation: get_thread(format:) + group_history(view:)
description:
Research finding F11 (docs/skill-mcp-research-findings.md, 2026-05-31). The thread query family (get_thread, get_thread_status, get_thread_summary, group_history, list_threads, query_events) has severe overlap confirmed by 6/6 agents — everyone had to stop and reason about which tool to use, and several picked by name-matching rather than understanding.

WHAT (two consolidations):
1. get_thread(format: 'json' | 'transcript' | 'status' | 'summary') replaces the current three-verb family. get_thread_status and get_thread_summary become format params. Verified by opus who called get_thread(format=transcript) and found the response already carried status and summary subsets — they're shapes, not verbs. 3 tools → 1.
2. group_history(view: 'flat' | 'threads') absorbs list_threads. Both are 'read this group's contents,' different projections. The redundant group_history(thread_of:) overlap with get_thread is eliminated.

query_events stays as the low-level escape hatch but is demoted to reference-tier. If it's a superset of group_history, group_history should be the default-friendly alias with sensible defaults; query_events appears only in reference docs.

WHY: The get_thread_* prefix 'screams overlap' (opus). Worst name-clarity AND worst overlap offender — the same fix resolves both. Backed by an actual tool call, not inference.

FILES: daemon.ts (merge response shapes), MCP tool definitions, skill reference/threads.md.
notes:
User clarification 2026-05-31: thread-tool consolidation needs a design pass before implementation. Do not sacrifice existing surfaces, and do not blindly combine responses in a way that pollutes model context with unrelated thread/status/summary data. Consolidation should be projection-based: explicit format/view selects the minimal surface needed, with richer variants available on demand. query_events remains the expert/debug escape hatch, not the default agent path.
Design plan drafted and Plannotator feedback applied in docs/thread-tool-consolidation-plan.md. Key v2 decisions: remove overlapping MCP tools instead of keeping compatibility aliases; bridge_get_thread defaults to summary with validated selectors; selectors default to last 5 and support first/last/all; summary is cache-first and does not regenerate when cache exists; bridge_group_history is top-level only and never expands thread replies inline; bridge_query_events remains expert/debug.
Added ASCII flow diagrams to docs/thread-tool-consolidation-plan.md for group catch-up, thread reply, active-thread discovery, known-event reread, forensic routing, and selector choice.
close_reason: Implemented thread MCP consolidation: bridge_get_thread formats/selectors, bridge_group_history views/selectors, removed redundant MCP thread tools, updated skills/tests.

title: F14: Add react/ack primitive (emoji reaction, no message body, no thread push)
description:
Research finding F14 (docs/skill-mcp-research-findings.md, 2026-05-31). Confirmed by opus and haiku as the single highest-value addition from the MCP lean audit. Every '+1, agreed' reply in the research session added noise to signal-dense threads. No lightweight acknowledgment exists; a full bridge_send_group is the only affordance, making noise the path of least resistance.

WHAT: bridge_react(event_id: <id>, emoji: '👍') — or similar. No message body, no push notification, no thread pollution. Just a structured acknowledgment attached to an event.

WHY: Directly attacks the ground rule the operator set ('don't +1'). If the tool surface makes +1 the path of least resistance and ack doesn't exist, the failure mode is structural, not behavioral. Also composes with P2 mention digest: agents can clear a digest row with a thumbs-up instead of a full paragraph reply.

DESIGN NOTES:
- Does NOT substitute for P2 mention digest — they solve orthogonal problems. react = output-side (noise I emit); P2 = input-side (attention I miss).
- Append-only compatible. No mutation of existing events.
- Should appear in bridge_get_thread response shape as a reactions: field per event.
- Consider whether reactions are visible to all group members or only discoverable via get_thread (latter is fine for v0).

PRIORITY RATIONALE: Cheapest high-leverage addition. Clean new primitive. Reduces message volume rather than adding surface.
notes: Expanded implementation scope from current user request: include durable daemon reaction storage, bridge_react/list visibility for agents, reactions on thread/history/event responses, and Slack-style web UI chips/picker/popover showing who reacted. Preserve sync-ever design intent: no message body, no thread pollution, no push notification for reactions.
close_reason: Implemented durable emoji reactions across daemon schema/API, MCP tools, and Slack-style web UI. Added targeted web reaction reconciliation with no /web/state reset, browser-verified add/remove in main and thread panes, and covered with API/data-source tests.

title: P3: Reply-surface marker hook — tell agents which surface to reply on
description:
Research finding P3 (docs/skill-mcp-research-findings.md, 2026-05-31). Agents mirror bridge posts back into their own session as prose because they don't know whether their session user is watching the GUI or synchronize. This defensive hedging doubles the token cost of every bus turn.

WHAT: A UserPromptSubmit/event hook that injects a one-line surface marker per turn:
- When a synchronize channel event triggers: 'Surface: synchronize thread <id> → reply via bridge_send_group; session output = stub only'
- When the user is GUI-only: 'Surface: GUI → reply here, do not post to synchronize'

WHY: The mirror reflex is defensive uncertainty (not habit) — agents narrate to session because they don't know who's watching. An authoritative marker removes the ambiguity. Research confirmed: marker alone (no violation-nudge) is sufficient for agents who want to comply; it must state both positive (reply via bridge) AND negative (session output = stub) explicitly.

IMPORTANT CAVEAT: Marker must be authoritative, not best-guess. A wrong marker loses the one message the human sees, causing agents to rationally keep mirroring. Marker reliability > wording. Needs the host adapter to actually know the active surface.

OPTIONAL V2: violation nudge (Stop-hook heuristic that detects mirroring by comparing session output against the bridge post). Ship the preventive marker first; treat auto-correction as stretch.

HOW TO APPLY: Part of A1 injection layer (sync-n151). Shared injection substrate with P1 and P2.

title: P2: Pending-mention digest pushed via channel injection (threshold-triggered)
description:
Research finding P2 (docs/skill-mcp-research-findings.md, 2026-05-31). The silo failure: a heads-down agent never pulls bridge_inbox, so unhandled mentions pile up silently. The P2 digest is the proactive fix — it pushes a compact list of threads where the agent has unhandled mentions.

WHAT: When an agent has >=1 NEW pending mention (since last digest delivery) that they haven't replied to, inject a compact digest via the channel-injection path:
  thread_root_id | alias who's waiting | one-line of their last message | bridge_get_thread(root_event_id: X) jump command

WHY: Push-not-pull is required — heads-down agents never think to poll inbox. Must ride the channel-injection path (not inbox row) to actually interrupt. Cadence-based delivery is wrong — fire on NEW pending-mention threshold only, never on a timer.

KEY DESIGN CONSTRAINTS (from research):
- Per-(agent,thread) answered-state flag: stop nagging once THIS agent has replied, regardless of whether others have.
- @mention != needs-response: only count direct questions or replies-to-me as digest-worthy, not broadcast @mentions.
- De-dupe hard: never re-push a thread row whose pending count hasn't increased.
- Snippet required: count alone won't cause a context switch; the last-message snippet is the hook.

HOW TO APPLY: Part of A1 injection layer (sync-n151). The per-(agent,thread) answered-state data structure is shared with F15 corrects/supersedes tracking.

title: P1: First-contact identity + group context injection on bus arrival
description:
Research finding P1 (docs/skill-mcp-research-findings.md, 2026-05-31). All six agents in the research panel hit identity ambiguity on turn 1 — they didn't know their own alias, the group purpose, or how to reply. The daemon injects nothing on arrival; agents must pull identity via bridge_whoami and group context via bridge_list_groups before they can act.

WHAT: On the first bus-join, first group-ping, and first thread-pull for a given (peer, scope), inject a short context note automatically:
- bus-join: 'you are <alias>; this is an agent message bus, not human input.'
- group ping: '+ group <name>: <purpose>.'
- thread pull: '+ thread started by <alias>, about: <intent>.'

WHY: The identity-is-pull gap caused mis-signing (pi-medium and pi-low both signed as pi-high), wrong-surface replies, and register-vs-whoami paralysis. Push-not-pull is the fix; pull tools (whoami, list_groups) already exist but aren't triggered on arrival.

SERVER-SIDE STATE NEEDED: per-peer 'seen bus', per-(peer,group) 'seen group', per-(peer,thread) 'seen thread' flags. Fires once per scope, not on every message.

MAX LENGTH: 3-5 lines hard cap. Must be tiny or weak models ignore it.

HOW TO APPLY: Part of the A1 injection layer (sync-n151). Must respect the shared priority order and budget.

title: [epic] Unified ambient-context injection layer (A1 from skill-mcp research)
description:
During the 2026-05-31 skill+MCP customer research session (docs/skill-mcp-research-findings.md), all three proactive-context proposals — P1 first-contact identity injection, P2 mention-digest push, P3 reply-surface marker — were found to share the same delivery path (the channel injection substrate) and the same risk (notification fatigue). Shipping them as independent injectors compounds that risk.

This epic frames the shared injection layer so P1/P2/P3 land as coordinated children rather than three separate injectors that accidentally stack.

WHY: The research established that: (a) identity-on-arrival, pending-mention digests, and reply-surface markers all need to ride the proven channel-injection interrupt path to actually reach heads-down agents; (b) a shared budget + priority order prevents the fatigue failure mode; (c) without this framing, each child issue gets designed in isolation and the combined behavior is never reviewed.

HOW TO APPLY: File P1, P2, P3 as children of this epic. Any new proactive-context injection proposal must justify its place in the priority order and its contribution to the shared budget before shipping.

title: Script launch stress matrix and browser verification
description:
Whole-plan context: docs/plans/launch-lifecycle-kernel.md defines Phase 1 acceptance around sequential, three-at-a-time, and five-parallel launch behavior. sync-6wlv contains the original observed failure and preserved runtime coordinates.

Objective: create repeatable stress/test tooling that launches Claude agents through /agent-sessions/launch, captures lifecycle timing/stage data, inspects AOE/tmux and SQLite/web state, and verifies the in-app browser lifecycle display.

What to build: script or documented command harness for sequential five-agent, three-at-a-time, and five-parallel tests. Record per-launch accepted/spawned/prompt_seen/prompt_accepted/registered/joined/running/failed timings and final state. Include cleanup for test AOE sessions and stale peers using throwaway SYNCHRONIZE_HOME by default.

Impact area: scripts or tests under tests/scripts, docs/handoff notes, live validation against /tmp/synchronize-sql-live.ULORGu only when explicitly intended, in-app browser verification.

Dependencies: local worker, durable reconcile, and web lifecycle state.
design: Stress failure is diagnostic if the lifecycle records why it failed. The acceptance target is no lost intent or unexplained auto-join miss.
acceptance_criteria:
- Sequential five-agent test records per-launch timing and final lifecycle state.
- Three-at-a-time test records per-launch timing and final lifecycle state.
- Five-parallel stress test records the furthest stage for every launch.
- Harness defaults to throwaway SYNCHRONIZE_HOME and documents preserved runtime usage separately.
- In-app browser verification confirms lifecycle state is visible in /web.
notes:
Implemented scripts/launch-stress.ts and ran live preserved-runtime validation: sequential 5/5 Claude launches reached running+joined; parallel 5/5 reached running+joined; restart variation recovered 3 accepted launches after daemon kill/restart. A final patched parallel run reached 5/5 running+joined, though one launch crossed the script timeout by a few seconds and then completed. In-app Browser plugin attempts to open localhost/127.0.0.1/LAN/proxy URLs all failed with ERR_BLOCKED_BY_CLIENT, so browser-visible verification remains blocked by the browser connector rather than daemon behavior.
In-app Browser verification completed after connector recovery: launched fresh five-parallel Claude batch d1-d5 into llstress; all reached running+joined+online. Follow-up roster bug found during demo: stopped stress launches were removed from AOE but remained active group_members. Fixed daemon stop/startup cleanup and web active-membership filtering; live llstress now shows only d1-d5 plus web user.
close_reason: Stress harness and browser verification completed; roster cleanup regression fixed and verified live.

title: Use persisted backend titles for attach and stop flows
description:
Whole-plan context: docs/plans/launch-lifecycle-kernel.md calls out misleading attach commands when title derivation depends on current group membership after auto-join failure.

Objective: make AOE attach/stop title lookup use launch_intents.backend_title for launch-bound peers. Current deriveBackendTitleForPeer recomputes the title from launch_id, peer_id, session_name, tool, and current active group membership; that changes when the launch did not join its group.

What to build: persist backend_title at launch acceptance and use it in deriveBackendTitleForPeer/deriveAoeSessionForPeer and stop-by-peer paths. Keep explicit title stop behavior unchanged. Add fallback/error behavior for older agent_sessions without launch_intents rows if needed.

Impact area: src/daemon.ts attach/stop derivation, web aoe_session metadata, launch service tests, API tests around /web/state aoe_session.

Dependencies: schema and durable acceptance path.
design: Backend title is launch provenance, not a derived fact from mutable group membership.
acceptance_criteria:
- A registered but unjoined launch still exposes the original AOE attach command.
- stop by peer_id uses the persisted backend_title for launch-bound peers.
- Explicit stop by title remains supported.
- Tests cover joined and registered_unjoined title derivation.
- /web/state ETag changes still include aoe_session metadata changes.
notes:
How to verify:
- Create a launch intent with backend_title, register the peer without joining the group, and verify /web/state still returns the original attach command.
- Stop by peer_id and assert LaunchService.stop receives the persisted backend_title.

Test plan:
- API/web-state tests for joined and registered_unjoined launches.
- Existing explicit stop-by-title tests remain unchanged.

Improvements:
- Add a compatibility fallback for old launch-bound sessions only if current live state requires it.
close_reason: Stop/attach derivation now prefers persisted launch_intents.backend_title for launch-bound peers, preserving original AOE titles even if current group membership changes.

title: Rework registration reconcile around durable launch intent
description:
Whole-plan context: docs/plans/launch-lifecycle-kernel.md identifies durable register/reconcile as the core correctness fix for lost auto-join.

Objective: make SessionStart registration reconcile by loading launch_intents from SQLite instead of consuming an in-memory pending map. Registration with a matching launch_id and peer_id should auto-join the target group even after daemon restart or HTTP timeout.

What to build: update reconcileLaunch to load durable intent, check pinned peer_id, record peer mismatch without consuming the valid intent, insert group path, join target group idempotently, and transition to joined/running or registered_unjoined with explicit reason. Unknown launch_id and no-group standalone launches should be explicit no-op/fact states, not silent mysteries.

Impact area: src/daemon.ts registration route and reconcile helper, launch store/reducer, group join tests. This issue directly addresses sync-6wlv failure signature.

Dependencies: schema, reducer, durable acceptance path.
design: Reconcile owns group membership mutation. Executors report facts only; they do not mutate group_members directly.
acceptance_criteria:
- Registration after daemon restart auto-joins the persisted target group.
- Foreign peer_id under the same launch_id does not join and does not consume the real intent.
- Alias collision becomes registered_unjoined with durable reason.
- Standalone launch registration is represented consistently without creating a group.
- Existing launch-reconcile tests are updated from pending-map assumptions to durable intent assumptions.
notes:
How to verify:
- Register an agent with persisted launch_id/peer_id and verify group auto-join after restart-like service reconstruction.
- Register an imposter peer_id with the same launch_id and verify the real intent is preserved.
- Force alias collision and verify registered_unjoined with a durable reason.

Test plan:
- Rewrite tests/launch-reconcile.test.ts around durable launch_intents instead of in-memory pending launches.
- Add integration coverage through /agent-sessions/register.

Improvements:
- Later expose reconcile repair commands if operators need to retry registered_unjoined launches manually.
close_reason: Reworked registration reconcile to load durable launch_intents, enforce pinned peer_id, preserve intent on mismatch, auto-join persisted target groups after restart, and transition joined/running with durable tests.

title: Add local launch worker for AOE spawn and prompt confirmation
description:
Whole-plan context: docs/plans/launch-lifecycle-kernel.md keeps side effects outside the HTTP request and drives them from durable launch_work rows.

Objective: add the Phase 1 local worker that claims launch_work rows, runs bounded local AOE/tmux side effects, appends lifecycle events, advances launch state, and enqueues follow-up work. This issue owns local AOE execution only, not remote executors.

What to build: worker loop or scheduler startup in daemon, work claiming with lease/attempts, spawn work that calls AoeBackend.spawn, prompt confirmation work that observes/sends tmux keys, retry/backoff behavior, and stale/failure classification when max attempts or deadlines are reached.

Impact area: daemon startup/shutdown, src/launch/backend.ts integration, new worker module, tests with injectable backend/clock/sleep. Must not leave hanging sessions in tests.

Dependencies: durable schema, reducer, and durable acceptance path.
design: Keep local AOE/tmux behind LaunchExecutor-compatible seams so Phase 2 can add remote executors without changing state semantics.
acceptance_criteria:
- Worker claims queued launch_work with lease protection and attempt tracking.
- AOE spawn is idempotent or guarded by idempotency_key semantics.
- Claude prompt confirmation records prompt_seen/prompt_accepted or prompt timeout state.
- Worker resumes queued/running-expired work after daemon restart.
- Tests cover success, retry, max-attempt failure, and daemon restart recovery at the work-queue level.
notes:
How to verify:
- Run worker tests with injectable backend, clock, and sleep.
- Simulate daemon restart by leaving queued/running-expired work in SQLite and starting the worker again.
- Force prompt timeout and AOE spawn failure to inspect failed/stale lifecycle events.

Test plan:
- Unit tests for work claiming and retry/backoff.
- Integration-style tests with fake AoeBackend; no live AOE required for CI.
- Live AOE validation deferred to sync-txpj.8.

Improvements:
- Add executor concurrency knobs only after the first worker is stable and measured.
close_reason: Implemented local launch worker over launch_work with lease claiming, restart recovery for local orphaned work, idempotent existing-backend spawn handling, explicit prompt confirmation work, retryable failures, and live AOE stress validation.

title: Persist launch acceptance before spawning AOE sessions
description:
Whole-plan context: docs/plans/launch-lifecycle-kernel.md makes durable acceptance the Phase 1 request boundary.

Objective: change /agent-sessions/launch and LaunchService so a launch request is validated, assigned launch_id/peer_id/backend_title, persisted to launch_intents, records an accepted event, enqueues spawn work, and returns quickly. This replaces the in-memory pending map as correctness source.

What to build: refactor LaunchService.launch around durable launch acceptance. Preserve existing request/response shape where possible, but include current lifecycle state if needed. Spawn must no longer be required to finish before HTTP 201. Failures before durable insert still return errors; failures after insert become lifecycle states/work failures.

Impact area: src/launch/service.ts, src/daemon.ts launch route, storage helpers, launch route tests. Later local worker issue will actually process the queued spawn work.

Dependencies: schema/storage helpers and transition reducer.
design: Prompt acceptance and AOE spawn move to work items. HTTP latency is no longer part of launch correctness.
acceptance_criteria:
- /agent-sessions/launch persists launch_intents before any AOE side effect.
- Launch response returns launchId, peerId, title, group, and current accepted/queued lifecycle information.
- No correctness-critical state remains only in pendingByLaunch.
- A simulated backend delay cannot block the HTTP response path.
- Tests cover durable acceptance and spawn work enqueueing.
notes:
How to verify:
- POST /agent-sessions/launch against an injected slow backend and verify the request returns after durable acceptance.
- Query launch_intents, launch_events, and launch_work to confirm accepted state and spawn work exist before any backend side effect completes.

Test plan:
- Focused route/service tests with fake backend and fake clock.
- Regression test showing no pending-map-only correctness remains.

Improvements:
- Consider returning lifecycle state in the launch response only if web/client flows need it; avoid response churn otherwise.
close_reason: Implemented durable launch acceptance in LaunchService: /agent-sessions/launch now persists launch_intents, appends launch.accepted, enqueues spawn work, and returns before AOE spawn; covered by durable service tests and full bun test/typecheck.

title: Implement launch transition reducer and event vocabulary
description:
Whole-plan context: docs/plans/launch-lifecycle-kernel.md separates pure lifecycle transitions from side effects. Phase 1 needs a maintainable state model now; Phase 2/3 need the same event vocabulary later.

Objective: implement the launch state/event vocabulary and a pure reducer: current_state + lifecycle_event -> next_state + work_to_enqueue. This issue owns modeling and tests only; storage and workers are separate issues.

What to build: define LaunchState, LaunchLifecycleEvent, transition results, invalid-transition behavior, terminal state handling, and reason codes for registered_unjoined/stale/failed. Include states accepted, spawning, spawned, prompt_waiting, prompt_accepted, registered, reconciling, joined, running, registered_unjoined, stale, failed, stopped.

Impact area: new lifecycle module under src/launch/, unit tests, shared types later used by daemon route, worker, reconcile, and web-state derivation.

Dependencies: none beyond the epic plan.
design: Use TypeScript discriminated unions first. Defer XState unless this reducer becomes hard to maintain.
acceptance_criteria:
- Reducer covers nominal accepted -> running path.
- Reducer covers failed, stopped, stale, and registered_unjoined paths.
- Invalid transitions are explicit and tested.
- Transition results can request follow-up work without executing side effects.
- Types are reusable by storage, worker, reconcile, and web-state code.
notes:
How to verify:
- Run reducer unit tests for every documented nominal, failure, stale, stopped, and registered_unjoined transition.
- Assert invalid transitions are explicit and do not enqueue side effects.

Test plan:
- Pure unit tests only; no daemon or SQLite required.
- Snapshot or table-driven tests for the state/event matrix.

Improvements:
- Revisit XState only if the reducer becomes hard to inspect or if visual statechart tooling becomes useful.
close_reason: Implemented pure launch lifecycle state/event reducer with nominal, failure, stale, stopped, registered_unjoined, invalid, and terminal transition coverage; full bun test and typecheck pass.

title: Add durable launch schema and storage helpers
description:
Whole-plan context: docs/plans/launch-lifecycle-kernel.md defines a durable launch lifecycle kernel for AOE launches now and remote executors later.

Objective: add SQLite persistence for launch intent, append-only lifecycle events, and queued work items. This issue only owns schema and storage helpers; it should not change launch runtime behavior by itself.

What to build: create launch_intents, launch_events, and launch_work tables with indexes for launch_id, peer_id, state, work status, next_run_at, and lease_expires_at. Add typed helpers for inserting accepted launch intent, appending lifecycle events, updating state timestamps/failure fields, enqueueing work, claiming work with a lease, and completing/failing work.

Impact area: src/db.ts migrations, likely new src/launch/store.ts or src/launch/lifecycle/store.ts, tests for schema/storage. Later issues will consume these helpers from LaunchService and reconcile paths.

Dependencies: none beyond the epic plan.
design: Owns the SQLite durability layer from Phase 1. Do not introduce DBOS/Temporal or remote executor behavior in this issue.
acceptance_criteria:
- New tables exist after openDatabase migration and are safe for existing databases.
- Storage helpers are typed and covered by focused tests.
- Work claim uses status, next_run_at, claimed_by, lease_expires_at, attempts, and idempotency_key.
- Appending lifecycle events and updating launch_intents can happen in one transaction.
- Existing tests still pass for databases without prior launch lifecycle rows.
notes:
How to verify:
- Run focused storage/schema tests for launch_intents, launch_events, and launch_work.
- Open a fresh database and an existing test database through openDatabase and verify migrations are idempotent.
- Inspect indexes with SQLite pragma output in tests or a focused migration assertion.

Test plan:
- Unit tests for insert/update/query helpers.
- Work-queue claim tests for lease expiry, attempts, idempotency_key uniqueness, and next_run_at filtering.
- Full bun test after integration with later issues.

Improvements:
- If storage helpers grow too broad, split store modules by intent/events/work while keeping one public LaunchStore facade.
close_reason: Implemented launch_intents, launch_events, and launch_work schema plus typed storage helpers with focused schema/work-queue tests; full bun test and typecheck pass.

title: Build durable launch lifecycle kernel for local and remote-ready agent launches
description:
Goal: replace the current synchronous, memory-local AOE launch lifecycle with a small durable lifecycle kernel owned by synchronize. The whole implementation plan is in docs/plans/launch-lifecycle-kernel.md.

Why this exists: Claude launch stress testing showed that agents can register with the expected launch_id and peer_id while group auto-join still fails because launch intent was only kept in daemon memory. HTTP timeouts, daemon restarts, slow prompt acceptance, stale AOE sessions, and concurrent launches all expose the same structural problem.

What this epic covers: durable launch intent in SQLite, append-only lifecycle events, a typed transition reducer, an idempotent launch work queue, local AOE/tmux worker execution, durable register/reconcile, persisted backend title lookup for attach/stop, web-visible lifecycle state, and scripted sequential/parallel stress validation.

Future direction: Phase 1 stays local-first and SQLite-backed. The design must keep LaunchStore, LaunchWorkQueue, LaunchExecutor, and LaunchTransition seams clean so Phase 2 can add remote executors and Phase 3 can evaluate DBOS/Temporal without rewriting the launch domain model.
design: See docs/plans/launch-lifecycle-kernel.md. Phase 1 owns a narrow lifecycle kernel in this repo; Phase 2 adds remote executors; Phase 3 considers DBOS/Temporal only if distributed orchestration complexity justifies it.
acceptance_criteria:
- Durable launch intent replaces the in-memory pending launch map as source of truth.
- /agent-sessions/launch returns after durable acceptance and does not depend on Claude prompt acceptance.
- Registration with launch_id can reconcile after daemon restart or HTTP timeout.
- Sequential, three-at-a-time, and five-parallel launch behavior is scripted or captured with per-launch lifecycle states.
- /web/state exposes enough lifecycle information to distinguish pending, registered_unjoined, stale, failed, joined, and running launches.
- The implementation remains local-first while preserving remote executor seams documented in the plan.

title: Characterize and harden Claude launch lifecycle under concurrency
description:
Claude spawn testing exposed that the current daemon launch lifecycle is fragile under delayed registration, prompt acceptance timing, stale peer state, and concurrent launches. This issue captures the observed behavior and defines a focused follow-up test matrix so the next session can resume from evidence instead of rediscovering the failure modes.

Observed behavior:
- Stale UI state: cl-ui-vf2 appeared online/working/initializing even though no live AOE/tmux session existed. It was a stale peer row after the AOE session was removed outside the daemon stop path. A prior daemon/env also used a very long SYNCHRONIZE_LEASE_MS, so the peer stayed online for years.
- Five parallel Claude launches were too aggressive for the current implementation. Multiple panes stayed at the Claude development-channel confirmation prompt. Some panes got past the prompt, but registered sessions still did not auto-join release-checks.
- Sequential launches improved prompt acceptance. cl-seq-1 and cl-seq-2 cleared the prompt and created agent_sessions rows with the correct launch_id, but still did not auto-join the target group.
- Reconcile failure signature: agent_sessions.launch_id was present and matched the launch response, but no group_members row was created for the alias. The UI then derived a different AOE attach title because title derivation depends on current group membership.
- Pending launch warning count grew across failures. This should not directly block reconcile because consume is keyed by exact launch_id and peer_id, but it indicates pending launch intents are accumulating and not being consumed.
- Bun request timeout appeared during stress runs. Claude prompt rendering plus auto-accept can exceed the default HTTP request budget, which means the AOE session may continue while the request lifecycle is already broken.

Key hypothesis:
The launch contract is currently too synchronous and too memory-local. /agent-sessions/launch tries to spawn, prompt-accept, then rely on later SessionStart registration to consume an in-memory pending launch. Under slow Claude startup, HTTP timeout, daemon restart, or interrupted request flow, the agent can still register with a launch_id but no durable launch intent remains to drive auto-join.

Hypotheses to check:
1. Pending launches do not logically block later auto-join, but stale pending intents indicate missing consumption or lost lifecycle completion.
2. Reconcile is skipped when registration happens after the in-memory pending launch is lost, even if agent_sessions.launch_id is correct.
3. Waiting inline for prompt acceptance can hit Bun idleTimeout and should not be required for correctness.
4. Prompt auto-accept timing varies by Claude startup state; batch launching changes prompt render time enough that a fixed short window is unreliable.
5. Removing AOE sessions outside /agent-sessions/stop leaves stale peer state; the UI needs either better stop semantics or stale launched-agent detection.
6. Long leases make failed launched agents appear alive for too long; launched agents stuck in initializing need a separate status/timeout from ordinary lease presence.
7. Derived AOE title for a registered but unjoined launch can differ from the original launch title because deriveBackendTitleForPeer includes current group membership; this makes UI attach commands misleading for failed auto-join cases.

Simple test plan:
1. Start a clean daemon with preserved state but default lease: env -u SYNCHRONIZE_LEASE_MS SYNCHRONIZE_HOME=/tmp/synchronize-sql-live.ULORGu SYNCHRONIZE_PORT=55244 bun run src/daemon.ts.
2. Clear prior test AOE sessions and stale peers for aliases cl-ui-*, cl-ok-*, cl-seq-*, cl-batch-*, cl-triple-*.
3. Launch one Claude into release-checks through /agent-sessions/launch with model claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 and thinking high.
4. Record HTTP status/time, returned title, launchId, peerId, and pending warning.
5. Poll tmux pane state until it is prompt/listening/ready.
6. Poll /agent-sessions for matching launch_id and peer_id.
7. Poll /web/state memberships for active alias in release-checks.
8. Expected: prompt clears, agent registers, launch auto-join log appears, group_members row exists, UI shows alias in release-checks.

Sequential five-agent test:
- Launch cl-seq-1 through cl-seq-5 one at a time.
- Wait for each agent to either succeed end-to-end or hit a timeout before launching the next.
- Capture per-agent timings: launch HTTP latency, prompt-clear latency, registration latency, auto-join latency.
- This is the baseline for reliability without concurrency pressure.

Three-at-a-time test:
- Launch cl-triple-1 through cl-triple-3 concurrently or within a one-second window.
- Validate all three panes pass the prompt, all three register, and all three auto-join.
- Compare prompt-clear and register latency against the sequential run.
- This is the realistic concurrency target before attempting five.

Five-parallel stress test:
- Launch cl-batch-1 through cl-batch-5 concurrently.
- Treat failure as diagnostic, not unexpected.
- Capture exactly which stage each launch reaches: AOE created, tmux pane exists, prompt visible, prompt cleared, SessionStart registered, auto-joined, activity moved out of initializing.

Instrumentation needed:
- Log reconcileLaunch entry with launch_id and peer_id before consume.
- Log consume miss reason: missing launch_id vs peer_id mismatch vs no group.
- Log pending launch count and age distribution.
- Log prompt confirmer state transitions: tmux session found, pane found, prompt found, Enter sent, C-m sent, prompt cleared.
- Consider surfacing launch lifecycle in /web/state so UI can distinguish pending launch, prompt_waiting, registered_unjoined, joined, failed, and stale.

Likely fix directions:
- Persist launch intents in a launches table instead of keeping them only in memory.
- Make launch reconcile durable so registration after daemon restart or HTTP timeout can still auto-join.
- Return launch accepted quickly, then run prompt acceptance and reconcile in a background launch worker.
- Increase or explicitly configure Bun idleTimeout only as a mitigation, not the core correctness mechanism.
- Add cleanup/repair for launched peers with no live AOE session or no group membership after a timeout.
- Avoid deriving AOE title from current group membership for launch-bound peers when agent_sessions.launch_id can map back to the original title.

Relevant commands used during diagnosis:
- curl /agent-sessions/launch with Claude Haiku high into release-checks.
- aoe -p synchronize-bdb55d16 list --json.
- tmux capture-pane -p -J -S -160 -t <pane>.
- curl /web/state filtered over peers, memberships, and agent_sessions.
- sqlite3 /tmp/synchronize-sql-live.ULORGu/synchronize.db queries over peers, group_members, and agent_sessions.

This issue should be resumed as a testing plus design-hardening session. Do not mark done until sequential, three-at-a-time, and five-parallel behavior have each been characterized with logs and the durable fix direction is selected.
design: Superseded as implementation design by docs/plans/launch-lifecycle-kernel.md and epic sync-txpj. Keep sync-6wlv as the original bug/evidence bead for stress failure signatures and preserved runtime coordinates.
acceptance_criteria: A follow-up session can reproduce the issue from this bead alone; sequential, three-at-a-time, and five-parallel Claude launch tests are scripted or documented; reconcile miss reasons are instrumented; a durable launch lifecycle fix is proposed or implemented; stale launched-agent UI behavior is explained and either fixed or filed separately.
notes:
Planning split created: epic sync-txpj with children sync-txpj.1, sync-txpj.2, sync-txpj.3, sync-txpj.4, sync-txpj.5, sync-txpj.6, sync-txpj.7, sync-txpj.8, sync-txpj.9.
Durable launch lifecycle implementation resolved the original concurrency failure modes. Live preserved runtime validation covered sequential five, parallel five, restart recovery, and final browser-visible five-parallel Claude demo. Stale launched-agent UI behavior was root-caused to stop not deactivating peer/group membership rows; fixed by soft-deleting stopped launch peers, startup-sweeping legacy stopped launches, and filtering /web active memberships.
close_reason: Original launch lifecycle bug fixed and live stress/browser validation completed.

title: Harden Claude prompt auto-accept for batch spawn
description: Batch spawning five Claude agents left every pane at the development-channel confirmation prompt. Harden the tmux auto-accept path so it does not block on AOE metadata lookup before sending acceptance keys.
acceptance_criteria: Five fresh Claude launches can be spawned into release-checks and all pass the development-channel prompt, register, and auto-join without manual keypresses.

title: Use deterministic 20-char AOE backend titles for launched agents
description:
Implement a deterministic, human-readable backend title convention for AOE-launched agents so tmux/AOE prompt automation is robust despite AOE truncating titles to 20 chars.

Convention:
- Backend title format: <hash8>-<alias11>, total <= 20 chars.
- hash8 is deterministic from canonical launch identity: launchId, peerId, group name, session/group alias, and tool. Tool is included in the hash input but not visible in the title.
- alias11 is the sanitized group alias/readable session name, max 11 chars, lowercase letters/numbers/dashes.
- Enforce alias length/syntax before launch; UI should guide/block invalid aliases.
- Store/use the computed backend title in launch pending/result paths; do not depend on full mutable session name for backend operations.
- Fix Claude dev-channel prompt auto-confirm to locate tmux panes through AOE metadata/id or the deterministic 20-char backend title convention.

Acceptance:
- Launching Claude from the web group context menu creates a session with a <=20 char backend title and auto-confirms the initial Claude dev-channel prompt.
- Launched Claude registers back to synchronize and joins the selected group.
- Pi launch still works with OpenAI Codex gpt-5.4-mini.
- Tests cover title derivation, alias validation, and AOE tmux session resolution.
close_reason: Implemented deterministic <hash8>-<alias11> AOE backend titles, alias validation, AOE tmux-id prompt lookup, and verified Claude/Pi UI launches in fresh rooms.

title: Show stale UI feedback when daemon is unreachable
description: Add explicit web UI feedback when the Synchronize daemon becomes unreachable or /web/state cannot be refreshed. The UI should detect that the rendered roster/messages may be stale, show a clear stale/disconnected/reconnecting state, and distinguish daemon-dead/stale-client state from real peer presence. Include tests or browser verification that a dead daemon does not leave agents appearing as live/working without a visible warning.

title: Live-test Pi idle longevity after AOE launch
description: Run a production-style AOE Pi launch in a throwaway Synchronize runtime, join it to a group, verify initial UI delivery, let it idle for multiple heartbeat intervals, then send a second UI/group mention and confirm the Pi process, subscription, heartbeat, and UI status remain healthy. Include a deterministic recovery pass similar to scripts/integration_pi_revival.py if possible. Note: actual natural-language reply requires fixing Pi provider auth; delivery/subscription can be validated from daemon and Pi extension logs.

title: Web roster shows stale presence: /web/state ETag ignores time-derived presence (304 serves frozen body)
description: The web UI roster froze every peer at its page-load presence: lease-lapse (online->offline) and activity transitions (working/idle) never reached the UI. Confirmed live: a normal poll returned claude2:online while fetch(url,{cache:'no-store'}) returned claude2:offline from the SAME /web/state URL — proving stale HTTP cache, not logic. The daemon set Cache-Control:no-cache with ETag=W/"${state.cursor}". Presence is time-derived (lease_expires_at>now) and activity pushes / lease lapses create NO new event, so state.cursor never moved, the browser's revalidation got 304 Not Modified, and the browser served the frozen page-load body indefinitely. Store logic, bundle, and /web/state payload were all correct; the 304 lied. Exposed by the 3-state presence feature (sync-ztr) which made /web/state carry time-derived fields under an event-cursor validator.
design: Root cause: ETag validator (event cursor) does not capture time-derived presence. Fix (src/daemon.ts /web/state handler): fold a digest of per-peer presence into the ETag — etag = W/"${cursor}.${Bun.hash(presenceSig)}" where presenceSig is sorted peer_id:presence over state.peers + state.memberships. Lease-lapse and working/idle transitions now bust the 304 while genuinely-unchanged state still 304s (optimization preserved). Regression test added in tests/api.test.ts: a setPeerActivity push must change the ETag and make a conditional GET return 200 not 304. Verified end-to-end in the live web UI: online=green, working=pink, idle=amber, offline=grey all render and transition correctly.
close_reason: Fixed: /web/state ETag now folds a per-peer presence digest into the validator (W/"cursor.hash") so time-derived presence transitions (lease-lapse offline, working/idle activity pushes) bust the 304 instead of serving a frozen page-load body. Regression test added in tests/api.test.ts. Verified end-to-end in the live web UI: online=green, working=pink, idle=amber, offline=grey all render and transition correctly; initializing maps to busy/pink; pulse on active states only.

title: Rust daemon supervisor: resolve baseUrl, loadURL(/web), reconnect overlay (Phase 1)
description: At app launch the Rust shell calls 'synchronize ensure-daemon --print-url', then window.loadURL(baseUrl + '/web'). On webview load failure (daemon died mid-session) show a native 'reconnecting…' overlay, re-run ensure-daemon, and reload. This makes the app never open to a blank window. Completes the thin-shell-over-daemon core.
acceptance_criteria: Cold launch with no daemon running auto-starts it and shows the live UI; killing the daemon shows reconnect overlay and recovers.

title: Scaffold Tauri shell + native macOS chrome (Phase 1)
description: Create desktop/ Tauri v2 project (Mac target). Native window chrome: hiddenInset titlebar + traffic-light positioning, app icon, native menu bar, core shortcuts (Cmd+W/R/comma, copy/paste). No daemon wiring yet (that is the supervisor issue). This is the native-feel surface.
acceptance_criteria: A signed-for-dev Tauri window opens with native titlebar, menu, and shortcuts; loads a placeholder; bun-dev workflow documented.

title: Add 'synchronize ensure-daemon --print-url' CLI subcommand (Phase 1)
description: The Tauri shell needs a way to guarantee the daemon is up and learn its baseUrl at launch, reusing existing logic instead of reimplementing it in Rust. Add a thin CLI subcommand wrapping ensureDaemon() (src/client.ts) that ensures health (spawning the daemon if needed) and prints the resolved baseUrl to stdout. Shared launch plumbing; not mobile-relevant.
acceptance_criteria: 'synchronize ensure-daemon --print-url' prints the daemon baseUrl, starting it if down; non-zero exit on failure.

title: Spike: validate Tauri loads daemon /web and SPA live-mode fires (Phase 0)
description: Before building anything, prove load model A works. Confirm the web SPA's live-mode branch (web/src/App.tsx:49, web/src/data/daemon.ts:146) fires when a Tauri WKWebView loads an EXTERNAL http://127.0.0.1:<port>/web URL (origin==daemon, pathname startsWith /web). Also stand up a bare Tauri window doing loadURL of the daemon's /web. Output: go/no-go on model A.
acceptance_criteria: Bare Tauri window renders the live web UI from the local daemon; live-mode auto-detect confirmed working; documented in the epic.

title: Central daemon deployment on VPS (tailnet bind + token service)
description: Run synchronize daemon on the always-on VPS bound to its tailnet IP with a stable port and SYNCHRONIZE_TOKEN, as a managed service. Document the client env contract. Proven feasible: daemon binds to 100.96.245.110 with token auth. See plan Phase 1.2.

title: Remote-URL override for clients (SYNCHRONIZE_REMOTE_URL)
description: ensureDaemon() must use a configured remote daemon URL when set, skipping local auto-start and daemon.json. HARD REQ: error loudly if remote unreachable; never silently spawn a local daemon (would create two isolated daemons). See docs/plans/multi-machine-support.md Phase 1.1.

title: Multi-machine support: sessions over LAN/Tailscale join one daemon
description: Enable Claude/Codex sessions on remote machines (e.g. VPS over Tailscale) to register with a single synchronize daemon so the UI renders local + remote sessions. Planning/feasibility + groundwork phase: access VPS, update Claude there, map required daemon/client/discovery/auth changes.

title: POST /agent-sessions/launch + client.launchAgent (tracer: Claude into group)
description: New REST endpoint accepting {tool,name,repo,group?,args?[]} (repo required, tool knobs in args[]). client.launchAgent wrapper. Tracer milestone: launch one Claude into a synchronize group end-to-end (spawn -> AOE pane -> hook registers -> auto-join). docs/plans/aoe-agent-launch.md decision 8.

title: reconcileLaunch: server-side group auto-join at register
description: In POST /agent-sessions/register, after upsert, look up launch intent in the in-memory map by launch_id/peer_id; if a group is present, join the peer (alias=name, fresh=true) via existing join handler; record join_failed on active-alias collision (session stays alive); drop map entry. Single clearly-named reconcileLaunch() that no-ops when no intent. docs/plans/aoe-agent-launch.md decisions 1,4.
close_reason: reconcileLaunch server-side auto-join; joinGroupCore extracted (group join coverage green isolated); 6 in-process tests; no regressions

title: Launch orchestration + in-memory launch map + warning
description: Mint launch_id + peer_id; resolveLaunchSpec (ad-hoc now, config-ready seam, separate from execution); profile + AOE-group naming (idempotent create); in-memory map launch_id/peer_id -> {group, alias} dropped on register; warning on each launch counting still-unregistered launches pointing at AOE HUD. No pending_launches table. docs/plans/aoe-agent-launch.md decisions 5,8,9.
close_reason: LaunchService + in-memory map + warning; 10 unit tests pass, typecheck clean

title: Extract shared command/env builder from launch.ts
description: Move buildCommand + launch env construction from src/cli/commands/launch.ts into src/launch/build.ts; refactor launch.ts to consume it. The daemon launch path reuses the same builder, wrapping output as env K=V ... <cmd> for AOE --cmd-override. Inject SYNCHRONIZE_HOME/HOOK_ENABLE/LAUNCH_ID/SESSION_NAME/PEER_ID. docs/plans/aoe-agent-launch.md decision 6.
close_reason: Builder extracted to src/launch/build.ts; launch.ts refactored; 6 unit tests pass, typecheck clean

title: SessionBackend interface + AoeBackend
description: Define SessionBackend seam (ensureProfile/ensureGroup idempotent, spawn=aoe add + session start, stop=aoe remove, list=aoe list --json). AoeBackend only for v0; vanilla-tmux later. Spawn fn injectable so unit tests run without aoe installed. Daemon route must never name an aoe command directly. Use add+session start (NOT --launch, which fails headless). docs/plans/aoe-agent-launch.md decision 9 + AOE mechanics.
