title: Audit + finalize token surface (decision doc, no code)
description: Enumerate every literal visual value currently in web/src/styles.css, web/src/extra.css, and the ~17 inline-style leaks in web/src/components/MessageRow.tsx, ThreadPane.tsx, primitives.tsx. Produce a mapping table: literal -> token name -> intended :root value (light + dark). Decide token naming scheme (recommend: --radius-{none,sm,md,lg,pill}, --shadow-{sm,md,lg}, --line/--line-thin/--line-hair, --space-bubble-pad, --space-row-gap, --font-{display,ui,mono}, --density-{cozy,compact}-* — but the bead-owner decides). This issue ships ZERO code; it ships the mapping table in this bead's notes/design field. All later sub-issues consume that table.
design:
Token audit / mapping decision for sync-75p.

Scope correction:
- The epic text mentions `web/src/extra.css`, but the actual imported file is `web/src/components/extra.css` (`web/src/main.tsx` imports `./components/extra.css`). Later child beads should use the actual path.

Baseline captured before implementation:
- `.codex-artifacts/ui-token-baseline/01-live-chat-baseline.png`
- `.codex-artifacts/ui-token-baseline/02-thread-open-baseline.png`
- Captured after restarting the production daemon at `http://127.0.0.1:58405/web` and sending: "Baseline CSS audit dummy message from Codex via web UI."

Naming scheme is final for this epic. Use CSS custom properties only; no component refactor, selector reshaping, or visual retuning in the implementation sweeps.

1. Foundation tokens to add in `web/src/styles.css` `:root` and `:root[data-theme="dark"]`

Typography:
- `--font-display: "Archivo Black", sans-serif`
- `--font-ui: "Space Grotesk", "Helvetica Neue", sans-serif`
- `--font-mono: "JetBrains Mono", monospace`
- `--text-9: 9px`
- `--text-9-5: 9.5px`
- `--text-10: 10px`
- `--text-10-5: 10.5px`
- `--text-11: 11px`
- `--text-11-5: 11.5px`
- `--text-12: 12px`
- `--text-12-5: 12.5px`
- `--text-13: 13px`
- `--text-13-5: 13.5px`
- `--text-14: 14px`
- `--text-15: 15px`
- `--text-16: 16px`
- `--text-17: 17px`
- `--text-22: 22px`
- `--text-24: 24px`
- `--tracking-tight: -0.01em`
- `--tracking-none: 0`
- `--tracking-xs: 0.02em`
- `--tracking-sm: 0.04em`
- `--tracking-md: 0.06em`
- `--tracking-lg: 0.08em`

Radius:
- `--radius-none: 0`
- `--radius-hair: 1px`
- `--radius-xs: 3px`
- `--radius-sm: 4px`
- `--radius-md: 5px`
- `--radius-lg: 6px`
- `--radius-xl: 8px`
- `--radius-2xl: 10px`
- `--radius-pill: 999px`
- `--radius-panel-top: 6px 6px 0 0`
- `--radius-panel-bottom: 0 0 6px 6px`

Lines/borders:
- Keep existing `--line`, `--line-2` for compatibility.
- Add `--line-bold: 3px solid var(--rule)`
- Add `--line-md: 2.5px solid var(--rule)`
- Add `--line-sm: 2px solid var(--rule)`
- Add `--line-xs: 1.5px solid var(--rule)`
- Add `--line-hair: 1px solid var(--rule)`
- Add `--line-none: none`
- Add `--line-transparent: 2px solid transparent`
- Add `--line-md-transparent: 2.5px solid transparent`
- Add `--line-dashed-md: 2.5px dashed var(--ink)`
- Add `--line-dashed-sm: 2px dashed var(--ink)`
- Add `--line-dashed-xs: 1.5px dashed var(--ink)`
- Add `--line-dashed-faint: 1.5px dashed var(--ink-faint)`
- Add `--line-rule-dashed-sm: 2px dashed var(--rule)`
- Add `--line-rule-dashed-xs: 1.5px dashed var(--rule)`

Shadows:
- Keep existing `--shadow`, `--shadow-sm`, `--shadow-lg`, `--shadow-hover` for compatibility.
- Add `--shadow-none: none`
- Add `--shadow-xxs: 0.75px 0.75px 0 var(--rule)`
- Add `--shadow-xs: 1px 1px 0 var(--rule)`
- Add `--shadow-chip: 1.5px 1.5px 0 var(--rule)`
- Add `--shadow-sm: 2px 2px 0 var(--rule)` (already exists; preserve name/value)
- Add `--shadow-md: 4px 4px 0 var(--rule)`
- Add `--shadow-lg: 6px 6px 0 var(--rule)` (already exists; preserve name/value)
- Add `--shadow-hover: 3px 3px 0 var(--rule)` (already exists; preserve name/value)
- Add `--shadow-hover-sm: 2.5px 2.5px 0 var(--rule)`
- Add `--shadow-accent-pink: 2px 2px 0 var(--pink)`
- Add `--shadow-accent-pink-strong: 2.5px 2.5px 0 var(--pink), 3.5px 3.5px 0 var(--rule)`
- Add `--shadow-accent-inset: inset 0 -2px 0 var(--accent)`
- Add `--shadow-tab-active: inset 0 -4px 0 var(--yellow)`
- Add `--shadow-focus-pink: var(--shadow-sm), 0 0 0 2px var(--pink)`

Spacing / density:
- Add primitive exact spacing tokens for repeated visual rhythm: `--space-0`, `--space-1`, `--space-2`, `--space-3`, `--space-4`, `--space-5`, `--space-6`, `--space-7`, `--space-8`, `--space-9`, `--space-10`, `--space-11`, `--space-12`, `--space-14`, `--space-16`, `--space-18`, `--space-20`, `--space-22`, `--space-24`, `--space-30`, `--space-38`, `--space-40`, `--space-50`, `--space-56`.
- Add semantic composites for common visual spacing:
  - `--space-row-gap: var(--space-14)`
  - `--space-row-gap-compact: var(--space-10)`
  - `--space-bubble-pad: 12px 16px`
  - `--space-thread-bubble-pad: 7px 11px`
  - `--space-chip-pad-xs: 1px 5px`
  - `--space-chip-pad-sm: 1px 6px`
  - `--space-chip-pad-md: 2px 7px`
  - `--space-sticker-pad: 4px 10px`
  - `--space-button-pad-sm: 4px 8px`
  - `--space-button-pad-md: 8px 12px`
  - `--density-cozy-pad: 16px 20px 14px`
  - `--density-compact-pad: 10px 16px 12px`

2. Literal-to-token mapping table

CSS radii:
- `0` -> `var(--radius-none)`
- `1px` -> `var(--radius-hair)`
- `3px` -> `var(--radius-xs)`
- `4px` -> `var(--radius-sm)`
- `5px` -> `var(--radius-md)`
- `6px` -> `var(--radius-lg)`
- `8px` -> `var(--radius-xl)`
- `10px` -> `var(--radius-2xl)`
- `999px` -> `var(--radius-pill)`
- `6px 6px 0 0` -> `var(--radius-panel-top)`
- `0 0 6px 6px` -> `var(--radius-panel-bottom)`

CSS borders:
- `3px solid var(--rule)` -> `var(--line-bold)` or existing `var(--line)`
- `2.5px solid var(--rule)` -> `var(--line-md)`
- `2px solid var(--rule)` -> `var(--line-sm)` or existing `var(--line-2)`
- `1.5px solid var(--rule)` -> `var(--line-xs)`
- `1px solid var(--rule)` -> `var(--line-hair)`
- `none` -> `var(--line-none)`
- `2px solid transparent` -> `var(--line-transparent)`
- `2.5px solid transparent` -> `var(--line-md-transparent)`
- `2.5px dashed var(--ink)` -> `var(--line-dashed-md)`
- `2px dashed var(--ink)` -> `var(--line-dashed-sm)`
- `1.5px dashed var(--ink)` -> `var(--line-dashed-xs)`
- `1.5px dashed var(--ink-faint)` -> `var(--line-dashed-faint)`
- `2px dashed var(--rule)` -> `var(--line-rule-dashed-sm)`
- `1.5px dashed var(--rule)` -> `var(--line-rule-dashed-xs)`

CSS shadows:
- `none` -> `var(--shadow-none)`
- `0.75px 0.75px 0 var(--rule)` -> `var(--shadow-xxs)`
- `1px 1px 0 var(--rule)` -> `var(--shadow-xs)`
- `1.5px 1.5px 0 var(--rule)` -> `var(--shadow-chip)`
- `2px 2px 0 var(--rule)` -> `var(--shadow-sm)`
- `2.5px 2.5px 0 var(--rule)` -> `var(--shadow-hover-sm)`
- `3px 3px 0 var(--rule)` -> `var(--shadow-hover)`
- `4px 4px 0 var(--rule)` -> `var(--shadow-md)` or existing `var(--shadow)`
- `6px 6px 0 var(--rule)` -> `var(--shadow-lg)`
- pink/focus/inset special cases -> the named accent shadow tokens above.

CSS typography:
- `"Archivo Black", sans-serif` -> `var(--font-display)`
- `"Space Grotesk", "Helvetica Neue", sans-serif` and `"Space Grotesk", sans-serif` -> `var(--font-ui)`
- `"JetBrains Mono", monospace` -> `var(--font-mono)`
- px font sizes from 9px through 24px map to the exact `--text-*` token above.
- letter spacing values map to `--tracking-*` above.

CSS spacing:
- Repeated single-value gaps/paddings map to exact `--space-*` tokens.
- Common repeated multi-value padding maps to semantic composite tokens above.
- One-off layout-specific padding may stay literal only if it is documented as structural layout math in the implementation bead notes.

TSX inline style leaks:
- `MessageRow.tsx` author chip: static `fontFamily`, `fontSize`, `letterSpacing`, `padding`, `border`, `borderRadius`, `boxShadow` map to `var(--font-display)`, `var(--text-12)`, `var(--tracking-xs)`, `var(--space-chip-pad-author)` (or exact composite `3px 9px` if added), `var(--line-sm)`, `var(--radius-md)`, `var(--shadow-sm)`.
- `ThreadPane.tsx` parent author chip: static `padding`, `border`, `borderRadius`, `boxShadow`, `fontFamily`, `fontSize` map to token equivalents. `background: parentAuthor.color` remains dynamic.
- `primitives.tsx` Avatar: static `borderRadius`, `border`, `fontFamily`, `boxShadow` map to tokens. `width`, `height`, `background`, `color`, `fontSize: Math.round(size * 0.45)` remain dynamic.
- `primitives.tsx` StatusDot: static `borderRadius`, `border` map to tokens. `width`, `height`, `background`, and conditional `animation` remain dynamic.
- `primitives.tsx` Sticker: static `padding`, `border`, `borderRadius`, `boxShadow`, `fontFamily`, `fontSize`, `letterSpacing`, `color` map to tokens. `background: color ?? ...` and `transform: rotate(${tilt}deg)` remain dynamic/intentional.

3. Intentional exceptions

Do not tokenize these in the sweep unless a later validation screenshot proves they are theme blockers:
- Structural layout dimensions: sidebar width, timeline width, roster width, thread-pane clamps, grid column definitions, avatar/tile dimensions, scrollbar widths, virtualizer heights/transforms, absolute offsets, negative margins used for grouped messages or avatar stacks.
- Dynamic data styles: agent colors, mention colors, computed positions, computed percentages, virtualizer `height` / `translateY`, `StatusDot` status animation selection, `Sticker` tilt, `Avatar` size-derived font size.
- Code block colors: `--code-bg` / `--code-fg` intentionally stay dark in both light and dark themes per `web/DESIGN.md`.
- Component-specific geometry that encodes shape rather than theme, such as tooltip arrows and timeline marker alignment comments, can remain literal if documented in the relevant child bead.
acceptance_criteria: (1) Mapping table committed to this bead's --append-notes or --design field. (2) Every literal value the audit flagged has a destination token. (3) Naming scheme reviewed and final — no renames during the implementation sweeps. (4) Identifies any literals that should INTENTIONALLY stay literal (e.g. magic numbers tied to specific layout math — document each exception with rationale).
notes: Audit correction before implementation: add two TSX chip composite tokens to the frozen list: --space-author-chip-pad: 3px 9px for MessageRow author chips, and --space-thread-author-chip-pad: 2px 8px for ThreadPane parent author chips. Use these instead of the provisional name --space-chip-pad-author in the design text. This is a naming correction made before any implementation sweep starts.
close_reason: Completed no-code token audit and finalized mapping/naming scheme in bead design/notes. Baseline screenshots captured in .codex-artifacts/ui-token-baseline.

title: Web UI: tokenize design system for multi-theme readiness
description: Migrate the web UI's visual decisions (radii, shadows, borders, spacing, typography) from literal CSS/TSX values to CSS custom-prop tokens, so that future aesthetic themes (vanilla Slack/WhatsApp-style minimal, etc.) can be added as a single :root override block — without changing component code. Driven by the audit dated 2026-05-23: components already use semantic class names and rarely hardcode aesthetic values; the remaining literals are concentrated in styles.css/extra.css (~116 raw values) and ~17 TSX inline-style leaks. This epic finishes that job. Out of scope for this epic: Radix accessibility migration (separate epic, deferred behind validating this lands cleanly); shipping any actual second theme (deferred until a concrete second design exists). The neo-brutalist default theme is not changed visually by this work — see the Final Verification sub-issue.
design: Architecture: Option A from the 2026-05-23 design discussion. Pure tokens + :root overrides. Rejected alternatives: Option B (per-theme override stylesheets) deferred until a real second mock exists; Option C (CVA / variant-per-component) rejected as invasive and unjustified given speculative theme count. Audit numbers grounding the scope: 25 inline style={} usages across 10 components, of which only ~17 leak literal aesthetic values (rest pass dynamic data — agent color, position, computed percent — and stay); raw hex colors in TSX = 14 of which 12 are the AgentColorPicker palette swatches (acceptable, they ARE the palette); 182 className= usages, all semantic. CSS bulk: extra.css ~116 raw visual-value declarations, mixed token discipline (some use var(--shadow), others hardcode '2px 2px 0 var(--rule)'); radii not tokenized; DESIGN.md's rounded scale exists conceptually but lacks --radius-* CSS vars. The 'switch theme on click' UX is enabled by toggling document.documentElement.dataset.theme; same mechanism as the existing light/dark toggle.
acceptance_criteria: (1) Every child issue closed. (2) bun test green; bun run typecheck green. (3) Default neo-brutalist theme is visually identical to baseline (master at epic-start) — verified via the Final Verification sub-issue. (4) A throwaway alternate-theme [data-theme=...] block exists (or existed during validation) that demonstrates every component re-skins on attribute swap without layout breakage. (5) web/DESIGN.md documents the token contract so a future contributor knows which side of the inline-vs-CSS-vs-token line a value belongs on.

title: Test real Pi MCP group join mention and thread workflow
description:
What/why/how:
Add a real Pi AoE/tmux MCP smoke scenario for group-policy v0. The scenario should use isolated per-worktree Pi config/auth like the existing Pi DM smoke, launch at least two Pi agents, wait for auto-registration, then prompt one or more agents to use synchronize MCP tools for a group workflow. Keep prompts high-level and self-discovery based.

Impact area:
- New real Pi scenario under scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/.
- Existing PiEnvironment, AoE, tmux named Enter submission, transcript evidence, and REST validation helpers.
- MCP group tools: bridge_whoami, bridge_list_peers, bridge_join_group, bridge_send_group, bridge_group_history, and optionally bridge_rename_in_group.

Acceptance criteria:
- Two Pi agents launch in AoE/tmux with isolated Pi home/session dirs and current-worktree MCP config.
- The sender Pi calls bridge_whoami and bridge_list_peers before choosing another live Pi peer or group participant.
- Pi agents join a test group through bridge_join_group without being spoon-fed their own peer_id/session name.
- A Pi agent sends a group message with an @alias mention and then sends or reads a thread reply via MCP tools.
- REST assertions confirm group membership, group_message events, mentions_json or thread parent_event_id, and history visibility.
- Transcript evidence contains the expected MCP tool calls and no shell/CLI fallback for the tested workflow.
- --keep preserves AoE profile and run state for manual inspection.

How to verify:
- Run the new real Pi group-policy wrapper with generous command/registration/MCP timeouts.
- Inspect artifacts: pi-peers.json, transcript evidence, group history JSON, pane captures.
- Run with --keep once and attach through AoE for manual validation.

Test plan:
- Manual/local e2e smoke only; not part of bun test or CI.
- Deterministic fake-shell issues provide the stable regression layer; this verifies real-agent tool use.

Dependencies:
- Scaffold group-policy scenario entry points
- Test CLI group threads mentions and inbox routing

Improvements:
- Later expand to three Pi agents to make mention routing and thread participant behavior more realistic.
close_reason: Implemented real Pi AoE/tmux group-policy MCP workflow; validated warmup, self-discovery prompts, group aliases, mention resolution, thread reply state, and transcript evidence.

title: Cover group-policy v0 with real Pi MCP workflows
description:
Goal statement: add a real Pi AoE/tmux smoke test for group-policy v0 through MCP tools, preserving the self-aware auto-registration model.

What is the goal of the epic?
After deterministic CLI coverage exists, add a smaller real-agent workflow where Pi sessions use synchronize MCP tools to discover themselves and each other, join a group, send a mention/thread message, and read history. This validates the practical agent behavior that the API tests and fake-shell tests cannot cover.

Affected components/modules/systems/files:
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/ real Pi group-policy scenario.
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/pi_env.py and tmux.py existing Pi/AoE primitives.
- src/mcp/tools/groups.ts public MCP tool descriptions and schemas.
- skills/synchronize-pi/SKILL.md only if prompt/tool guidance gaps are discovered.

How to verify / test the epic:
- Run the real Pi group-policy scenario locally with isolated Pi state, copied auth.json, and worktree-local MCP config.
- The scenario should not spoon-feed peer IDs except where the test is explicitly asserting low-level REST state; Pi should use bridge_whoami and bridge_list_peers to reason about identity.
- The run can be kept with --keep for manual AoE inspection.
close_reason: Completed real Pi MCP group-policy workflow and docs/diagnostics for the AoE/tmux integration suite.

title: Test CLI group threads mentions and inbox routing
description:
What/why/how:
Add a deterministic fake-shell AoE/tmux workflow for Slack-style threads, mention resolution, default history filtering, thread history, unresolved mention warnings, and durable inbox behavior. This should exercise public CLI commands while using REST to assert precise daemon state.

Impact area:
- New deterministic group-policy scenario module or a second phase in the deterministic scenario.
- src/cli/commands/group.ts commands: send --in-reply-to, history --thread-of, history default view.
- Daemon behavior: parent_event_id normalization, mentions_json, warnings, inbox fanout, and push-routing assumptions where practical.

Acceptance criteria:
- Three fake agents join one group with aliases alice, bob, and carol.
- A root message is sent, then a reply, then a reply-to-reply; REST confirms replies collapse to the root event_id.
- Default group history hides thread replies; thread history returns root plus replies in chronological order.
- A main-channel message mentioning @bob and @ghost returns an unresolved warning for @ghost and stores bob's peer_id in mentions_json.
- Inbox assertions confirm non-sender active members receive durable group message rows regardless of mention status.
- Roster events are either filtered out intentionally in message assertions or separately asserted as durable inbox events.

How to verify:
- Run the deterministic group-policy wrapper and inspect the REST validation artifact.
- Confirm command transcripts show CLI usage rather than direct REST mutation for workflow actions.
- Existing tests/api.test.ts mention/thread tests still pass.

Test plan:
- End-to-end local integration test via fake shell agents.
- REST artifact assertions for exact event fields.
- Optional push-sink coverage can be added if the harness scaffolding exposes a stable local callback server.

Dependencies:
- Scaffold group-policy scenario entry points

Improvements:
- Add local push-sink assertions later to prove mention-only push delivery at integration level, not just API-test level.
close_reason: Implemented and verified deterministic AoE/tmux CLI workflow for thread normalization, default/thread history, mention resolution, unresolved warning probe, and durable inbox assertions.

title: Test CLI group identity alias and description workflow
description:
What/why/how:
Add a deterministic fake-shell AoE/tmux workflow that exercises group creation, description metadata, joining with aliases, self-rename, leave/rejoin, and alias reclaim audit events through the synchronize CLI. Use REST assertions to confirm the daemon state and event history.

Impact area:
- New deterministic group-policy scenario module under scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/.
- The scenario drives src/cli/commands/group.ts via tmux shell commands.
- REST assertions validate daemon semantics for groups, members, events, and inboxes.

Acceptance criteria:
- At least two fake agents register through CLI in AoE/tmux panes.
- Agent 1 creates a durable group with --description and joins with a stable alias.
- Agent 2 joins with a different alias.
- Agent 1 renames itself; history includes group_member_renamed and the active roster shows the new alias.
- Agent 1 leaves; Agent 2 claims Agent 1's freed alias; history includes exactly one group_member_alias_reclaimed event with previous_peer_id.
- Group description can be updated and cleared through CLI, with REST/list verification.
- The test captures pane output and REST artifacts for debugging.

How to verify:
- Run the new deterministic group-policy CLI wrapper without --keep and confirm PASS.
- Run once with --keep for manual AoE inspection if needed.
- Verify artifacts include group history, members/peers, and command transcripts.

Test plan:
- End-to-end local integration test via AoE/tmux fake shell agents.
- Existing API tests remain the lower-level regression suite.

Dependencies:
- Scaffold group-policy scenario entry points

Improvements:
- Later add a third fake agent to make alias collisions and roster inbox fanout more explicit.
close_reason: Implemented and verified deterministic AoE/tmux CLI workflow for group create/description, join, rename, leave, alias reclaim, and REST history/roster assertions.

title: Cover group-policy v0 with deterministic AoE CLI workflows
description:
Goal statement: build deterministic fake-shell AoE/tmux integration tests for group-policy v0 behavior using the synchronize CLI plus REST assertions.

What is the goal of the epic?
The existing API/unit tests prove individual daemon semantics. This epic adds end-to-end workflow coverage where multiple tmux-hosted fake agents operate through the CLI in AoE sessions, and the harness asserts daemon state. This should be the reliable first layer before using nondeterministic real Pi agents.

Affected components/modules/systems/files:
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/ deterministic CLI group-policy scenario.
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/sync_rest.py helper surface.
- src/cli/commands/group.ts public CLI commands exercised indirectly.
- daemon group routes for create/join/rename/send/history/description/inbox semantics.

How to verify / test the epic:
- Run the new deterministic group-policy AoE wrapper locally.
- It should launch fake shell agents, drive CLI commands inside their panes, assert REST state, capture diagnostics, and clean up unless --keep is passed.
- Existing bun tests and existing integration DM smoke should continue to pass.
close_reason: Deterministic AoE CLI group-policy workflows are implemented and passing.

title: Scaffold group-policy scenario entry points
description:
What/why/how:
Create stable scenario entry points for group-policy integration tests, following the existing thin-wrapper pattern from scripts/integration_tmux.py and scripts/integration_pi.py. The goal is to make group-policy tests discoverable and runnable without turning the current DM smoke into a large conditional script.

Impact area:
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/: add separate scenario modules for deterministic CLI/fake-shell group policy and real Pi MCP group policy.
- scripts/: add thin public wrappers if needed, for example integration_group_policy_tmux.py and integration_group_policy_pi.py, or extend existing wrappers with a scenario flag only if that stays simple.
- scripts/README.md and docs/integration-tmux.md should later list the new commands.

Acceptance criteria:
- There is a clear command for deterministic fake-shell group-policy workflow tests.
- There is a clear command for real Pi group-policy MCP workflow smoke.
- Both commands support the same core operational flags as the existing harness: --keep, run/profile naming, timeouts, and state/log directories where relevant.
- The scenarios use shared AoE/tmux/runtime/REST/Pi primitives rather than duplicating lifecycle setup.

How to verify:
- --help works for the new entry points.
- Python compile passes for all integration harness modules.
- Existing DM wrappers still import and run their current scenarios.

Test plan:
- Harness-level smoke via --help/compile initially.
- Functional validation lands in the child workflow issues.

Dependencies:
- Add group-policy REST helpers to AoE harness

Improvements:
- If scenarios multiply, introduce a small scenario registry instead of many top-level wrappers.
close_reason: Added stable group-policy tmux/Pi wrappers plus scenario modules with full flag surfaces; verified compile and --help for both commands.

title: Add group-policy REST helpers to AoE harness
description:
What/why/how:
Add reusable REST helper methods for group-policy workflows in the AoE integration package. The current SyncRestClient supports status, peers, events, inbox, and agent_sessions. Group-policy scenarios will need create/list/join/leave/rename/send/history/describe helpers and possibly subscription helpers for push assertions. Keep this issue focused on shared plumbing, not a specific test workflow.

Impact area:
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/sync_rest.py: add typed-ish Python helper methods around /groups, /groups/:name/join, /leave, /rename, /messages, /history, PATCH /groups/:name, and optional /events/subscribe if used.
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/: consumers should call helpers instead of hard-coding URL strings.

Acceptance criteria:
- Helpers cover group create, patch description, join, leave, rename, send with optional in_reply_to, history with optional thread_of, list groups, inbox, and peer events.
- HTTP errors remain surfaced as HarnessError with useful path/body context.
- No existing integration scenario regresses.
- Helper names mirror the public synchronize concepts rather than daemon implementation details.

How to verify:

- Existing AoE fake-shell scenario remains the regression check.

Improvements:

title: Define reusable AoE group-policy scenario scaffolding
description:
Goal statement: create the reusable test-harness shape needed for group-policy v0 workflows on top of the existing AoE/tmux integration suite.

What is the goal of the epic?
This epic documents the shared harness work required before adding multiple group-policy workflows. The current suite has CLI DM and real Pi DM scenarios; group-policy tests need reusable group REST helpers, scenario entry points, and artifact conventions so future workflow tests do not duplicate AoE/tmux lifecycle code.

Affected components/modules/systems/files:
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/sync_rest.py for group REST helper methods.
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/ for new scenario modules.

How to verify / test the epic:

- The real Pi group-policy scenario can reuse the same daemon discovery, AoE/tmux, transcript, and artifact primitives.

close_reason: Reusable AoE group-policy scaffolding is in place: REST helpers, stable wrappers, and scenario entry modules are implemented and verified by compile/help checks.

title: Accept peer_id alias for bridge_dm MCP calls
description: Real Pi agents sometimes call bridge_dm with peer_id instead of recipient_peer_id on the first attempt, causing a validation error before retrying successfully. Accept peer_id as a compatibility alias while keeping recipient_peer_id canonical, and tighten Pi harness/skill wording toward recipient_peer_id.
close_reason: Accepted peer_id as a bridge_dm alias, tightened Pi prompt/skill wording, added MCP e2e coverage, and verified with typecheck, full bun test, and a kept real Pi harness run.

title: Render alias#<suffix> in TUI and web UI rosters/history
description: Daemon now exposes host_session_id on every group_members row and peer_id is already canonical. UI consumers should render the canonical disambiguator alongside alias everywhere a member or sender is shown: prefer alias#<host_session_id[0:6]> when bound, otherwise alias#<peer_id[0:4]>. Targets: TUI top/status output (src/cli/render/), the web chat roster (web/src/components/AgentRoster.tsx and any message-author rendering), and group history events that surface a sender. This is the deferred display piece from Phase 1 (sync-2l1) and supersedes sync-0oc (duplicate session names in top/status).
design: Daemon already returns host_session_id on member rows via MEMBER_SELECT_SQL. UI layers compute a deterministic short suffix and append it. No daemon-side changes required. Test by running multiple peers with the same session_name (and/or one bound + one cli fallback) and confirming the rendered names visually distinct.
acceptance_criteria: TUI peer/member listings show suffix; web roster shows suffix; duplicate session_name + same host_session_id is impossible by construction so the suffix never collides; sync-0oc verified closed by this rendering.
close_reason: TUI portion shipped on worktree-sync-dx2-tui-suffix. SummaryPeer now carries host_session_id; renderSummary composes alias#<suffix> for every peer (host_session_id[0:6] when bound, peer_id[0:4] otherwise). 27 tests pass. Web UI rendering is structurally blocked by sync-jix (DaemonDataSource is still a throwing stub); when sync-jix lands, the same peerDisplayName helper can be reused. Closing because the actionable TUI work is done — file a fresh bead under sync-jix if/when web rendering needs separate tracking.

title: Document AoE tmux harness usage
description:
What/why/how: Document how to run the local AoE/tmux integration harness, what it requires, what state it creates, how cleanup works, and how to debug a failed run. The documentation should make clear that this is a local integration smoke suite, not required CI.

Impact area: Developer-facing docs only, plus any script help text needed for discoverability. It should reference the harness command and the meaning of key flags.

Dependencies: depends on the validated harness behavior.
acceptance_criteria:
- Docs include setup requirements for aoe, tmux, bun, uv, and Python/libtmux.
- Docs show the default run command and examples for --keep, --verbose, and --log-dir.
- Docs explain that AoE profile/session state is test-owned and synchronize uses a temporary SYNCHRONIZE_HOME.
- Docs explain where logs and captured pane output are written.
notes: Improvements: Later document real Pi-agent mode under its own epic once implemented.
close_reason: Implemented AoE/tmux fake-shell harness with uv/libtmux preflight, AoE profile/session setup, tmux pane mapping, CLI DM smoke, REST validation, diagnostics, docs, and verified five-agent smoke locally.

title: Group policy phase 3b: mentions + notification routing
description: Rewrite the group message notification fanout to be mention-aware and thread-aware. Add events.mentions_json TEXT NULL column storing resolved peer_ids at send time. Daemon parses @-tokens, resolves each via group_members.alias (active=1 only), and returns a warnings array for unresolved tokens (alias_not_in_group). The message still sends -- warnings are non-fatal. Push fanout rules: main channel msg -> push to mentions only; thread reply -> push to root_author U thread_posters U new mentions; group_joined/left/group_member_alias_reclaimed -> inbox only, no push. Inbox rows always written for every active member regardless of push. Bundles tightly with phase 3a (threads) because both rewrite notifySubscribers; recommend keeping them in adjacent worktrees with a shared base branch.
design: See session-tracker/plan-group-policy-v0.md sections "Mentions & notification routing," "Message routing in main channel," "Message routing in a thread," and the routing table. Schema: ALTER TABLE events ADD COLUMN mentions_json. API: bridge_send_group response shape becomes { event, warnings? }. Touchpoints: src/daemon.ts (notifySubscribers rewrite + mention resolver), src/api/groups.ts (response type), src/mcp/tools/groups.ts (warnings passthrough), tests for each routing rule.
acceptance_criteria: @alice in body resolves to peer_id of active member alice; unknown @bob yields warnings entry but does not block send; main-channel push reaches only mentioned peers; thread reply push reaches root author, prior thread posters, and new mentions in this reply; group_joined/left/reclaimed events land in inbox of all members but never push; all members get inbox rows for every message regardless of mention status.
close_reason: Phase 3b shipped: mentions_json + warnings response + mention/thread-aware push + roster events fan out to inbox-only. Tests: 32 pass, 0 fail.

title: Group policy phase 3a: Slack-style threads
description: Add Slack-style threads to group conversations. New events.parent_event_id INTEGER NULL column with FK to events(event_id). Daemon normalizes thread depth to one level: if in_reply_to targets a reply, daemon resolves to that reply's parent_event_id (i.e., the thread root) and stores that. Adds in_reply_to? param to bridge_send_group and thread_of? query param to bridge_group_history. Index on (group_id, parent_event_id, event_id) for fast thread navigation. Pure storage + query work in this phase -- notification routing for thread replies lands in phase 3b alongside the rest of the fanout rewrite.
design: See session-tracker/plan-group-policy-v0.md sections "Threads" and "Message routing in a thread." Schema: ALTER TABLE events ADD COLUMN parent_event_id; CREATE INDEX idx_events_group_parent_event. API: bridge_send_group({ name, message, in_reply_to? }); bridge_group_history({ name, thread_of? }). Touchpoints: src/db.ts, src/daemon.ts, src/api/groups.ts, src/mcp/tools/groups.ts.
acceptance_criteria: Reply-to-reply collapses to thread root in stored parent_event_id; getGroupHistory with thread_of= returns root + replies in chronological order, excluding unrelated messages; main-channel history excludes thread replies by default; tests cover the normalization and filtering.
close_reason: Phase 3a shipped on integration branch: parent_event_id schema + thread normalization + thread_of/in_reply_to params + tests (29 pass, 0 fail). Notification routing remains in sync-6kt.

title: Group policy phase 2: bug cluster
description: Two remaining bugs in the group lifecycle (down-scoped from the original Phase 2 cluster after re-evaluation in 2026-05-23): (1) Case-collision in groups.media_dir on case-insensitive filesystems: 'Foo' and 'foo' are distinct DB rows but share the on-disk media_dir. Fix by validating against case-folded name collision at the daemon layer (or lowercase the path component). (2) Ephemeral groups are dropped on daemon startup (db.ts:142) but their media_dir trees remain on disk -- disk leak. Fix by rm -rf'ing the media_dir for each ephemeral group during the same startup pass.\n\nDe-scoped during re-evaluation: sync-rwy (CLI duplicate peers) verified fixed in current code via resolveCliRegisterPeerId + findReusablePeer; sync-0oc (top/status duplicate display) superseded by sync-dx2 (suffix rendering).
design: See session-tracker/plan-group-policy-v0.md "Phase plan" section. Touchpoints: src/daemon.ts (group create validation, startup cleanup), src/db.ts (ephemeral cleanup pass), src/cli/commands/register.ts + src/cli/identity.ts (sync-rwy). Tests: tests/api.test.ts for case-collision, tests/health.test.ts for ephemeral cleanup.
acceptance_criteria: Daemon rejects group create when name collides case-insensitively with an existing group (or normalizes to lowercase media_dir -- pick during impl); ephemeral group media_dir trees are deleted on startup alongside the DB row purge; sync-rwy and sync-0oc moved forward in tandem.
close_reason: Phase 2 bug cluster shipped on worktree-phase2-bug-cluster (commit on top of integration branch). Case-insensitive group-name collision rejected pre-insert with a 409 + lowercased media_dir; ephemeral media directories now rm -rf'd alongside the row purge on daemon startup. 26 tests pass.

title: Replace mention-autocomplete popup with Radix Popover + cmdk
description:
## Why
\`web/src/components/Composer.tsx\` has a homegrown \`@mention\` autocomplete:
- Custom \`useLayoutEffect\` + \`getBoundingClientRect\` positioning (works, but fragile on resize).
- Arrow / Enter / Tab / Escape keyboard handlers tangled into the textarea \`onKeyDown\`.
- No \`combobox\` ARIA pattern — screen readers don't know it's an autocomplete.
- No focus management between textarea and popup.
- Filter logic is hand-rolled \`startsWith\` matching.

A proper \`combobox\` is one of the trickiest a11y patterns to implement correctly — exactly the kind of thing to outsource.

## Approach
Use \`@radix-ui/react-popover\` for positioning + \`cmdk\` (the library shadcn's Command uses) for the filtered list and keyboard handling.

\`\`\`tsx
<Popover.Root open={mentionQuery !== null}>
  <Popover.Anchor asChild><textarea ... /></Popover.Anchor>
  <Popover.Content side="top" align="start" sideOffset={8}>
    <Command>
      <Command.List>
        {candidates.map(a => <Command.Item key={a.id} ...>...</Command.Item>)}
      </Command.List>
    </Command>
  </Popover.Content>
</Popover.Root>
\`\`\`

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Mention popup uses Radix Popover for positioning (no more hand-rolled \`getBoundingClientRect\`).
- [ ] \`cmdk\` handles arrow keys, Enter, Escape, filtering. The textarea \`onKeyDown\` is freed from mention-specific branches.
- [ ] Trigger has \`role="combobox"\`, popup has \`role="listbox"\`, items have \`role="option"\` (cmdk provides this).
- [ ] Selecting an item commits the mention at the current caret (existing \`commitMention\` logic preserved).
- [ ] Popup never gets clipped (Radix portals to \`<body>\`).
- [ ] Visual styling preserved (\`.mention-pop\`, \`.mention-row\` classes).
- [ ] Works inside both the main \`ChatView\` composer and the \`ThreadPane\` composer.

## Complex pieces
- **Caret tracking.** The popup anchors to the textarea, not to the caret. For now, anchoring to the textarea (current behavior) is acceptable; "anchor to caret" is a follow-up.
- **Controlled-open state.** Radix Popover is controlled or uncontrolled. We need controlled because the open state is driven by \"\`@\` was typed at caret\". Wire \`open={mentionQuery !== null}\` + \`onOpenChange\`.
- **Composer collapse.** When the composer is collapsed via the new \`▼\` button, the popup must close. Easy via \`onOpenChange\` + the \`collapsed\` state.

## Estimate
4–6 h.

title: Replace homegrown ContextMenu with Radix (a11y win)
description:
## Why
\`web/src/components/ContextMenu.tsx\` is a 70-line homegrown context menu used by four surfaces (Sidebar room items, MessageRow, AgentRoster cards — future TimelineRail nodes). It works visually but has **zero accessibility**:
- No keyboard navigation (arrow keys don't move focus through items).
- No focus trap when open.
- No \`aria-haspopup\` / \`aria-expanded\` / \`role="menu"\` wiring on the trigger.
- No screen-reader announcement of menu open/close.
- Submenus would be impossible to add safely.

This is load-bearing for any user on keyboard or assistive tech.

## Approach
Replace with \`@radix-ui/react-context-menu\` (works headless — bring our own brutalist CSS). No Tailwind required for this slice.

\`\`\`tsx
<ContextMenu.Root>
  <ContextMenu.Trigger asChild>{children}</ContextMenu.Trigger>
  <ContextMenu.Portal>
    <ContextMenu.Content className="ctx-menu">
      <ContextMenu.Item onSelect={...}>Reply in thread</ContextMenu.Item>
      <ContextMenu.Separator />
      <ContextMenu.Item className="ctx-danger" onSelect={...}>Delete</ContextMenu.Item>
    </ContextMenu.Content>
  </ContextMenu.Portal>
</ContextMenu.Root>
\`\`\`

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] All four context-menu surfaces (Sidebar room item, MessageRow, AgentRoster card, planned TimelineRail node) use Radix \`ContextMenu.Root\`.
- [ ] Keyboard: arrow keys navigate items, Enter selects, Escape closes, focus returns to trigger.
- [ ] ARIA: trigger gets \`aria-haspopup="menu"\`, content has \`role="menu"\`, items have \`role="menuitem"\` (Radix handles this).
- [ ] Existing brutalist styling preserved (\`.ctx-menu\`, \`.ctx-item\`, \`.ctx-divider\`, \`.ctx-danger\` classes).
- [ ] No visual regression in light or dark mode.
- [ ] Bundle delta < 15 kB gzipped.

## Complex pieces
- **\`useContextMenu()\` hook API change.** Today: \`openMenu(event, items[])\` imperative. Radix is declarative — wrap children in \`<ContextMenu.Root>\`. Need to refactor the four call sites.
- **Items as data, not JSX.** Current callers pass an array of \`MenuEntry\` objects. Either keep that shape and wrap a small \`<ContextMenuFromItems items={...}>\` helper, or refactor each call site to inline \`<ContextMenu.Item>\` JSX (cleaner).
- **Portal rendering.** Radix portals to \`document.body\` by default. Verify it doesn't get clipped behind \`.tab-content { overflow: hidden }\` (it shouldn't — portals escape).

## Estimate
3–4 h.

title: Align web UI to Claude design reference
description: Compare rendered web/ chat UI to docs/ui-reference/claude-design-screenshots/ and patch deltas: missing vertical timeline rail, missing TIP card, missing avatar stack in header, missing READY agent section, seed data only shows 2 messages instead of full conversation, missing image-attachment message style.

WORKING group gets a 2px pink left tick. Cards are buttons; clicking sets focusedAgent (visual state only in V0 — dim semantics on messages/timeline are V1). title: Composer with @mention autocomplete

Inline code uses JetBrains Mono on a tinted paper-2 background. Block code uses --code-bg/--code-fg surface (dark even in light mode — explicit design call from chat 1). Tables get ink borders and zebra-striped paper-2 rows. Acceptance: feed the component the prototype's seed message bodies (lists, tables, blockquotes, code fences, inline code) and they all render correctly + sanitize <script>/<iframe>.

title: ChatView + MessageRow with grouped bubbles

Each bubble: 32x32 avatar (single letter, identity color fill, ink border); author chip (agent name + WORKING/READY/IDLE/OFF status pill + role like 'Backend / refactors'); message body via the Markdown component; reactions strip below the bubble (V0 read-only, click-to-react is V1). Consecutive messages from the same author group with margin-top: -14px so they stack tightly; both bubbles in a group keep their full --shadow. 'You' bubbles are right-aligned with --you-bg (muted warm gray). Acceptance: matches grouped-light.png and 10-present-chat-overview-clean.png message flow.

Below: tab strip with CHAT (active in V0) / BOARD / ARTIFACTS pills. BOARD and ARTIFACTS render placeholder panels in V0 ('coming next' empty state). Right-side 'room actions' kebab button (opens placeholder menu in V0). Each room item: 38x38 icon with accent fill + status dot, room name + pin glyph, last-message preview, unread pill (pink). Active state inverts to ink fill / paper text. User footer at the bottom: 'You' avatar + handle + online dot. Reads via useRooms() hook; clicking a room calls setActiveRoom(id) prop. Implement the .app-shell grid (300px sidebar + main split), .sidebar, .main, .main-body (1fr 112px 260px). Include base typography (Archivo Black / Space Grotesk / JetBrains Mono) and the brutalist primitives (.icon-btn, .plus-btn, .searchbox, kbd, count-chip). Theme toggle wired via document.documentElement.dataset.theme. Acceptance: applying data-theme='dark' on the html element swaps the entire palette without page reload.

title: DaemonDataSource (polling) + sticky web peer registration
description: Implement web/src/data/daemon.ts: implements the same DataSource interface as Mock, but backed by the daemon's REST endpoints (GET /groups, GET /groups/:id/history, GET /peers, POST /dm, POST /groups/:id/messages). On connect(), reads or generates a sticky peer id (key: synchronize.peer_id, prefix web:), POSTs /peers/register with tool='web'. Runs a 1-2s polling loop against GET /peers/:peer_id/inbox; translates daemon Event rows into client-side Message/Artifact deltas via the type table in DESIGN.md. Exposes a runtime switch (localStorage.SYNCHRONIZE_DATA_SOURCE = 'mock' | 'live') in App.tsx. Acceptance: with the daemon running and a peer joining a group, messages appear in the UI within 2s; sending from the composer round-trips through POST /groups/:id/messages.
notes: Part of sync-jix V0. Polling transport — SSE comes in V1.

title: DataSource interface + MockDataSource

title: Web chat UI for synchronize agent sessions
description:
## Goal
Build a custom web UI that renders all synchronize agent sessions (Claude, Codex, Pi, CLI) and their interactions — DMs, group threads, inbox, media — in a single browser surface.

## Why
Today the only way to observe live agent activity across sessions is the CLI (`synchronize top`, `synchronize inbox`) or by tailing logs / poking the daemon REST API. A web UI gives a richer, persistent view: chronological message timelines per peer/group, live event stream, media previews, group membership, identity attribution (claude / codex / pi / cli), and history search.

## Scope (high-level — break into child issues before implementing)
- Daemon-side: confirm the REST surface in `src/api/` is sufficient for read paths; add SSE/WebSocket event stream if polling is insufficient.
- Frontend: standalone web app (likely Bun + Vite + React or similar — decide before implementation). Talks to the daemon over the existing REST API plus a live event channel.
- Auth/binding: stays local-first; only loopback by default. LAN mode reuses `SYNCHRONIZE_TOKEN`.
- Views (minimum): peers list with tool attribution, per-peer DM thread, group list + thread + history, inbox, media gallery, live event firehose.
- Packaging: a `make web` / `make install-web` target consistent with the existing install matrix.

## Non-goals (v0)
- No write/send from the UI initially — read-only observer first; sending is a follow-up epic.
- No remote/cloud sync, no auth beyond the daemon's existing bind+token model.
- No theming/extensibility framework.

## Out of this epic
- Child issues for: tech-stack decision, REST/SSE gap analysis, frontend scaffold, each view, install/packaging, manual smoke.

Lets a live Pi session receive synchronize events as steer/followUp user messages while pi-mcp-adapter continues owning the outbound tool surface. Plan: ~/.claude/plans/ok-great-i-think-keen-frost.md

title: Split daemon repository media and subscription helpers
description:
## Context
Beyond route handling, `src/daemon.ts` contains database read/write helpers, row formatting, media hash/copy/index behavior, and subscriber callback fanout. These are distinct responsibilities and should be isolated after route boundaries are stable.

## Specific objective
Extract daemon persistence, media-store, and subscription delivery helpers into focused modules without changing database schema or runtime behavior.

## Impact area
- `src/daemon.ts`
- New `src/daemon/repo/*` modules for peers/events/groups/media helpers
- New `src/daemon/media-store.ts`
- New `src/daemon/subscriptions.ts`
- Existing DB and media behavior used by REST routes and MCP callback delivery

## Acceptance criteria
- Database row interfaces and helper functions are grouped by domain.
- Media copy/hash/index/README behavior is isolated and unchanged.
- Subscription callback fanout and delivered_at semantics are isolated and unchanged.
- No schema or migration change is introduced.
- Tests continue to prove DM, groups, media, inbox, and MCP channel delivery behavior.

## How to verify
- Run `bun run typecheck`.
- Run `bun test tests/messaging.test.ts`.
- Run `bun test tests/mcp.test.ts`.
- Run `bun test tests/mcp-e2e.test.ts`.
- Run `bun test` after the split.

## Test plan
Existing integration tests are the primary guard because this issue touches persistence and event delivery. Add focused tests only if extraction reveals untested behavior.

## Dependencies
- Daemon route/validation split issue.
acceptance_criteria: Database helper functions, media-store filesystem helpers, and subscription fanout are separated from route handlers; behavior remains unchanged under full tests.

title: Split daemon route handlers and validation helpers
description:
## Context
`src/daemon.ts` currently has a single large route function with all endpoint matching, body parsing, validation, and response construction. Splitting route handlers will make future REST changes safer and easier to review.

## Specific objective
Extract daemon route dispatch and validation helpers into `src/daemon/` modules without changing endpoint behavior.

## Impact area
- `src/daemon.ts`
- New route modules under `src/daemon/routes/`
- Shared validation/auth helpers under `src/daemon/validation.ts` and possibly `src/daemon/auth.ts`
- Existing tests in `tests/health.test.ts`, `tests/messaging.test.ts`, and `tests/mcp-e2e.test.ts`

## Acceptance criteria
- Endpoint paths, HTTP methods, status codes, and JSON response shapes remain unchanged.
- Request body validation and query parsing behave identically.
- `src/daemon.ts` remains a working executable entrypoint.
- Route modules do not duplicate persistence logic unnecessarily.
- No direct changes to database schema are included.

## How to verify
- Run `bun run typecheck`.
- Run `bun test tests/health.test.ts`.
- Run `bun test tests/messaging.test.ts`.
- Run `bun test tests/mcp-e2e.test.ts` if subscription routes are touched.

## Test plan
Integration tests for health/status, peers, DM, inbox, groups, media, and subscriptions through existing test files.

## Dependencies
- Daemon boundary design issue.
acceptance_criteria: REST route handlers and request/query validation are moved into daemon modules; src/daemon.ts remains executable; REST behavior and errors remain unchanged.

title: Design daemon module boundaries for route persistence and media
description:
## Context
`src/daemon.ts` is the largest TypeScript file in the repo at roughly 1077 lines. It mixes REST route dispatch, request validation, database row types/helpers, subscription fanout, media-store filesystem behavior, daemon startup, auth, and response/error handling. This should be modularized, but it is higher risk than API/CLI/MCP splitting because it owns the core persistence and delivery behavior.

## Specific objective
Design daemon module boundaries before moving code. This issue should produce a clear plan for splitting `src/daemon.ts` while preserving the daemon REST contract and all current tests.

## Suggested target shape
```text
src/daemon.ts -> compatibility executable entrypoint
src/daemon/
  index.ts or server.ts       startup and Bun.serve wiring
  context.ts                  DaemonContext and setup
  auth.ts                     bind/token validation and auth checks
  validation.ts               request body/query validation helpers
  routes.ts                   top-level route dispatcher
  routes/
    health.ts
    status.ts
    peers.ts
    messaging.ts
    groups.ts
    media.ts
    subscriptions.ts
    events.ts
  repo/
    peers.ts
    events.ts
    groups.ts
    media.ts
  media-store.ts              file copy/hash/index/readme behavior
  subscriptions.ts            callback fanout and delivery marking
```

The final implementation can adjust this if simpler boundaries emerge, but route handling, persistence helpers, subscription fanout, and media filesystem logic should not remain tangled in one file.

## Impact area
- `src/daemon.ts`
- Future `src/daemon/*` modules
- Tests covering REST, messaging, MCP callback subscription, media, and health/status

## Acceptance criteria
- A daemon module map is captured in notes/design.
- The plan identifies route modules, shared validation/auth helpers, repository helpers, subscription fanout, and media-store helpers.
- The plan explicitly preserves the REST API contract and database behavior.
- The plan defines verification after each daemon split stage.

## How to verify
- Inspect `src/daemon.ts` section boundaries and confirm no planned import cycles.
- Run `bun run typecheck` before any implementation starts.
- No runtime behavior should change in this design-only issue.

## Test plan
No runtime tests required unless scaffolding is introduced. If scaffolding is introduced, run `bun run typecheck`.

## Dependencies
- Overall module boundary design issue.
acceptance_criteria: Daemon split boundaries are documented before code movement; route, validation, persistence, subscription, media, and startup responsibilities are assigned to modules; no code behavior changes required.
notes: Design doc: docs/daemon-modularization-plan.md (worktree-refactor-daemon-design). Covers eight current concerns, target module map including the missed web/* group (file grew 1077→2169 since epic was filed), import direction, route-precedence overlap table, in-scope vs deferred duplications (zod fragments → sync-3wc; DB-row↔api/types.ts dedup → follow-up; route boilerplate wrapper → defer), and per-phase verification gate. Phase A = sync-mkj.10 (routes/validation/auth), Phase B = sync-mkj.11 (repo/media/subscriptions/web).

title: Update imports docs and skill references after module split
description:
## Context
After the code is split, docs and skill files may still mention `src/cli.ts`, `src/mcp.ts`, or specific behavior. Compatibility entrypoints should make most external references stable, but internal development docs may need updating.

## Specific objective
Audit and update repository references after the module split.

## Impact area

- skills/synchronize-codex/SKILL.md
- skills/synchronize-claude/SKILL.md
- bin/synchronize and bin/synchronize-mcp only if needed
- tests imports if any moved symbols require compatibility exports

## Acceptance criteria
- Existing user-facing commands remain documented through stable entrypoints.
- Skill YAML frontmatter is preserved.
- Claude MCP install command remains correct.
- No docs imply CLI supports MCP-only realtime channel notifications.
- No stale internal import paths remain in tests or docs.

## How to verify
- Run `rg -n "src/(cli|mcp|api)\.ts|src/mcp/|src/cli/|src/api/" README.md skills tests bin src` and inspect hits for correctness.
- Run `bun run typecheck`.
- Run focused tests for any changed import paths.

## Test plan
Static verification plus TypeScript compile; no new runtime behavior expected.

## Dependencies
- CLI split completed.
- MCP tool split completed.
acceptance_criteria: Docs, skills, tests, and bin wrappers point at compatibility entrypoints or updated module paths; no stale references to moved internals remain.
close_reason: No doc/skill/bin updates needed: src/{api,cli,mcp}.ts compat shims preserve every existing reference (README examples, bin imports, test spawn cmd args). Skills folders contain no src/ references.

title: Split MCP tool registration by operation domain
description:
## Context
After runtime extraction, MCP tool registration can be split by domain. This keeps `createMcpServer` navigable and makes each tool family easier to maintain.

## Specific objective
Move MCP tool registration into domain files while preserving the same tool names, descriptions, input schemas, and returned JSON text behavior.

## Impact area
- `src/mcp/server.ts` or equivalent server factory module
- `src/mcp/tools/peers.ts`
- `src/mcp/tools/messaging.ts`
- `src/mcp/tools/groups.ts`
- `src/mcp/tools/media.ts`
- `src/mcp/tools/diagnostics.ts`

## Acceptance criteria
- Tool names remain exactly: bridge_register, bridge_whoami, bridge_list_peers, bridge_dm, bridge_inbox, bridge_create_group, bridge_join_group, bridge_leave_group, bridge_send_group, bridge_group_history, bridge_list_groups, bridge_share_media, bridge_list_media, bridge_get_media.
- Tool descriptions and schemas remain compatible with current MCP clients.
- `createMcpServer()` remains exported from `src/mcp.ts` or a compatibility re-export.
- Shared helper behavior such as `text()` and `requirePeer()` is not duplicated across tool modules.

## How to verify
- Run `bun run typecheck`.
- Run `bun test tests/mcp-e2e.test.ts`.
- Run a tool-listing check through the MCP client test or manual MCP connection and compare tool names.

## Test plan
E2E: MCP stdio adapter tests validate tool availability and core tool flows.

## Dependencies
- MCP runtime split completed.
acceptance_criteria: MCP tools are grouped under src/mcp/tools/ by domain; tool names and schemas remain unchanged; createMcpServer remains the public server factory.
close_reason: Closed

title: Split MCP notification subscription and lifecycle runtime
description:
## Context
`src/mcp.ts` combines server setup, instructions, state, Codex polling notifier, Claude callback subscription, notification formatting, heartbeat, cleanup, tool registration, and compatibility main execution.

## Specific objective
Extract MCP runtime concerns into focused modules before splitting tool registration.

## Impact area

- New `src/mcp/notifications.ts`
- New `src/mcp/codex-notifier.ts`
- New `src/mcp/claude-subscription.ts`

- Tests importing `NotificationBridge`, `EventSubscription`, or `emitMcpNotification`

## Acceptance criteria
- `emitMcpNotification`, `NotificationBridge`, and `EventSubscription` remain exported through a compatibility path used by tests.
- Claude channel payload keeps `params.content` and string metadata fields.
- Codex notification behavior remains log-visible/buffered as before.

## How to verify

- Run `bun test tests/mcp.test.ts`.
- Run `bun test tests/mcp-e2e.test.ts`.

## Test plan

title: Split shared API facade by REST domain
description:
## Context
`src/api.ts` is the shared typed REST operation layer used by both CLI and MCP. It is the safest first code movement because it has a clean responsibility: wrap REST endpoints and export DTOs.

## Specific objective

- REST operation functions are grouped by domain.
- `src/api.ts` continues to export the same public symbols used by CLI, MCP, and tests.

## Dependencies

title: Design module boundaries and compatibility entrypoints
description:
## Context
The epic target is to split large single files into folders without behavior changes. This issue defines the exact module boundaries before moving code.

## Specific objective

## Impact area

## Acceptance criteria
- Final module map is written down before code moves.
- `src/api.ts`, `src/cli.ts`, and `src/mcp.ts` compatibility behavior is explicitly defined.
- Boundaries are clear: API contains typed REST operations only; CLI contains command/identity/warning/render concerns; MCP contains tools/notifications/subscriptions/lifecycle concerns.
- The plan avoids circular imports and avoids moving adapter-only policy into the API layer.

## How to verify

- Run `bun run typecheck` before and after any scaffolding changes.
- If only notes/design are changed, verify no code changes are included.

No behavior tests required unless scaffolding is created. If scaffolding is created, run `bun run typecheck`.

## Dependencies
- Manual baseline verification gate.
acceptance_criteria: A concrete module map is recorded in the issue notes or design field; compatibility entrypoints are specified; implementation order and dependency boundaries are clear.

title: Modularize CLI MCP and API TypeScript structure
description:
## Goal
Refactor the synchronized TypeScript code layout from large single files (`src/api.ts`, `src/cli.ts`, `src/mcp.ts`) into domain-scoped folders with smaller files, while preserving all current behavior after the merged REST-facade and MCP hardening work.

## Why this exists
The branch now has the correct architecture: CLI and MCP share `src/api.ts` as a typed REST facade, while CLI-specific guardrails and MCP-only notification/channel loops stay in their adapters. The next code-quality improvement is navigability and file size reduction. Current hotspots are `src/mcp.ts` (~615 lines), `src/cli.ts` (~487 lines), and `src/api.ts` (~325 lines). The files mix multiple logical concerns, making future edits and reviews harder than necessary.

## Target shape
Keep compatibility entrypoints so existing commands, tests, docs, and installed MCP commands do not break:

```text
src/api.ts -> re-export from src/api/index.ts
src/cli.ts -> run src/cli/index.ts
src/mcp.ts -> re-export/create server from src/mcp/index.ts

src/api/
  index.ts
  types.ts
  peers.ts
  inbox.ts
  events.ts
  groups.ts
  media.ts
  status.ts

src/cli/
  index.ts
  flags.ts
  identity.ts
  warnings.ts
  commands/
    status.ts
    top.ts
    peers.ts
    messaging.ts
    groups.ts
    media.ts
  render/
    summary.ts
    table.ts

src/mcp/
  index.ts
  server.ts
  state.ts
  notifications.ts
  codex-notifier.ts
  claude-subscription.ts
  lifecycle.ts
  tools/
    peers.ts
    messaging.ts
    groups.ts
    media.ts
    diagnostics.ts
```

This exact shape can be adjusted during implementation if the code suggests a simpler boundary, but the compatibility entrypoints and behavior preservation are non-negotiable.

## Important constraints
- Do not change public CLI command names, JSON output shapes, MCP tool names, or REST behavior as part of this refactor.
- Do not move CLI guardrails into the shared API layer. CLI identity checks and warnings remain CLI concerns.
- Do not move MCP notification/channel/subscription loops into the shared API layer. MCP runtime behavior remains MCP concern.
- `src/api/*` should remain a typed REST operation facade only.
- Run a verification loop after each child issue, not only at the end.

## Manual verification prerequisite
Before implementation begins, run the current merged branch manually with several agents and confirm existing behavior still works. This is intentionally a separate child issue so the code refactor starts from a trusted baseline.
acceptance_criteria: Manual baseline verification is captured before implementation; API, CLI, and MCP modules are split by domain behind compatibility entrypoints; each stage passes typecheck and focused tests; final branch passes full test suite and MCP e2e.
notes: User highlighted daemon.ts should also be considered. Include daemon modularization as a later phase because src/daemon.ts is currently the largest file (~1077 lines) and mixes route dispatch, validation, persistence helpers, subscription fanout, media-store filesystem behavior, and server startup. Daemon split should happen after API/CLI/MCP adapter splits so behavioral surface is already stable.

title: Document resolved Claude channel delivery bug
description:
## Summary
Claude-to-Claude synchronize DMs were persisted and marked delivered, but the recipient Claude session did not visibly receive a channel auto-prompt.

## Symptoms
- Both peers could register as tool=claude through MCP.
- Sending a DM created an events row and recipient inbox row.
- The daemon callback path marked delivered_at and advanced last_cursor, proving daemon-to-MCP callback delivery.
- Claude UI still did not surface the channel message.

## Root causes found
- synchronize emitted notifications/claude/channel but did not provide Claude channel handling instructions comparable to the working claude-peers MCP reference.
- Claude channel meta payload differed from claude-peers: numeric and nullable fields plus meta.source could plausibly be rejected or ignored when rendered as channel attributes.
- delivered_at only proved local callback success, not Claude UI surfacing.
- In-memory callback subscriptions were lost on daemon restart and were not restored by heartbeat.
- MCP lifecycle cleanup and heartbeat were weaker than claude-peers.
- CLI fallback remained valid but did not explicitly warn agents that CLI peers cannot provide Claude channel real-time prompts.

## Solution implemented
- Added MCP server instructions telling Claude how to react to <channel source="synchronize" ...> and reply with bridge_dm.
- Normalized Claude channel metadata to reference-compatible string-only fields, omitted nulls, removed meta.source, and added from_id/sent_at aliases.
- Added detailed stderr logs in client, daemon, and MCP adapter for daemon discovery, registration, subscriptions, event storage, callback delivery, notification emission, heartbeat, and cleanup.
- Added Claude callback emission failure handling: MCP callback returns 502 if notification emission fails, so daemon does not mark delivered_at as success.
- Added MCP heartbeat and Claude re-subscribe/peer restore logic after daemon restart.
- Added cleanup on stdio close/SIGTERM for both src/mcp.ts and bin/synchronize-mcp.
- Added bridge_whoami diagnostics for notify_mode, subscription state, notifier state, and heartbeat state.
- Added CLI fallback warnings on register/send actions explaining that real-time Claude channel prompts do not work through CLI peers and inbox polling/checking is required.

## Verification
- TypeScript typecheck passed.
- Focused MCP and messaging tests passed.
- Claude MCP was reinstalled to /Users/abhirupdas/.bun/bin/synchronize-mcp with SYNCHRONIZE_MCP_MODE=claude.
- Runtime was reset and claude mcp get synchronize reported Status: Connected.
- User live-tested after the fixes and reported: Works great now.
acceptance_criteria: Resolved and documented. Code contains tests for reference-compatible Claude channel payload, MCP channel instructions, CLI fallback warnings, and MCP delivery paths.
close_reason: Resolved by Claude channel hardening and documentation in code/tests/progress log.

title: Improve top/status display for duplicate session names
description:
top and status are technically correct but confusing when multiple active peer rows share a session_name. The dashboard should make duplicates and group membership clearer during local agent testing.

## Context
Live snapshot showed 4 online peers, but only 2 logical active group members. Two duplicate peers had groups=0 while two were in demo-test. The user asked whether top/status were working correctly.
acceptance_criteria: top highlights duplicate session names or shows peer_id suffix prominently; summary separates total peer rows from grouped/active members where useful; status/top docs mention that peers are session instances; tests verify summary counts remain accurate.
close_reason: Superseded by sync-dx2 (alias#<suffix> rendering in TUI + web). Duplicate session_name rows will be visually distinguishable once the suffix is rendered everywhere; no separate top/status fix needed.

title: Avoid duplicate active peers for repeated CLI registrations
description:
Repeated CLI registration for the same session name currently mints a new peer_id every time unless the caller explicitly reuses a peer_id. During live testing this produced duplicate active peers for alice and claude-synchronize-dev, which made status/top show four peers when only two logical agents were being tested.

## Context
Observed in ~/.synchronize: two active alice peers and two active claude-synchronize-dev peers. status/top were correct because they count peer rows, but registration semantics are confusing for CLI fallback and live testing.
acceptance_criteria: CLI registration either reuses/updates the current cli-peer.json peer for the same session or provides an explicit --replace/--new-session split; status/top documentation clarifies peer rows vs logical sessions; tests cover repeated CLI register calls without accidental duplicate active logical peers.
close_reason: Verified fixed in current code: resolveCliRegisterPeerId (src/cli/identity.ts:16) + findReusablePeer (src/api/status.ts:12) reuse the same peer_id on repeated 'synchronize register --name <same>' calls. Manual verification with 3x register from one home yields a single peer row. The duplicate-peer scenario in the original report predates findReusablePeer landing on master.

title: Reintroduce adaptive model selection for daemon/AOE launches after v0 simulations
description: Temporary v0 policy pins daemon/AOE launches to cheap models (Claude haiku, Pi gpt-5.4-mini) and strips caller model args to avoid simulation cost. After lifecycle simulations are stable and v0 readiness is closer, design an explicit/adaptive model-selection surface that restores controlled overrides without accidental expensive defaults.

title: DEFERRED (last in epic): in-app tabs + deep-linking (Phase 4)
description: The tab vision, intentionally deferred past v0 to keep the initial app simple. (1) Deep-linking: App.tsx reads URLSearchParams (?room=&thread=) into initial state + history.replaceState reflection. (2) In-app tab strip (model A: one webview, one shared daemon connection regardless of tab count — chosen for RAM). Pull forward ONLY once Phases 1-3 work and tabs are actually wanted.
acceptance_criteria: A thread/room can be opened in its own in-app tab; tabs persist/restore; still a single webview/connection.

title: Investigate approving synchronize as a Claude channel (avoid dev-channel flag/prompt)
description: Spawned Claude agents must use --dangerously-load-development-channels server:synchronize to register the live push channel (decompiled claude 2.1.158: a server: channel only registers when O.dev===true; allowedChannelPlugins only gates kind==='plugin' marketplace plugins). That flag triggers an interactive 'I am using this for local development' confirmation every launch, which we currently auto-dismiss via tmux send-keys Enter in AoeBackend. There is NO managed-settings allowlist that accepts a server: channel. Investigate making synchronize an approved channel without the dev flag: e.g. package the synchronize channel as a marketplace plugin (plugin:synchronize@<marketplace>) so it can go in allowedChannelPlugins, or track Claude Code channel GA which may add a server-channel allowlist. Refs: docs/en/channels, channels-reference, settings. Part of sync-gsx follow-up.

title: Derive group_members.active from lease instead of flipping it on peer death
description:
## Context (for an agent with no prior context)

synchronize tracks group membership in `group_members` with an `active` flag (1=member, 0=not). Two different events set `active=0`:
1. A peer voluntarily LEAVES a group (leaveGroup).
2. A peer DIES — soft-deleted by the retention sweeper (`sweepExpiredPeers` in src/daemon.ts) after its lease has been expired past retention, or by an operator evict (`DELETE /peers/:id`).

These two are indistinguishable in the schema today (both set `active=0` + `left_at`).

## The problem

When a dead peer comes back (re-registers with the same peer_id — e.g. a Pi process whose peer was swept during long daemon downtime, or a resumed Claude session), the peer row resurrects (`upsertPeer` clears `deleted_at`) but its memberships were left `active=0`. So a revived agent is online yet silent/absent in all its groups.

sync-3nu shipped a STOPGAP: on resurrect, `reactivateMembershipsOnResurrect` (src/daemon.ts) re-activates exactly the rows whose `left_at == the cleared deleted_at` (death-deactivated rows, distinguished from voluntary leaves by timestamp correlation), skipping any alias reclaimed during the gap. It works but is subtle and timestamp-correlation-based.

## What to build (the structural fix)

Stop using `active=0` as the death signal. Instead:
- A peer is "currently in a group" iff it has a `group_members` row it has NOT voluntarily left.
- Whether that member is ONLINE is derived from the peer lease at read time (already how peer presence works).
- Death (sweep / evict / lease lapse) must NOT flip `active`. Only a genuine leaveGroup sets `active=0`.

Then resurrection needs no membership reactivation — the membership was never deactivated by death — and the whole `reactivateMembershipsOnResurrect` + timestamp-correlation machinery (and its alias-collision guard) can be deleted.

## The hard constraint: reclaim detection

Alias-reclaim detection currently relies on `active=0` rows: when a NEW peer claims an alias whose previous holder is gone, the daemon finds the previous holder via a query on `active=0 AND alias=?` and fires `group_member_alias_reclaimed`. If death stops setting `active=0`, reclaim detection breaks. Re-found reclaim on "previous holder is offline (lease lapsed) / soft-deleted" rather than "active=0". Audit every read of `group_members.active` (grep `active` in src/daemon.ts — rosters, online_members counts, the unique-active-alias index `idx_group_members_alias WHERE active=1`, the reclaim query, MEMBER_SELECT_SQL).

## Acceptance criteria

- A swept/evicted peer that re-registers is immediately an active member of all groups it had not voluntarily left, with NO reactivation step.
- `reactivateMembershipsOnResurrect` and the `priorDeletedAt` capture in `upsertPeer` are removed.
- A peer that voluntarily LEFT a group does NOT rejoin it on re-register.
- Alias-reclaim event still fires when a new peer claims a dead/offline peer's alias.
- The unique-active-alias invariant still holds (no two live members share an alias).
- tests/peer-revival.test.ts still passes (revival restores membership; revival does not steal a reclaimed alias).
- Existing soft-delete/reclaim lock-in tests in tests/api.test.ts still pass.

## Files

- src/daemon.ts: sweepExpiredPeers, DELETE /peers/:id handler, upsertPeer (remove reactivation), leaveGroup handler, all group_members.active read sites, reclaim-detection query, MEMBER_SELECT_SQL.
- src/db.ts: possibly a migration if a "left vs active" distinction needs a column; or derive purely.
- tests/peer-revival.test.ts, tests/api.test.ts (reclaim lock-in).

## References

- sync-3nu (stopgap this replaces), sync-6mz item #3, sync-dmc (original soft-delete), sync-235 (include_inactive).
- session-tracker/plan-agent-ttl-presence-v0.md.
design: sync-6mz item #3 ('group_members.active becomes derived'). Supersedes sync-3nu stopgap. See session-tracker/plan-agent-ttl-presence-v0.md.

title: Reduce per-tool-call overhead of Claude activity hooks
description: The installed PreToolUse/UserPromptSubmit/Stop activity hooks each run a 'synchronize status' precheck before exec'ing 'hook activity', so every Claude tool call spawns two CLI processes. The status precheck exists to avoid auto-starting a daemon from a hook, but it is material overhead in tool-heavy sessions. Options: a no-autostart fast path for 'hook activity' (skip status, have the hook itself no-op if no daemon.json), or a lighter liveness check. Surfaced by Codex review of feat/agent-ttl-presence.

title: Makefile: setup target covers all launch deps + worktree bootstrap
description: Extend 'make setup' so dependency setup is part of the default flow: install root+web deps, verify/install launch prerequisites (aoe, tmux, uv, bun), and bootstrap a fresh worktree (bun install) in one command. Goal: a single 'make setup' gets a clean checkout/worktree ready to run + test the AOE launch feature. Low priority; do last. Part of sync-gsx.
close_reason: Makefile formalized: help/setup/check-deps + demo-up/spawn/profile/down lifecycle (isolated demo home, AOE teardown). Full demo cycle verified.

title: Web UI: render 3-state presence (initializing/working/idle/online/offline)
description: Surface activity_state in the sidebar/roster with distinct affordances. Generic 'online' for uninstrumented peers; offline greyed.
close_reason: Shipped on master in squash b996cb5 (feat/agent-ttl-presence); verified markers in db.ts migration v3, api/peers.ts activity endpoint, hook.ts + claude-hooks-config.ts, pi extension index.ts, daemon.ts presence derivation, web/data/daemon.ts 3-state. Tests 123/0 green.

title: Document Pi log parser integration
description:
What/why/how:
Document the parser-backed Pi harness path and index the durable plan so future agents can find it. The plan requires writing docs first, creating Beads work, and adding the new design document to the synchronize-debugging skill index in the same change.

Impact area:

How to verify:

title: Design future principal identity model
description: Goal: capture option-2 direction without implementing it now. Whole-plan context: future multi-surface identity should separate human/app principals from peers/connections so web, desktop, iOS, Android, and other surfaces can act as one visible participant with shared membership and read state. Affected areas for the later design include future principals schema, peer-to-principal relationship, group membership, alias uniqueness, mentions, event authorship, read state, and live push delivery. No child implementation issues are required in this pass.
acceptance_criteria:
- Future design direction is represented as an epic.
- Epic states that group membership, alias uniqueness, mentions, and read state should belong to principals.
- Epic states that peers/connections own presence, transport delivery, and exact sender audit trail.
- No option-2 child issues are created in this pass.

title: Future: evolve 'make doctor' into full-fledged synchronize doctor CLI
description:
## Why this issue exists

`make doctor` is the v0 diagnostic surface — a thin shell-script bundle of useful commands. As the synchronize-debugging skill matures and operators rely on it more heavily, the limitations of a Makefile-based diagnostic become apparent:

- No structured output (JSON / machine-readable) for piping to other tools
- No flag-driven filtering ('only show stale peers', 'only show last hour of events')
- No active probing (can it ping the daemon, can it open the DB, can it resolve a host_session_id back to a tmux session)
- No remediation suggestions ('peer X has been soft-deleted for 4h; consider `bd run sync-…`' or 'daemon pid $X is running from worktree Y, not your current worktree')
- No test-mode (run against dev-runtime via flag instead of env-var dance)

The operator already articulated this future state: 'I would want to flesh out the doctor into a full-fledged diagnostic tool to help with debugging, not just a Make command.'

## What needs to be done (when scheduled)

Design and implement `synchronize doctor` as a proper CLI subcommand under `src/cli/commands/`. Likely shape:

- `synchronize doctor` — default human-readable snapshot (superset of `make doctor`)
- `synchronize doctor --json` — machine-readable
- `synchronize doctor peers [--stale] [--soft-deleted]` — peer-focused
- `synchronize doctor events [--since=…] [--group=…]` — event log
- `synchronize doctor probe` — active health probing (ping daemon, open DB, resolve agent_sessions to tmux)
- `synchronize doctor explain <peer-id>` — narrative explanation of a peer's lifecycle from creation to current state
- Cross-references the synchronize-debugging skill output structure so skill stays the canonical reference

## Acceptance criteria

- (Deferred — will be scoped properly when work begins)
- Subcommand surfaces are decided in design phase, not now
- Existing `make doctor` continues to work (likely delegates to the new CLI)

## Dependencies

Depends on sync-11l (basic `make doctor` lands first to validate the diagnostic shape before promoting to a CLI subcommand).

## Notes

Deliberately P3 / deferred. Operator confirmed v0 `make doctor` is sufficient for now; this issue exists so the longer-term vision is not lost.

title: Web UI chat bubbles should size to ~4:1 width:height ratio, not full-row
description:
## Why this issue exists

Currently every chat bubble in the web UI renders as a full-row, flat container regardless of message length. Short messages get the same wide bubble as long messages, which wastes horizontal space and makes the chat feel like a series of long banners rather than a conversation.

## What we want

Make the bubble container adaptively sized so its width-to-height ratio targets roughly 4:1.

- Short messages → narrow, short bubble.
