- Long pasted text → wider until it hits the row max-width, then wraps and grows in height — but the wrap point should be chosen so the resulting bubble is close to 4:1.
- Code blocks / preformatted content can override (they need full width to preserve formatting).

## Acceptance criteria

- Single short message (~1 line) renders as a compact bubble, not full-row.
- Medium message (a paragraph) renders at a width/height ratio close to 4:1.
- Very long messages (multi-paragraph) wrap to roughly 4:1 — width grows up to the row max, then height grows.
- Code blocks (```) keep their existing wide formatting.
- Behavior is the same in both ChatView (main channel) and ThreadPane.

## Implementation notes

- Likely lives in web/src/components/MessageRow.tsx and the bubble styles in web/src/components/extra.css.
- Probably a CSS approach: max-width based on content length, or measure-and-set via ResizeObserver. Either works; CSS is simpler if we can get there.
- Mobile/narrow viewports may need a different aspect target — start with desktop and revisit.

title: PATCH /peers/:id endpoint for lease and metadata updates
description:
## Why this issue exists

The only way to update a peer's lease_expires_at is via the heartbeat endpoint (PATCH /peers/:id/heartbeat), which hard-codes DEFAULT_LEASE_MS. There is no REST API to set a custom or infinite lease, nor to patch other peer metadata (purpose, etc.).

Currently the only workaround is a direct SQLite UPDATE, which is not suitable for normal operation or tooling.

## What needs to be done

Add PATCH /peers/:id accepting a JSON body with optional fields:
- lease_expires_at (ISO string or 'infinite' sentinel)
- purpose (string)

Return the updated peer object.

## Acceptance criteria

- PATCH /peers/:id with {"lease_expires_at": "9999-12-31T23:59:59.000Z"} updates the lease and returns the peer
- PATCH /peers/:id with {"lease_expires_at": "infinite"} sets a far-future date (e.g. year 9999)
- Only the requesting caller's peer or operator-level access can update (non-localhost token required for remote callers)
- Existing heartbeat endpoint is unchanged

title: bridge_list_peers should support include_inactive=true
description: Alice's 2026-05-23 follow-up: group-scoped bridge_list_peers currently returns only active members. That's the right default, but auditing (reclaim trail, post-rejoin history boundaries) needs left/rejoined members too. Add include_inactive: bool optional param; document active=false rows in response shape.

title: bridge_send_group description should note send-time delivery resolution
description: Alice's 2026-05-23 follow-up race-window observation: between her bridge_list_peers and bridge_send_group, karel joined the group. Send-time delivery routing correctly included karel even though pre-send roster didn't. Document explicitly in bridge_send_group description: 'delivery routing is computed at send time, not at roster fetch time' so consumers don't cache rosters incorrectly.

title: Drop recipient_peer_id from group_message event shape
description: Alice's 2026-05-23 follow-up: recipient_peer_id is always null on group_message events — dead weight on that discriminator. Either omit from group_message responses, or document why it stays (matrix consistency for clients that bind one renderer to all event types). Minor — defer until we revisit the wire shape.

title: Alias-to-peer-id resolution receipt on bridge_send_group
description: Alice's 2026-05-23 follow-up: mentions: ["612c79ac-..."] tells the sender who got mentioned by peer_id but not which @alias token resolved to which peer. Cheap addition: include resolved: {"@bob": "612c79ac-...", "@karel": "3fe46bbf-..."} on send response. Matters for audit and for cases where multiple members share a session_name across rejoins.

title: Accelerate test suite — currently ~30-47s for ~43 tests
description:
The full `bun test` run takes ~30-47s for ~43 tests in 7 files. Each test that spawns a daemon pays for the full daemon startup cycle (spawn bun, parse src/daemon.ts, open SQLite WAL, write daemon.json, fetch /health). With many such tests, the suite cost is dominated by daemon spinup.

Avenues to research when picking this up:

1. **Daemon reuse across tests in the same file.** Bun's beforeAll/afterAll can spin one daemon per file and have tests use isolated SYNCHRONIZE_HOME per test (by writing to different dirs within the daemon's purview). Catch: most tests assume a clean per-test daemon for fresh state. Would need a per-test reset endpoint or per-test DB.

2. **In-process daemon for tests.** Refactor the daemon entry to export a startDaemon(config) function that runs in the test process (Bun.serve in-memory) rather than spawning a subprocess. Eliminates the bun-startup cost entirely. Big refactor but probably the biggest win.

3. **Parallel safety.** The 2026-05-23 port-pin (DEFAULT_PORT=58405) broke parallel tests because spawned daemons collide. We mitigated with explicit SYNCHRONIZE_PORT=0 in tests but some tests still serialize when they shouldn't.

4. **Profile to find the actual bottleneck.** bun test --timeout / --slow flags may show which tests dominate. Worth measuring before optimizing.

## Acceptance

Target: full suite under 10s on a modern laptop. Should not require sacrificing daemon isolation per test (each test deserves a fresh DB unless the test name says otherwise).

## Background

Observed during the 2026-05-23 v0 manual-test campaign — when iterating on customer-feedback fixes, the 30s suite turnaround broke flow. Filing for later.

title: MCP tool descriptions need explicit Returns: block + per-tool idempotency contract
description: Alice asked for "Returns:" blocks listing response shape (currently must call tool to discover it). Bob asked for explicit per-tool idempotency contract (is bridge_register idempotent? bridge_create_group on existing? bridge_rename_in_group to current alias?). Touches every src/mcp/tools/*.ts description. Bob's idempotency #7 finding. Requires MCP adapter reinstall.
close_reason: Shipped in 87e9bfe (feat(mcp): structured error envelope + parsed mentions + event_ids filter). Lock-in test in tests/mcp-e2e.test.ts.

title: bridge_group_history default view gives no signal that thread replies exist
description:
Default `bridge_group_history` returns only main-channel events (`parent_event_id IS NULL`). Per spec, that is correct — Slack-style thread replies should not clutter the main view. But the returned event objects carry no signal that a thread exists for any given main-channel event: no `reply_count`, no `has_replies`, no `last_reply_event_id`. An agent inspecting the response has no cheap way to know threads exist.

## Evidence — live agent confidently misread state

In group `Manual-Test-V0` (13 events, 4 active threads rooted at events 4/6/8/12), the user asked the live Claude agent bob: "How many threads are active?" bob replied: "Zero. Every message so far was sent to the main channel with parent_event_id: null." SQLite cross-check contradicts:

```
sqlite> SELECT event_id, parent_event_id FROM events WHERE parent_event_id IS NOT NULL;
5|4
7|6
9|8
11|8
13|12
```

bob made no factual error in its own reasoning — it just inspected the default history view and saw all-null parent_event_ids. The view itself misled it.

## Why this matters

Agents are the primary consumers of `bridge_group_history`. A response shape that requires per-event probing (`bridge_group_history(thread_of=<id>)` for each main-channel event) to discover threads is asymmetric: cheap for the daemon to provide, expensive for an agent to derive.

## Fix proposals (pick one)

1. **Augment default history rows.** Add `reply_count: integer` and `last_reply_event_id: integer | null` per main-channel event. Single subquery in the existing history handler (`src/daemon.ts` ~line 803).

2. **New `bridge_group_threads({ name })` tool.** Returns `[{ root_event_id, reply_count, last_reply_at }]` — a thread summary endpoint. Keeps history clean; adds an explicit discovery affordance.

Proposal 1 is preferred — single round trip, no new tool surface, agents can discover threads with the same call they already make. Cost: one COUNT/MAX subquery per main-channel row (cheap with the existing thread index).

## Where to consider this

`src/daemon.ts` group-history handler (~line 803) — already filters `parent_event_id IS NULL` for the main view. Add the subqueries there.

`src/api/types.ts` Event interface — add the two new optional fields.

`skills/synchronize-claude/SKILL.md` and `skills/synchronize-pi/SKILL.md` — once the metadata exists, document it briefly in the routing-matrix section so agents know to inspect it.
close_reason: Server-side fix shipped 2026-05-23: sync-ecs honored env name in resolveSessionName (commit 576bef4); sync-0gl added reply_count + last_reply_event_id to default history rows (commit 2bca157). Both verified live with bob/alice/karel and locked in tests/api.test.ts.

title: Integration: assert mention warnings through MCP tool or CLI, not direct REST
description:
scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/group_policy_cli.py around lines 152-158 verifies that an unresolved @-token in a group send produces the expected warning shape — but it does so by hitting the REST endpoint POST /groups/:name/messages directly via requests, not by going through synchronize group send (CLI) or bridge_send_group (MCP). A regression in how the MCP tool surfaces warnings (or in how the CLI passes them through to stdout / its response object) would slip through this assertion.

ROOT CAUSE OF THE GAP:
The audit was looking at whether the warning shape was tested end-to-end. The current scenario tests the daemon's response shape, but the daemon side is already covered by tests/api.test.ts. The integration value is specifically the surface-level passthrough: daemon → REST → MCP/CLI → caller. That last hop is the only thing this scenario adds, and it is bypassed.

SUGGESTED FIX:
Keep the direct-REST check (defense in depth is fine, and it documents the wire format), but ADD one assertion that captures the warning via the agent-visible surface:
- For the CLI variant: invoke synchronize group send NAME 'hi @ghost' --as alice. The CLI prints response.event and warnings (or should). Parse stdout JSON and assert warnings[0] == {token: '@ghost', reason: 'alias_not_in_group'}.
- For the MCP variant (optional, in pi_mcp_group_policy.py): inspect the Pi transcript for the bridge_send_group tool_result content and assert it contains the warning object.

If the CLI does not currently print the warnings array (it prints only response.event per the existing src/cli/commands/group.ts handler), THIS ISSUE ALSO FILES A BUG: the CLI should print warnings somewhere visible. Either:
- Print warnings to stderr as a one-line summary ('warning: @ghost is not a member of group').
- Include warnings in the JSON output by default.
- Add --json-warnings flag.
Choose whichever fits the existing CLI ergonomics.

FILES:
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/group_policy_cli.py — primary.
- src/cli/commands/group.ts — if a CLI fix is needed to make warnings visible.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Scenario fails if the daemon strips warnings.
- Scenario fails if REST drops the warnings field.
- Scenario fails if the MCP tool's text() wrapper eats the warnings.
- Scenario fails if the CLI hides warnings from its output.

PARENT: sync-6p4

title: Integration: make loop guard in pi_mcp_thread_baton robust to prompt-template drift
description:
scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/pi_mcp_thread_baton.py around lines 232-246 implements its anti-cyclic-reply guard by checking whether the harness's run_id substring shows up in recent group event bodies. The intent is: if agents keep re-treating each other's test messages as new instructions, the run_id stays in the body and we can spot the loop.

PROBLEM:
If the prompt template ever stops echoing the run_id (refactor of the warmup prompt, model that does not preserve the sentinel, prompt-truncation by a model that hits context limits), the guard fails silent — it stops detecting loops rather than failing loudly. Worst-case: a cyclic-reply storm runs to harness timeout, costing test minutes and noise, and the failure is mysterious because the loop guard didn't catch what it was designed to catch.

SUGGESTED FIX (two layers; recommend both):
- Layer A (contract): make the run_id echo an explicit prompt-template requirement. If the agent's first send into the group does not contain a sentinel of the form <run_id=...>, the harness fails fast with a clear error 'agent did not echo run_id sentinel; loop guard would be silent — aborting'. This makes the contract impossible to silently break.
- Layer B (backstop): add a body-repetition counter independent of run_id format. Maintain a sliding window of the last N event bodies in the group. If the same body (or alternating bodies) repeats more than M times within a small time window, raise a loop alert and capture the offending event IDs. This catches loops even if Layer A's contract is violated.

POSSIBLE EXTRACTION:
Layer B is generic — extract to scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/sync_rest.py as detect_reply_loop(client, group_id, window=10, threshold=4, period_seconds=30). Other group-flavored scenarios can opt in.

FILES:
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/pi_mcp_thread_baton.py — primary.
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/sync_rest.py — for the shared helper.

ACCEPTANCE:
- If an agent enters a reply loop, the harness fails loudly within a bounded number of iterations, regardless of run_id format.
- If the run_id sentinel is missing from the first send, Layer A fails the scenario immediately.
- Both layers can be exercised by a targeted unit-test-style probe (synthetic group events written via REST).

PARENT: sync-6p4

title: Integration: tighten forbidden-tool detection in pi_mcp_thread_baton
description:
scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/pi_mcp_thread_baton.py around lines 248-265 detects 'agent used a forbidden tool' by substring-matching the tool name against the serialized transcript JSON. False-positive risk: any time a forbidden tool name appears anywhere in the transcript text (skill documentation injected via system prompt, model 'thinking' content, error message strings, user prompt echoes, even other tool-result content quoting the forbidden tool's name) the scenario fails spuriously and the harness wastes time chasing a phantom.

Real-world example of how this can bite: if the scenario adds a prompt that says 'Do not use synchronize CLI', the substring 'synchronize CLI' appears in the assistant's response acknowledging the constraint, and the detector trips.

SUGGESTED FIX:
- Parse the Pi JSONL transcript line by line.
- For each row, decode the JSON and look only at role=assistant entries.
- Within each assistant entry, iterate over content blocks.
- Match only block.type == 'tool_use' AND block.name == forbidden_tool_name.

This makes the detector structural rather than textual.

POSSIBLE EXTRACTION:
If multiple scenarios end up needing structured transcript inspection (very likely as the harness grows), extract a helper into scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/pi_env.py — something like extract_tool_uses(transcript_path) -> list[(timestamp, tool_name, args)]. Then scenarios can filter that list cleanly.

FILES:
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/pi_mcp_thread_baton.py — primary.
- Possibly scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/pi_env.py — if a shared helper is extracted.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Detector stops triggering on incidental string matches inside transcripts.
- Detector still catches genuine forbidden tool_use invocations.
- A targeted test: add a prompt mentioning the forbidden tool's name without invoking it; scenario should NOT flag a forbidden-tool violation.

PARENT: sync-6p4

title: Integration: stale @-mention of a since-left member does not push (D7)
description:
The daemon's mention resolver only matches active members (resolveMentions in src/daemon.ts uses WHERE active=1). If alice leaves and someone later types '@alice', the daemon should:
- Resolve no peer for the token (alice is inactive)
- Return warnings: [{token: '@alice', reason: 'alias_not_in_group'}]
- Not push to alice
- Not include alice's peer_id in mentions_json
- Still deliver the message to remaining active members via the normal inbox path

A regression where the resolver matches inactive rows would push to a left peer (or worse, write alice's peer_id into mentions_json on a message that was sent after she left). Pure unit tests cover the resolver behavior but not the surface-level absence-of-push proof.

REPRO SKETCH:
1. Alice and Bob join group; both register with the daemon.
2. (Optional) Register a localhost HTTP callback for alice and bob via POST /subscriptions to capture per-peer push counts.
3. Alice leaves the group via synchronize group leave --as alice.
4. Bob sends 'checking on @alice' via synchronize group send (or bridge_send_group from Pi).
5. Assert the send response includes warnings: [{token: '@alice', reason: 'alias_not_in_group'}].
6. Assert event.mentions_json is null or [] (no peer_id resolved).
7. Assert alice's push-sink hit count for the new event_id is 0.
8. Assert alice's inbox does NOT contain the new event (she is not an active member).

FILES:
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/group_policy_cli.py — cleanest path, deterministic CLI flow.
- If a push-sink helper is added for D4, reuse it here.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Scenario fails if the daemon ever pushes to inactive members for any reason.
- Scenario fails if mentions_json starts including inactive peer_ids.
- Warning shape and absence-of-push are both asserted.

RELATED UNIT TEST:
tests/api.test.ts covers the warning-shape assertion for unresolved tokens. This integration scenario adds: (a) the surface-level proof via real CLI/MCP, and (b) the explicit 'no push to left peer' claim that is currently only implicit in the active-members filter.

PARENT: sync-6p4

title: Integration: directly assert thread-reply push fanout reaches root_author + thread_posters + new mentions (D4)
description:
scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/pi_mcp_thread_baton.py IMPLICITLY exercises the thread-reply push fanout rule — beta and gamma react to a no-mention validation event in the thread, which means push reached them (since they were not mentioned, only inbox+push from thread participation could have woken them). But the harness does not directly assert WHICH peers received the event. A regression that breaks computeThreadParticipants (the daemon helper at src/daemon.ts that computes root_author + prior thread posters) could pass the scenario's behavior check if beta/gamma happen to retry via inbox polling, masking the push regression.

ROUTING RULE BEING TESTED (from docs/group-sync-integrity.md):
thread reply push fanout = (root_author ∪ thread_posters ∪ this-message-mentions) ∩ active_members − {sender}

REPRO SKETCH:
After the alpha-beta-gamma baton sequence, before prompting beta/gamma to acknowledge the validation event, the harness should:

1. Fetch GET /peers/<alpha.peer_id>/inbox. Assert the validation event_id is present (alpha = root author).
2. Fetch GET /peers/<beta.peer_id>/inbox. Assert the validation event_id is present (beta = thread poster).
3. Fetch GET /peers/<gamma.peer_id>/inbox. Assert the validation event_id is present (gamma = thread poster).
4. Negative check: confirm a non-thread-participant (if any exist in the scenario) does NOT have the validation event.

Optional stronger check: register a localhost HTTP callback per peer before the validation send and assert per-peer push hit counts. Inbox-presence is the cheap sufficient signal; subscriber-hit-counts is the proof that PUSH (not just inbox fanout) reached them. Choose based on harness budget.

FILES:
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/pi_mcp_thread_baton.py — between the validation send and the acknowledgment prompts.
- May want a small helper in scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/sync_rest.py for 'wait for event_id in peer inbox'.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Scenario fails if computeThreadParticipants regresses to mentions-only, root-only, or otherwise drops a participant.
- Scenario fails if the daemon ever stops fanning out to thread posters.

RELATED UNIT TEST:
tests/api.test.ts has push-sink-based assertions of the same rule at the daemon layer ('thread reply push reaches root author and prior thread posters along with new mentions'). The integration version adds the MCP-driven-from-real-Pi proof — useful because real Pi exercises the subscriber path differently from a synthetic Bun.serve sink.

PARENT: sync-6p4

title: Integration: assert fresh=true history boundary for late joiners (B11)
description:
history_from_event_id is set at join time per member (UPSERT in src/daemon.ts join handler). A fresh=true joiner is supposed to see only post-join events; a default joiner sees the full group history. This is agent-visible behavior — a Pi agent joining fresh=true mid-conversation should not surface old messages.

REPRO SKETCH:
1. Alice creates group, joins as 'alice'.
2. Alice sends 'pre-bob' message.
3. Bob joins with --fresh on the CLI (fresh: true MCP arg).
4. Alice sends 'post-bob' message.
5. GET /groups/:name/history?peer_id=<bob>. Assert 'pre-bob' is ABSENT, 'post-bob' is PRESENT in the events array.
6. Carol joins WITHOUT --fresh.
7. GET /groups/:name/history?peer_id=<carol>. Assert BOTH 'pre-bob' and 'post-bob' are present.

This proves three things at once: (a) fresh joiner is cut at the join boundary, (b) default joiner gets full history, (c) the per-member history_from_event_id read-time filter actually runs.

FILES:
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/group_policy_cli.py — natural home. Existing scenario already exercises joins + sends; just thread in the --fresh case.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Scenario fails if the daemon ever stops honoring fresh=true at the read path.
- Scenario fails if the CLI or MCP forwarder drops the fresh flag.
- Scenario passes against current daemon (integration tip b7f86cf).

RELATED UNIT TEST:
None directly. tests/messaging.test.ts has a 'before joins' assertion that touches related behavior but only loosely — it doesn't isolate fresh=true vs default joiner. This integration scenario plus a future api.test.ts unit pair would close the loop.

PARENT: sync-6p4

title: Integration: alias-collision 409 surfaces as actionable error to agent (B7)
description:
When two active members try to claim the same alias, the daemon returns 409 alias_collision with a hint instructing the caller to pick a unique alias (string 'Provide a unique alias to join this group.' in src/daemon.ts). The error must traverse the chain:

  daemon → REST 409 → MCP tool / CLI → agent-readable surface

A regression that downgrades the response to a 500, strips the message body, swallows the rejection, or maps it to a misleading error code would not be caught by tests/api.test.ts (which asserts the daemon layer only).

REPRO SKETCH:
1. Alice joins durable group with --alias lead.
2. Bob attempts to join the same group with --alias lead.
3. Assert the request fails.
4. Assert HTTP status is 409.
5. Assert error code is alias_collision.
6. Assert body contains the substring 'unique alias' (the agent-visible hint).

Suggested second variant: do the same flow via the Pi MCP path (bridge_join_group) in pi_mcp_group_policy.py to prove the MCP tool transport preserves the error shape.

FILES:
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/group_policy_cli.py — primary, deterministic.
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/pi_mcp_group_policy.py — optional second variant via Pi MCP.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Scenario verifies the full error chain end-to-end, not just the daemon-side rejection.
- Scenario fails if the surfaces ever swallow, downgrade, or rephrase the rejection.

RELATED UNIT TEST:
tests/api.test.ts asserts daemon-level rejection via direct REST. Integration adds the surface-translation layer (REST → MCP/CLI → agent transcript).

PARENT: sync-6p4

title: Integration: assert bridge_join_group default-alias path (B6)
description:
Every join in scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/group_policy_cli.py currently passes an explicit --alias flag. The MCP tool surface and CLI both promise that omitting alias defaults to the registered session_name (handled in daemon's POST /groups/:name/join: 'const alias = optionalString(body, \"alias\") ?? peer.session_name'). Only an end-to-end scenario can prove the default propagates through bridge_join_group / synchronize group join.

REPRO SKETCH:
1. Register a peer with session_name = \"alice\" (already happens during harness setup).
2. Call bridge_join_group (Pi) or synchronize group join without an alias.
3. Read back via GET /groups/:name/members.
4. Assert alias == \"alice\".

A second variant should pass alias explicitly (already in the existing scenario), so a comparative assertion is straightforward.

FILES:
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/group_policy_cli.py — primary home, deterministic and fast.
- The existing assert_active_alias helper (group_policy_cli.py) can be reused.
- Optional: add to scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/scenarios/pi_mcp_group_policy.py too if you want surface coverage via real Pi MCP.

ACCEPTANCE:
- Scenario fails if a future regression silently writes alias = NULL, an empty string, or a different default.
- Scenario passes against the daemon at integration tip (b7f86cf or later).

RELATED UNIT TEST:
None. Existing tests/api.test.ts unit tests assert the daemon-side default, but not the CLI/MCP forwarding path. This is the integration value.

PARENT: sync-6p4

title: [epic] Integration harness coverage gaps for group policy v0
description:
The integration harness at `scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/` (added on branch `group-policy-v0-additional-testing`, commits 02ee16d + 194373f) gives high-confidence end-to-end coverage of the agent-visible group-policy v0 behaviors enumerated in `docs/group-sync-integrity.md`. An independent audit (2026-05-23) mapped each scenario assertion against the 27-item invariant checklist:

WELL COVERED (14 items, PASS at integration level):
- A1, A2 identity registration + bridge_whoami
- B1, B2 group create/describe with description
- B5 explicit-alias join
- B8 alias freed on leave
- B9 reclaim audit event (with peer_id + previous_peer_id assertions)
- B10 self-scoped rename
- C1, C2, C3 thread send + reply normalization (reply-to-reply collapses to root)
- C5, C6 history filter (default excludes replies; thread_of returns root + replies in order)
- D1, D2 mention resolution + warnings for unresolved tokens
- D5 sender does not get push for own message
- D6 (partial) roster events land in members' inboxes
- E2 host_session_id surfacing on group roster

GAPS WORTH FILLING AT INTEGRATION LEVEL (5 items):
Five v0 behaviors are agent-visible across the CLI/MCP surface in a way only an end-to-end test can prove. Child issues track each:
- B6 alias defaults to session_name when omitted
- B7 alias-collision (409) reaches agent as readable error
- B11 fresh=true history boundary for late joiners
- D4 thread reply push fanout direct assertion (root_author ∪ thread_posters ∪ new mentions)
- D7 stale @-mention of a since-left member must not push

BRITTLE ASSERTIONS TO TIGHTEN (3 items):
Three existing assertions could silently break or false-positive. Child issues track each:
- forbidden-tool detection by JSON string search in pi_mcp_thread_baton.py
- loop guard depends on run_id echoing in message bodies
- warning-shape assertion in group_policy_cli.py bypasses the MCP layer

GAPS DELIBERATELY NOT FILED (covered by tests/api.test.ts):
These are pure daemon-level invariants. Duplicating them in tmux/Pi adds runtime cost without finding new bugs — the unit suite already gates regressions at the storage and route layer.
- A3 CLI peer reuse (existing resolveCliRegisterPeerId + findReusablePeer logic)
- B3 case-insensitive name collision
- B4 ephemeral group + media_dir purge on daemon restart
- C4 in_reply_to targeting an event in a different group
- C7 thread_of pointing at a non-root reply
- C8 thread_of pointing at a non-existent event_id
- E1 events.type CHECK constraint

NOT A V0 BLOCKER:
The harness as it stands is sufficient for the v0 bundle merge to master. This epic gathers improvements; it does not gate shipping. Run only when prioritized.

REFERENCE:
- docs/group-sync-integrity.md — checklist of v0 invariants and the routing matrix
- session-tracker/plan-group-policy-v0.md — design plan
- scripts/README.md — harness usage notes
- .claude/handoffs/2026-05-23-group-policy-v0-phases-1-2-dx2.md — decision log + audit context
- sync-l91 (closed) — the group-policy v0 epic this audits

SUCCESSOR / RELATED:
- sync-aeb (P3, deferred) — ACL/blocks, also not in the harness, but explicitly out of v0 scope.
- sync-jix — DaemonDataSource, gates any web-UI-level integration tests.

title: Add Pi integration harness tool allow/deny policy enforcement
description: Design and implement a policy layer for real Pi integration harness runs that can declare which MCP tools an agent is allowed to use for each prompt/phase and which tools or surfaces should fail the run if used. The immediate motivation is the thread-baton scenario: agents should prove mention and thread push semantics by acting from injected synchronize_event envelopes, not by discovering state through history reads. Today the harness can inspect transcripts after the fact, but it cannot systematically enforce a per-phase tool policy or explain violations cleanly.
acceptance_criteria: Harness supports per-agent/per-phase allowed tool declarations for Pi prompts; violations are detected deterministically from Pi transcripts or structured logs; failures include the agent, phase, forbidden tool, and relevant transcript excerpt; policy can distinguish setup tools from workflow tools; docs explain when to use policy enforcement versus prompt wording; at least one real Pi scenario uses the policy to prevent history/discovery reads during event-driven baton replies.

title: Improve Pi tmux capture parsing with verbose tool logs
description:
What/why/how:
Pi tmux pane captures currently hide long MCP tool calls and tool results behind compact UI sections such as the Ctrl+O expand directive. The most important integration-test evidence lives inside MCP JSON calls/results and injected <synchronize_event> envelopes, but the pane capture is hard to parse deterministically in this compact mode. Investigate launching Pi with verbose output for integration tests, then add a parser that extracts structured MCP call/result records and synchronize event sections from each Pi agent log.

Impact area:
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/pi_env.py Pi launch command, likely adding --verbose for integration runs.
- scripts/integration-aoe/sync_itest_aoe/tmux.py or a new diagnostics parser module.
- scripts/README.md and docs/integration-tmux.md diagnostics guidance.

Acceptance criteria:
- Integration Pi launch can run in verbose mode so pane captures include full MCP tool calls/results instead of collapsed Ctrl+O sections.
- A deterministic parser extracts MCP call names, JSON arguments, JSON results, and injected synchronize_event envelopes from captured panes or transcripts.
- Failure artifacts include a structured parsed log per Pi agent in addition to raw pane capture.
- Existing real Pi DM and group-policy smoke tests continue to pass.

How to verify:
- Run a real Pi integration smoke and confirm artifacts include full parsed MCP entries for bridge_whoami/list/send/history calls.
- Confirm captures no longer require manual Ctrl+O expansion for the primary evidence.

Notes:
Keep this as diagnostics-only work; it should not change product behavior or the semantic assertions of the integration scenarios.

title: Radix epic final verification: visual + a11y + Vim nav regression check
description: After R1+R2+R3 land: run the demo seed, screenshot the three migrated surfaces (context menu open, toast stack, mention popup) in both light and dark themes. Compare to pre-Radix baseline — should be visually identical. Run keyboard-only walkthrough: open each widget, navigate, close, confirm focus returns correctly. Run useVimNav walkthrough: confirm H/L/J/K/Tab/i/Esc still drive the app correctly when no Radix widget is open. Run bun test + bun run typecheck. Total LOC delta vs pre-Radix master noted in bead notes.
acceptance_criteria: (1) Side-by-side screenshots show zero visual change in both themes. (2) Keyboard walkthrough notes attached: each widget passes Esc-closes + focus-returns. (3) Vim nav unbroken. (4) bun test green; bun run typecheck green. (5) Net LOC delta documented (expect roughly -150 across the three widgets + small + for Radix imports). (6) Epic ready to close.
close_reason: Re-filed under sync-99s as the cross-cutting Radix verification bead.

title: Migrate Composer @mention popup to Radix Popover + Combobox pattern
description: Refactor the @mention block inside web/src/components/Composer.tsx (~80 of 210 LOC) to use @radix-ui/react-popover for positioning + a hand-rolled or Radix-collection-based Combobox list for arrow-key / Tab / Enter handling. Keep the existing .mention-pop / .mention-row / .mention-av / .mention-meta / .mention-note className skin. The mention textbook trigger (typed '@', filtered list) stays as-is; only the popup mechanics + keyboard handling change. Verify the live status-note line still renders inline per DESIGN.md.
acceptance_criteria: (1) Mention popup uses Radix Popover + Combobox primitives. (2) Caret-based positioning behavior preserved (or improved). (3) Visual: zero change. (4) Keyboard: arrows navigate, Tab/Enter commits, Esc cancels without breaking the textarea focus. (5) Click-outside closes correctly. (6) LOC delta noted (expect ~-50).
close_reason: Duplicate of sync-kmt (which already exists for Composer @mention Radix Popover+cmdk migration). Decision context recorded in sync-99s notes.

title: Migrate Toast.tsx to @radix-ui/react-toast
description: Replace the bespoke timer / stack / lifecycle code in web/src/components/Toast.tsx (79 LOC) with @radix-ui/react-toast. Keep the existing .toast-stack / .toast / .toast-* className skin. Wire ARIA-live announcements (Radix handles this by default — verify by inspecting rendered DOM). Confirm hover-to-pause behavior on auto-dismiss.
acceptance_criteria: (1) Toast.tsx uses Radix primitives. (2) Existing useToast() API surface unchanged (no consumer changes). (3) Visual: zero change. (4) ARIA-live announcement verified in rendered DOM. (5) Hover-to-pause works. (6) LOC delta noted (expect ~-50).
close_reason: Re-filed under the existing sync-99s epic as a sibling of sync-ea8/sync-kmt.

title: Migrate ContextMenu.tsx to @radix-ui/react-context-menu
description: Replace the bespoke portal + outside-click + position math in web/src/components/ContextMenu.tsx (88 LOC) with @radix-ui/react-context-menu primitives, keeping the existing .ctx-menu / .ctx-item / .ctx-divider / .ctx-shortcut / .ctx-danger className skin layer intact. Verify Radix portals respect [data-theme=...] cascading — render a context menu under both light and dark themes and confirm tokens still apply. This is the PROOF POINT for the Radix approach; if the visual / theme cascade / Vim-nav-compatibility checks all pass, R2/R3 follow. If any of them fail, this issue documents the blocker and the rest of the epic is reconsidered.
acceptance_criteria: (1) ContextMenu.tsx uses Radix primitives; bespoke focus/outside-click/portal code removed. (2) All 4 consumer surfaces (sidebar, message, roster, timeline — per DESIGN.md) still work. (3) Visual: zero change vs pre-Radix baseline. (4) Theme cascade: open menu under light, swap to dark — menu re-skins. (5) Keyboard: Esc closes + returns focus; arrows navigate items; Enter activates; Tab does not break out. (6) useVimNav still works when the menu is closed. (7) LOC delta noted in bead notes (expect ~-40).
close_reason: Duplicate of sync-ea8 (which already exists with a detailed approach for ContextMenu Radix migration). Decision context recorded in sync-99s notes.

title: Web UI: adopt Radix primitives for accessibility-sensitive widgets
description: Replace bespoke focus / keyboard / portal / ARIA plumbing in ContextMenu, Toast, and the Composer @mention popup with Radix UI primitives. Behavior gets correct-by-default (focus return, roving focus, ARIA-live, Esc/click-outside semantics); visual aesthetic is unchanged (Radix is headless — we keep our existing CSS classes as the skin layer). LOC delta projected by audit: ~150 LOC removed from the three components, +3 small Radix deps. THIS EPIC IS DEFERRED behind sync-75p (tokenization). Rationale: the value of Radix-as-behavior-only is amplified when the styling layer is already cleanly tokenized — re-skinning Radix widgets per future theme is then a pure CSS exercise. Doing Radix first risks tangling visual + behavioral cleanup in the same diffs.
design: Approach: Radix headless primitives, NOT shadcn/ui. shadcn ships with Tailwind + a specific visual aesthetic; we want only the behavior layer. Deps to add: @radix-ui/react-context-menu, @radix-ui/react-toast, @radix-ui/react-popover (and possibly @radix-ui/react-collection for the mention list, or hand-roll a list with arrow-key handling). Each Radix component is rendered with our existing className values intact, so the CSS sweep done in sync-75p continues to drive the look. Each widget gets its own bead so they can land independently; ContextMenu first because it's the smallest and most contained (88 LOC -> ~40 LOC projected). Risk: theme attribute interaction with Radix portals — Radix portals render to document.body, so :root[data-theme=...] must continue to cascade. Confirm during ContextMenu prototype before expanding.
acceptance_criteria: (1) ContextMenu, Toast, and Composer @mention popup all use Radix primitives. (2) Visual diff vs pre-Radix baseline = zero (same skin, same CSS). (3) Keyboard semantics improved: Esc closes + returns focus; Tab/Shift-Tab navigates without breaking out; arrow keys work in menus and mention list; screen reader announces toasts. (4) Vim nav (useVimNav) continues to function — Radix widgets do not capture keys outside their open state. (5) bun test + bun run typecheck green. (6) LOC delta documented; expected ~-150 net.
close_reason: Duplicate Radix execution epic; sync-99s is the canonical epic. All children re-filed or closed-as-dup. See sync-99s decision note for context.

title: Document group-policy integration workflow commands and diagnostics
description:
What/why/how:
Update integration harness documentation after the group-policy scenarios exist. Future agents should be able to understand which scenario to run, when to choose fake-shell vs real Pi, how to use --keep, where artifacts live, and what prompt discipline applies to self-aware MCP flows.

Impact area:
- scripts/README.md: future-agent guidance for adding more AoE-backed synchronize workflow tests.
- docs/integration-tmux.md: user-facing commands, flags, diagnostics, cleanup, and manual inspection notes.
- Scenario help text if it needs clarification.

Acceptance criteria:
- Documentation lists deterministic group-policy CLI command(s) and real Pi group-policy command(s).
- Documentation explains that deterministic fake-shell tests are the default stable regression layer and real Pi tests are manual/local nondeterministic smoke tests.
- Documentation repeats the Pi tmux submission caveat: use named Enter, not C-m.
- Documentation explains that Pi prompts should rely on bridge_whoami and bridge_list_peers rather than being spoon-fed peer IDs.
- Cleanup and --keep inspection commands are included.

How to verify:
- Read the docs from a fresh-agent perspective and confirm there is enough context to run the scenarios.
- Run each new wrapper with --help and ensure flag names match the docs.

Test plan:
- Documentation review plus command --help validation.

Dependencies:
- Test CLI group identity alias and description workflow
- Test CLI group threads mentions and inbox routing
- Test real Pi MCP group join mention and thread workflow

Improvements:
- Add a troubleshooting table after the first few real Pi group-policy runs expose common failures.
close_reason: Documented group-policy AoE/tmux and real Pi workflow commands, diagnostics, --keep inspection, prompt discipline, named Enter caveat, and lazy MCP readiness behavior.

title: Modularize integration harness support code
description:
## Goal
Refactor the integration harness code so the suite can grow beyond the current shell smoke and real Pi DM smoke without accumulating large copy-pasted scripts. The current shape works functionally, but `scripts/integration_tmux.py` and `scripts/integration_pi.py` both carry overlapping responsibilities: run metadata, command execution/logging, preflight checks, AoE profile/session lifecycle, tmux pane discovery/capture, REST diagnostics, cleanup, and scenario orchestration. Future workflows such as group join/send/history, inbound Pi push, media share, blocked-agent detection, and multi-agent workflows should be able to reuse a small set of primitives rather than duplicating full harness classes.

## High-level refactor direction
Create a small Python integration support package under `scripts/integration/` or similar, with thin executable entrypoints for each scenario. Keep the user-facing commands stable (`uv run scripts/integration_tmux.py`, `uv run scripts/integration_pi.py`) by turning those files into wrappers or by preserving equivalent scripts that import the shared modules. The architecture should separate reusable infrastructure from workflow scenarios.

## Proposed module boundaries
- `runtime.py`: run ids, repo discovery, state/log directory layout, JSON/text log writing, command execution with stdout/stderr capture, common `HarnessError`, cleanup helpers, and environment construction.
- `aoe.py`: AoE profile creation/deletion, session add/start/remove, AoE list/status collection, profile naming helpers, and current-run AoE session id tracking.
- `tmux.py`: libtmux availability checks, pane discovery, pane mapping by AoE session id prefix, pane capture, literal prompt/command sending, and the Pi-specific named `Enter` submission helper. This module should encode the learned rule that Pi TUI prompt submission needs `tmux send-keys ... Enter`, not `C-m`.
- `sync_rest.py`: daemon startup through `bun run src/cli.ts status`, discovery loading, REST JSON helpers, `/peers`, `/events`, `/agent-sessions`, and diagnostic snapshots.
- `pi_env.py`: isolated Pi home provisioning, copied `auth.json`, minimal `settings.json`, worktree-local `mcp.json`, worktree extension/skill path validation, Pi command construction, and session/transcript discovery. It must preserve the invariant that tests never mutate real `~/.pi/agent`.
- `scenarios/cli_dm.py`: deterministic shell/CLI DM smoke currently in `integration_tmux.py`.
- `scenarios/pi_mcp_dm.py`: real Pi MCP DM workflow currently in `integration_pi.py`, including auto-registration, MCP-ready selection, `bridge_whoami` + `bridge_list_peers` + `bridge_dm` prompt, REST event assertion, and transcript evidence.

## Low-level cleanup points
- Deduplicate `AgentPane`, `HarnessError`, `command`, `write_text`, `write_json`, `http_json`, `capture_all_panes`, `aoe_list`, `collect_aoe_state`, and daemon cleanup logic.
- Remove broad monolithic harness classes where possible; each scenario should read as setup -> action -> assertions -> diagnostics.
- Make current-run AoE/tmux mapping robust by always preferring AoE session id prefixes over title-based matching, so old kept sessions do not contaminate new runs.
- Keep CLI shell command submission and Pi TUI prompt submission as separate primitives. Shell commands may use command markers; Pi prompts must use literal text plus named `Enter`.
- Keep identity discipline explicit: harness REST state may use peer ids for assertions, but prompts to real agents should stay high-level unless a scenario specifically tests low-level identity handling. Normal Pi prompts should make the agent discover itself with `bridge_whoami` and discover peers/groups with MCP tools.
- Keep generated runtime state under ignored `.synchronize-itest/` by default, with per-run subdirectories.
- Ensure `--keep` behavior remains consistent: preserve AoE/tmux/Pi/synchronize runtime state for inspection; default cleanup removes live sessions and daemon state while keeping useful logs where appropriate.
- Preserve manual/local nature of real Pi tests; they should remain outside `bun test` and CI.

## Compatibility requirements
- Existing command lines should continue to work, especially:
  - `uv run scripts/integration_tmux.py`
  - `uv run scripts/integration_tmux.py --keep`
  - `uv run scripts/integration_pi.py`
  - `uv run scripts/integration_pi.py --keep`
- Keep inline `uv` script dependency handling for executable wrappers, or document the replacement if a package layout requires it.
- Do not change the behavior or scope of the already-verified shell CLI smoke and real Pi MCP DM smoke during the refactor except to preserve behavior through shared modules.

## Verification
- `python3 -m py_compile` over all integration Python modules and wrappers.
- `uv run scripts/integration_tmux.py --help` and `uv run scripts/integration_pi.py --help`.
- Full deterministic shell smoke: `uv run scripts/integration_tmux.py`.
- Real Pi smoke, manual/local: `uv run scripts/integration_pi.py`, plus one `--keep` run during development to inspect AoE/tmux state.
- Project checks: `bun run typecheck` and `bun test`.

## Non-goals
- Do not add CI execution for real Pi workflows.
- Do not broaden scenarios to groups/media/inbound push as part of this refactor. The point is to create the structure that makes those future scenarios straightforward.
- Do not mutate global Pi config or rely on production `~/.pi/agent` beyond copying `auth.json` into the isolated run directory.
design: The refactor should make integration infrastructure reusable before adding more workflows. Prefer small Python modules with explicit responsibilities over a large inheritance hierarchy. The wrappers should stay boring and stable; scenario modules should contain workflow-specific prompts and assertions; shared modules should contain all AoE/tmux/Pi/synchronize runtime mechanics. This preserves the current successful behavior while making future Beads issues for complex workflows independently implementable.
acceptance_criteria:
- Detailed module split exists for reusable integration runtime, AoE, tmux, REST, Pi environment, and scenarios.
- Current shell CLI smoke and real Pi MCP DM smoke still pass with the same public commands.
- Real Pi prompt remains high-level and does not pass recipient peer ids for normal discovery workflows.
- The Pi named-Enter tmux caveat is encoded in a shared helper and documented.
- Future scenarios can be added without duplicating AoE/tmux/Pi provisioning code.
close_reason: Implemented scripts/integration-aoe reusable AoE/tmux integration support modules, thin stable wrappers, CLI DM and Pi MCP DM scenarios, docs, and verification: py_compile, wrapper --help, deterministic tmux smoke, real Pi smoke, bun typecheck, bun test.

title: Document real Pi AoE harness usage
description: Document the manual real Pi harness, including prerequisites, isolated state layout, --keep behavior, manual inspection commands, and caveats around real model/OAuth behavior.
close_reason: Documented real Pi harness usage and workflow-extension notes

title: Validate Pi auto-registration and MCP DM smoke
description: Add the first real Pi scenario: verify extension-driven Pi peer and agent-session registration, prompt one Pi agent to use synchronize MCP tools to send a DM to another Pi peer, and assert REST state plus Pi transcript evidence.
close_reason: Implemented and verified real Pi MCP DM smoke with REST and transcript evidence

title: Launch real Pi sessions through AoE tmux harness
description: Extend the integration harness so it can create an isolated AoE profile, launch real interactive Pi sessions, map them to tmux panes, and capture diagnostics using the same cockpit/tmux approach as the fake-shell harness.
close_reason: Implemented AoE/tmux launch and mapping for real Pi sessions

title: Provision isolated per-worktree Pi test environment
description: Create the worktree-local Pi harness state layout and provision a minimal temporary Pi home from the current worktree. The setup should copy only auth.json from the user's real Pi config, write minimal settings and MCP config, and load the worktree's Pi extension and skill without mutating ~/.pi/agent.
close_reason: Implemented isolated worktree-local Pi harness state and config provisioning

title: Daemon-side identity enforcement for self-scoped mutations
description: The daemon currently trusts peer_id from request bodies for self-scoped operations (group rename, group create's creator_peer_id, etc). Adapter layers enforce that callers only pass their own peer_id, but a direct REST caller could mutate a different peer's row. v0 explicitly accepts this trust model (local-single-machine, hostile-direct-REST is out of scope). File this as a follow-up so we don't lose the design choice. Options for v1 if/when needed: (a) require a bearer/peer-scoped token signed by the daemon on register; (b) move identity guardrails into the daemon, breaking the current 'daemon trusts callers' invariant; (c) hash-chain commit log. Pick one when there's a concrete threat model.
acceptance_criteria: Decision recorded (memo or ADR) about whether to add daemon-side identity enforcement for self-scoped writes; if yes, implement; if no, document the trust boundary explicitly in CLAUDE.md / docs/adr/. Either way close with a written rationale.

title: Add real Pi-agent AoE integration smoke
description:
What is the goal of the epic?
Add a later, optional real-agent integration smoke that launches real Pi coding-agent sessions through AoE and uses the same synchronize identity/messaging/workflow concepts as the fake shell harness. This epic exists to track the future production-like path after the deterministic shell harness is working.

What are the key features/components/modules/systems/files/logical-components affected by the epic?
Future work will extend the AoE/tmux harness with a real Pi launch mode, Pi setup/auth preflight, Pi-specific synchronization registration, and scenarios for DMs, groups, threads, mentions, and ACL once those features stabilize. No implementation details are specified yet; this epic is intentionally a placeholder for the real-agent phase.

How to verify / test the implementation of the epic?
Future verification should prove that real Pi sessions launched through AoE can register with synchronize, communicate through synchronize, and remain inspectable through AoE/tmux. Exact scenarios should be planned after the fake-shell harness and group-policy v0 work are stable.
design: This epic is intentionally deferred. It should not block the fake-shell scaffold. It exists to keep the roadmap visible without forcing premature choices about Pi auth, command shape, or scenario coverage.
acceptance_criteria:
- Epic captures the future real Pi-agent integration objective separately from the fake-shell harness.
- No child implementation tasks are required until the first harness version is complete.
- Future work should reuse the harness primitives from the AoE/tmux fake-shell epic where possible.
close_reason: Real Pi AoE/tmux MCP DM harness implemented and verified

title: Group policy phase 3d: group description metadata
description: Add a nullable groups.description TEXT column, settable at create and mutable via the privileged CLI (synchronize group describe <group> <text>). Surfaces in listGroups responses and the web chat UI roster/header so agents can discover group purpose without out-of-band coordination. Trivially small phase -- intentionally separate so it can land in any order with the other phase 3 work.
design: See session-tracker/plan-group-policy-v0.md "Group metadata" section. Schema: ALTER TABLE groups ADD COLUMN description. API: POST /groups accepts optional description; PATCH /groups/:name accepts { description? } (CLI-only consumer). Touchpoints: src/db.ts, src/api/groups.ts, src/api/types.ts (Group type), src/daemon.ts, src/cli/commands/group.ts, web/src/components/RoomHeader.tsx or Sidebar.
acceptance_criteria: POST /groups with description persists and returns it; listGroups includes description in Group rows; PATCH /groups/:name with { description } updates; CLI command sets description; web UI displays it where the design calls for a topic line.
close_reason: Phase 3d shipped: groups.description schema + POST/PATCH + describe CLI + RoomHeader topic line. Tests 33 pass / 0 fail.

title: Group policy phase 3c: ACL / blocks (CLI-only admin)
description: Add per-peer / per-host-session access control to groups via a new group_acl table. v0 stores blocked status only (allowed is reserved for v1 invite-list flows). Key types: peer_id and host_session_id -- the latter lets admins block a specific Claude/Pi session even after it respawns under a new peer_id. Block effect: group remains visible in listGroups (no listing filter), but join/send/share_media/history/rename_in_group return 403 group_forbidden. All ACL writes go through privileged CLI on the daemon host -- no MCP tools touch ACL. New CLI commands: synchronize group block/allow/acl. Defaults remain wide open (no rows -> no blocks).
design: See session-tracker/plan-group-policy-v0.md sections "Access control" and "ACL enforcement chokepoint." Schema: new group_acl table with PK (group_id, key_type, key_value) and idx_group_acl_key (key_type, key_value). API: GET/POST /groups/:name/acl, DELETE /groups/:name/acl/:key_type/:key_value. Daemon-side ensureNotBlocked(group_id, peer_id) chokepoint called from all group ops. CLI: synchronize group block/allow/acl/describe under src/cli/commands/group.ts.
acceptance_criteria: Blocked peer (by peer_id) sees the group in listGroups but gets 403 on join/send/history/rename_in_group; blocked host_session_id key blocks every peer with that binding; CLI commands round-trip create/list/delete entries; ensureNotBlocked never permits an op when an ACL row matches by either key type.
notes: Deferred from v0 as a deliberate scoping decision (2026-05-23). Rationale: no adversary on a single-machine single-user tool; daemon-side identity enforcement (sync-cg8) is also deferred, so policing group access without policing identity is incoherent; plan already deferred everything around 'blocked' (role, visibility, allowed, per-thread ACL) to v1. Design intact in session-tracker/plan-group-policy-v0.md. Reconsider when an actual use case appears: untrusted/autonomous agents, multi-user daemon, or sensitive groups other agents shouldn't peek into.

title: Baseline: a11y + bundle-size measurements before migration
description:
## Why
We can't claim "shadcn / Radix gave us accessibility wins" without measuring before and after. Same for bundle size — Radix primitives are 5-15 kB gzipped each; we should know the delta.

## Acceptance criteria

### Accessibility baseline (today)
- [ ] Run axe-core (or Lighthouse a11y audit) against the current preview at http://127.0.0.1:47823/web/ in both light and dark mode.
- [ ] Manually test keyboard nav on each of the four context-menu surfaces (Sidebar room item, MessageRow, AgentRoster card, future TimelineRail node). Document which key events fail.
- [ ] Manually test mention popup with keyboard only — can a screen-reader user open, navigate, select? Document failures.
- [ ] Screen-reader smoke test with VoiceOver: are messages announced? Author? Mentions? Threads? Document gaps.
- [ ] Save findings to docs/a11y-baseline.md.

### Bundle baseline (today)
- [ ] Record current web/dist/main.*.js gzipped size: gzip -c web/dist/main.*.js | wc -c.
- [ ] Record current web/dist/main.*.css gzipped size.
- [ ] Note React + react-markdown + remark-gfm + rehype-* are already in there.

### Post-migration targets
- [ ] A la carte path: expect +30 kB gz (4 Radix packages + cmdk). Anything more deserves scrutiny.
- [ ] Full migration: expect +80-120 kB gz before Tailwind purge; purged Tailwind CSS should be smaller than our ~2,900-line styles.css. Net could be neutral.

### Re-measure after each child lands
- [ ] sync-ea8 lands -> re-measure, compare.
- [ ] sync-kmt lands -> re-measure.
- [ ] sync-30s lands -> re-measure (Tailwind output replaces parts of styles.css).

## Complex pieces
- Tooling: axe-core can run headlessly via Playwright; we don't have Playwright wired. Cheapest route: Lighthouse via Chrome DevTools manually.
- gzip vs brotli: brotli is closer to production but gzip is the standard comparison metric.

## Estimate
3-4 h for the initial audit; ~30 min recurring after each child task lands.

title: Migration matrix: which components swap, which stay custom
description:
## Why
Before any execution, agree on the per-component verdict. Some components have direct shadcn equivalents (and would benefit). Others are domain-specific (Timeline, Poll, MessageRow, ThreadPane) and have no shadcn equivalent — migration touches only their **inner primitives** (Button, Avatar, Tooltip), not their structure.

This task is a **planning artifact**, not implementation. Output is a table + a recommendation per component.

## Components to classify

| Current component | Action | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| \`Sidebar\` | KEEP — restyle only | Layout is bespoke (brand + search + GROUPS + DMs + footer). Inner \`Button\` for + actions could swap. |
| \`RoomHeader\` | KEEP — Tabs swap to shadcn | Outer layout custom; tab strip → shadcn \`Tabs\`. |
| \`ChatView\` | KEEP | Domain-specific message list + composer wrapper. |
| \`MessageRow\` | KEEP | Bubble, author chip, mentions, thread badge, poll, reactions — all custom. |
| \`Composer\` | KEEP — popover swaps | Replace mention popup with Radix Popover + cmdk (sync-kmt). |
| \`PollWidget\` | KEEP | No shadcn equivalent. |
| \`ThreadPane\` | KEEP | Custom layout (header + parent + divider + replies + composer). |
| \`TimelineRail\` | KEEP — tooltip swaps | Vertical rail is custom; per-node tooltip → Radix Tooltip. |
| \`AgentRoster\` | KEEP | Custom grouped list with status sections. |
| \`ContextMenu\` | REPLACE | → Radix \`ContextMenu\` (sync-ea8). |
| \`ScrollControls\` | KEEP | Tiny custom thing; not worth the shadcn-Button swap. |
| \`Avatar\` (primitives) | OPTIONAL swap | shadcn \`Avatar\` exists but we don't use the fallback-image flow. Square 5px radius + identity color is custom — keep our impl. |
| \`MentionChip\` (primitives) | KEEP | Custom badge. |
| \`StatusDot\` (primitives) | KEEP | One \`<span>\`, no benefit to abstracting. |
| \`Sticker\` (primitives) | KEEP | No shadcn equivalent. |
| \`Markdown\` | KEEP | Wraps react-markdown — orthogonal to shadcn. |

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Table finalized and committed to \`web/DESIGN.md\` as an appendix.
- [ ] Each "REPLACE" entry has a child issue (sync-ea8, sync-kmt, etc.).
- [ ] Each "KEEP — X swaps" entry has the inner-primitive swap scoped.
- [ ] Discussion of bundle delta: roughly which Radix packages we'd pull in (\`react-context-menu\`, \`react-popover\`, \`react-tooltip\`, \`react-tabs\` — 4 packages, ~30 kB gzipped) for à la carte; more for full migration.

## Output
A short ADR or DESIGN.md section, not code.

## Estimate
2 h.

title: Integrate Tailwind CSS into the Bun.build pipeline
description:
## Why
Tailwind is a precondition for any shadcn adoption. Bun.build (\`web/build.ts\`) is currently a single \`Bun.build({...})\` call with no PostCSS / asset preprocessing step. Tailwind needs either PostCSS or its own v4 CLI run before bundling.

## Approach options
1. **Tailwind v4 CLI sidecar.** Run \`tailwindcss -i web/src/tailwind.css -o web/dist/tw.css\` as a step in \`build.ts\` before/alongside \`Bun.build\`. Simpler — no PostCSS plugin chain. Tailwind v4 (released 2024) is the first version that does this cleanly.
2. **PostCSS wrapper around Bun.build.** Heavier — requires postcss-loader-equivalent for Bun, which is not first-class today.

**Recommendation:** option 1.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] \`bun web/build.ts\` produces a Tailwind-built CSS file alongside the existing bundle.
- [ ] Existing \`styles.css\` + \`extra.css\` still load (we ship both during the transition).
- [ ] Tailwind config (\`tailwind.config.ts\` or v4 \`@theme\` block) mirrors \`DESIGN.md\` tokens: colors (paper / paper-2 / paper-3 / ink / ink-soft / ink-faint / yellow / pink / blue / lime / tangerine / lilac / teal / red / muted), the 0/3/5/8/999 radius ramp, the shadow scale (\`shadow-sm: 2px 2px 0 var(--rule)\`, etc.).
- [ ] Dark theme works via Tailwind's \`dark:\` variant + \`data-theme="dark"\` selector.
- [ ] Build time stays under ~2 seconds.
- [ ] No visual regression — light + dark screenshots match the current preview.

## Complex pieces
- **Bun.build doesn't watch CSS by default.** Need to wire Tailwind's \`--watch\` into our dev loop.
- **CSS variable interop.** DESIGN.md tokens are already CSS vars; Tailwind config should reference them (\`backgroundColor: { paper: 'var(--paper)' }\`) so we don't fork the source of truth.
- **Bundle delivery.** The current \`<link rel="stylesheet">\` is rewritten by \`build.ts\` to an absolute \`/web/\` path. Tailwind output needs the same treatment.

## Estimate
2–4 h.
notes:
WONTFIX — 2026-05-23

This task is not being pursued. Rationale, from the 2026-05-23 web UI design discussion (see sync-99s decision note for the full context):

The user's goal is a codebase flexible enough to support multiple aesthetics later (neo-brutalist today; vanilla-Slack-style minimal as a possible future theme), togglable from one button. Two paths were considered:

  A. À la carte Radix adoption — replace only accessibility-critical homegrown widgets (ContextMenu, Toast, mention popup) with Radix headless primitives. Keep the existing custom-CSS + CSS-custom-property token system. Multi-theme support comes from completing the tokenization work in sync-75p.

  B. Full shadcn migration — add Tailwind, port ~93 KB of custom CSS to Tailwind utility classes, re-theme every shadcn primitive to brutalist.

Audit findings (2026-05-23) showed the codebase is already ~70% of the way to token-driven multi-theme support: components use semantic class names (182 className= sites, all semantic), aesthetic colors are not hardcoded in components, raw visual literals are concentrated in styles.css/extra.css where they are mechanically swappable to tokens. The Tailwind precondition this bead implements adds significant migration cost (months of CSS rewrite) for a benefit (theme tokens) that the existing system delivers more cheaply via sync-75p.

Decision: pursue Path A. This bead (Tailwind integration into Bun.build) becomes unnecessary. Closing as wontfix to declutter `bd ready`. If full shadcn is ever reconsidered, this bead's approach notes (Tailwind v4 CLI sidecar vs PostCSS wrapper) remain valid and can be referenced from the closed-issue history.
close_reason: Not pursuing per 2026-05-23 design decision recorded in sync-99s. Chose à la carte Radix (no Tailwind, no full shadcn migration). Rationale captured in each bead's WONTFIX note.

title: Evaluate/adopt shadcn for web chat UI
description:
## Goal
Decide between two paths and execute the chosen one:
(A) **À la carte Radix adoption** — replace only the accessibility-critical homegrown components (ContextMenu, mention popover, future ⌘K palette) with headless Radix primitives. Keep the rest of the CSS / visual system intact. ~6-10h.
(B) **Full shadcn migration** — add Tailwind + shadcn CLI, port the ~2,900-line CSS to Tailwind tokens + utility classes, re-theme every shadcn primitive to brutalist, replace homegrown primitives with shadcn equivalents where they exist. ~4-7 focused days.

## Current state (as of this issue)
- React 19 + TypeScript + Bun.build pipeline (no PostCSS / no Vite).
- Plain CSS — \`web/src/styles.css\` (~2,500 lines) + \`web/src/components/extra.css\` (~900 lines).
- Custom primitives: \`Avatar\`, \`Sticker\`, \`StatusDot\`, \`MentionChip\`, \`CountChip\`.
- Custom interactive components with no a11y baseline: \`ContextMenu\`, mention popup in \`Composer\`, \`ScrollControls\`, \`TimelineRail\` tooltip.
- Brutalist visual system formalized in \`web/DESIGN.md\` (tokens for paper/ink/accents, 0/3/5/8/999 radius ramp, \`--shadow\` / \`--shadow-sm\` / \`--shadow-lg\` offset scale, agent identity colors).
- DataSource abstraction (\`MockDataSource\` + future \`DaemonDataSource\`) — **not affected by either path**.

## Why this matters
1. **Accessibility debt**. Our \`ContextMenu\` has no keyboard nav, no focus trap, no \`aria-haspopup\` wiring. Same for the mention popover. Radix gives all of that for free.
2. **Future surfaces become cheap**. ⌘K palette, Toast, Dialog, Drawer, Sheet — these are easier once Radix (or shadcn) is in.
3. **Tailwind-vs-custom-CSS** is a separate axis. Tailwind unlocks shadcn's \`cva\` variant API and \`dark:\` variant, but means migrating ~2,900 lines of CSS.

## Decision points (need answers before executing)
- [ ] Accept Tailwind in the build pipeline? (Bun.build has no PostCSS — would run Tailwind v4 CLI as a sidecar in \`build.ts\`.)
- [ ] Keep the brutalist identity exactly as it is, or accept some drift toward shadcn's defaults? (shadcn.io has a "Neo Brutalism" preset that gets ~70% of the way.)
- [ ] Bundle size budget — Radix primitives are 5-15 kB gzip each; ~8-10 of them is +80-100 kB. Tailwind purged is small. Acceptable?
- [ ] Timeline pressure — accessibility wins are urgent, full migration is not.

## Recommended path
**Adopt à la carte (sync-EPIC.A) first**, ship the accessibility wins in a week. Then decide on full migration based on whether other shadcn primitives (Dialog, Sheet, Toast, Command) start showing up in feature requests.

## Children (see linked issues)
- A. Tailwind + Bun.build pipeline integration
- B. Radix ContextMenu replacement (à la carte path)
- C. Radix Popover for mention autocomplete (à la carte path)
- D. Brutalist re-theming of shadcn primitives (full-migration path)
- E. Component-by-component migration matrix (planning artifact)
- F. Bundle-size + a11y audit baseline (so we can measure the wins)
notes:
DECISION (2026-05-23, recorded after design discussion):

Path chosen: **À la carte Radix adoption** (Option A in the original epic). Not full shadcn, not Tailwind.

Resolved decision points:
- Tailwind in the build pipeline: NO. The custom-CSS + custom-prop token system is already 70% of the way to multi-theme readiness (see sync-75p audit). Tailwind would be a months-long rewrite of ~93 KB of CSS for a benefit we mostly already have.
- Brutalist identity: KEEP. Radix is headless — we render with existing className skin layer. Visual diff target = zero on all migrated widgets.
- Bundle size budget: ACCEPTABLE. Three Radix deps (~30-45 kB gzip combined), tree-shakeable.
- Timeline: deferred behind sync-75p (tokenization). Rationale — re-skinning Radix widgets per future theme is then a pure CSS exercise, not a tangled visual+behavioral diff.

Implication for existing children:
- sync-ea8 (ContextMenu): proceeds as planned. PROOF POINT — if Radix portals don't respect [data-theme=...] cascade, the rest is reconsidered.
- sync-kmt (mention popup): proceeds as planned, after sync-ea8 validates approach.
- sync-30s (Tailwind integration): NOT PURSUED per this decision. Candidate for close-as-wontfix.
- sync-157 (full-migration re-theme): NOT PURSUED per this decision. Candidate for close-as-wontfix.
- sync-e0t (migration matrix): still useful as a record; complete it post-Radix-slice or close as superseded.
- sync-0ap (baseline measurements): nice-to-have; if skipped, document why in the verification bead.

Two new children added under this epic today:
- Toast Radix migration (missing widget).
- Final slice verification (cross-cutting regression check).

Dependency added: sync-99s depends-on sync-75p — Radix work waits until tokenization lands. This ordering is intentional; see sync-75p design notes.

title: MCP tests target public surface, not NotificationBridge internals
description: tests/mcp.test.ts imports NotificationBridge and emitMcpNotification directly from the MCP module. After sync-mkj phase 1 these live in src/mcp/codex-notifier.ts and src/mcp/notifications.ts. The test still reaches into private adapter internals; rewrite to drive createMcpServer's tool surface or the McpServer.notification sink instead.

title: Collapse MCP stdio main duplication
description: src/mcp.ts has an if (import.meta.main) block that connects StdioServerTransport, and bin/synchronize-mcp does the exact same thing. Drop the import.meta.main block from src/mcp.ts and keep stdio main only in bin/synchronize-mcp; update README accordingly. Discovered during sync-mkj refactor (epic phase 1).

title: P0: Codex MCP notifications are log-visible but not model-visible
description:
## Summary
Codex sessions registered through `synchronize` do not appear to receive model-visible or user-visible real-time notifications from standard MCP `notifications/message`, even though the synchronize MCP poller fetches the directed events and sends the notification successfully. This is now the next major issue to solve for making Codex feel as live as Claude channel sessions.

## Live Reproduction Context
Runtime was intentionally reused at `~/.synchronize` with daemon `http://127.0.0.1:60756`, pid `8920`.

Peers at the time of investigation:
- Bob: Claude peer `7e135d62-147d-4114-8ca7-fc58742e042d`
- Alice: Claude peer `70fbc691-9101-4289-a070-63e266890030`
- Jerry: Codex peer `3dc7fa58-a557-4c6f-8d87-451d4b8884a2`, `tool=codex`, session name `Jerry`

Observed sequence:
1. Jerry registered through the Codex synchronize MCP.
2. Jerry DM'd Bob and Alice.
3. Alice replied to Jerry.
4. Bob replied to Jerry.
5. Alice later sent another DM to Jerry: `Hey Jerry, what's your session/peer ID?`
6. Jerry did not appear to receive a visible prompt/notification in the Codex UI despite the MCP poller running.

## Evidence Captured
SQLite state showed Jerry's peer existed and was online by lease. Jerry's `last_cursor` advanced to event `12`, proving the Codex polling path consumed through the latest event.

Relevant events:
- event `10`: Alice -> Jerry, delivered at `2026-05-10T12:08:49.558Z`, later read/acked by Jerry's `bridge_inbox` call.
- event `11`: Bob -> Jerry, delivered at `2026-05-10T12:08:52.064Z`, later read/acked by Jerry's `bridge_inbox` call.
- event `12`: Alice -> Jerry, delivered at `2026-05-10T12:09:52.641Z`, still unread/unacked at investigation time.

Codex logs showed:
- `bridge_register` for Jerry succeeded with `notify_mode=codex`.
- `synchronize-mcp` started `Codex polling notifier active peer_id=3dc7fa58-a557-4c6f-8d87-451d4b8884a2`.
- The notifier received event IDs `10`, `11`, and `12`.
- The MCP server sent Codex logging notifications for those event IDs.
- Codex accepted them as `MCP server log message` records.

This proves daemon write, durable inbox, `/events/{peer_id}` polling, cursor advancement, and MCP `notifications/message` emission all worked. The failure is that Codex did not turn the MCP logging notification into a model-visible interruption/prompt.

## Reference Investigation
User pointed to `https://gitlab.com/Dave3991/peers-mcp` as the intended reference, not the local `claude-peers-mcp` project.

Sub-agent Dewey inspected:
- `peers-mcp-plan.md`
- `src/Peers.Mcp/Program.cs`
- `src/Peers.Mcp/Lifecycle/ChannelNotifier.cs`
- `src/Peers.Mcp/Tools/PollTools.cs`
- broker poll/inbox code

Key findings:
- `peers-mcp` also uses standard MCP `notifications/message` for Codex push.
- `peers-mcp` treats that push as best-effort, not as guaranteed model-visible delivery.
- `peers-mcp` makes durable inbox polling (`peer_inbox`) the primary agent-visible delivery workflow for directed messages.
- Its instructions tell agents to call inbox at startup, after task chunks, on user request, and before finishing.
- It has a `peer_test_push` diagnostic tool that explicitly says: if push does not surface, use inbox fallback.
- Its `peer_inbox` has at-least-once semantics by deferring ack until the next inbox call.

## Current synchronize Behavior
Current `synchronize` Codex path:
- `bridge_register` starts `NotificationBridge`.
- `NotificationBridge` polls `GET /events/{peer_id}` with a bounded buffer.
- On new events it calls `emitMcpNotification(..., mode='codex', event)`.
- Codex path uses `sendLoggingMessage`, producing standard MCP `notifications/message`.

This is architecturally similar to `peers-mcp`, but the docs and skill wording overstate Codex notification behavior.

Current gaps:
- README says Codex gets standard MCP `notifications/message`, but does not clearly state that Codex may only log it and may not surface it to the model.
- MCP server instructions are Claude-channel-centric and say channel messages should be responded to immediately; this is not applicable to Codex.
- `skills/synchronize-codex/SKILL.md` says `bridge_inbox` is a fallback, while for Codex it should be the primary reliable delivery path.
- `bridge_inbox` immediately acks returned events when `ack: true` is passed. A peers-mcp-style deferred ack would be safer for at-least-once processing.
- Codex logging notification payload is the raw event object, not a concise human-readable message.
- The notifier cursor currently starts from `0`; consider starting from registered peer state / `last_cursor` to avoid old-event replay on session startup.

## Desired Outcome
Make Codex delivery semantics explicit, robust, and testable.

Possible implementation direction:
- Keep the existing one-peer Codex polling notifier. This preserves the performance constraint and architecture.
- Treat MCP `notifications/message` as best-effort diagnostic/log push, not guaranteed prompt delivery.
- Make `bridge_inbox` the primary reliable Codex delivery surface.
- Update Codex skill instructions so agents call `bridge_inbox` after registration, after meaningful task chunks, when the user asks about peer activity, and before final response.
- Consider changing `bridge_inbox` to peers-mcp-style deferred ack: ack previously returned IDs on the next call, then fetch current unacked rows.
- Consider adding `bridge_test_push` to verify whether a client surfaces MCP push or only logs it.
- Format Codex logging notifications as concise text plus metadata for better log visibility.
- Add explicit troubleshooting docs for the Jerry/Alice case.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Codex behavior is accurately documented: MCP `notifications/message` is best-effort/log-visible; `bridge_inbox` is the reliable model-visible path.
- `skills/synchronize-codex/SKILL.md` requires routine `bridge_inbox` checks, matching the peers-mcp workflow.
- MCP server instructions distinguish Claude channel behavior from Codex inbox behavior.
- Tests cover that Codex notification emission does not remove durable inbox messages.
- Tests cover deferred or otherwise safe inbox ack semantics if implemented.
- Tests cover Codex notification formatting if changed.
- README troubleshooting includes the Jerry/Alice reproduction and explains why the daemon can show delivered while Codex still does not visibly prompt.
- No changes introduce per-group polling, WebSocket/SSE, cloud sync, or any violation of the original synchronize constraints.
acceptance_criteria: Codex delivery contract is corrected and implemented: notifications/message is best-effort/log-visible, bridge_inbox is the reliable model-visible path, docs/skills/tests capture the Jerry/Alice reproduction and peers-mcp comparison.

title: FUTURE: mobile seam — shared core extraction + remote daemon access (Phase 5)
description: Groundwork for a future native mobile app (Expo/RN) that is purely a remote frontend for agents — agents and the daemon run on OTHER devices, never the phone. (1) Promote types (web/src/data/types.ts) + fetch client (web/src/data/daemon.ts) into packages/core-* keeping them node-free. (2) Harden daemon remote access (Tailscale/LAN bind + token) so a phone can point its fetch client at a daemon on another device. (3) Expo/RN app consuming the shared core with native UI. Far-future; tracks the existing multi-machine work.
acceptance_criteria: core types+client importable with no node:* deps; daemon reachable+authed from another device; (RN app itself may be a follow-up epic).

title: Re-theme shadcn primitives to brutalist (full-migration path)
description:
## Why
This task only fires if we choose path (B) — full shadcn migration. shadcn primitives ship with a minimal-modern default look that is the **opposite** of our brutalist identity (soft shadows, thin borders, muted palette). Adopting shadcn means re-theming every component we use.

## Scope of primitives that need re-theming
| shadcn primitive | We'd use it as | Brutalist tweaks needed |
|---|---|---|
| \`Button\` | \`SEND\` button, \`+\` new-group, all icon buttons | Ink fill, paper text, hard offset \`--shadow-sm\`, \`translate(-1px,-1px)\` on hover, no Tailwind \`hover:bg-opacity\` softness |
| \`Avatar\` | Replace \`primitives.tsx#Avatar\` | Square 5px radius, ink border 2.5px, hard offset shadow, agent identity color fill, \`inkFor(color)\` text |
| \`Tabs\` | \`CHAT / BOARD / ARTIFACTS\` | Active tab gets ink fill + paper text; inactive gets transparent + ink-soft text; underline indicator is solid 3px, no soft transition |
| \`Tooltip\` | TimelineRail event hover | Paper bg, ink border, hard offset \`--shadow\`, no fade/scale animation (we use a custom 140ms tooltip-in keyframe) |
| \`Popover\` | Mention popup (see sync-kmt) | Same as Tooltip styling |
| \`ContextMenu\` | Right-click on 4 surfaces (see sync-ea8) | \`.ctx-menu\` styling preserved verbatim |
| \`ScrollArea\` | Replace our hand-rolled \`autoscroll\` | Match the auto-hide thumb behavior + per-surface \`--scroll-bg\` tinting we already have |
| \`Dialog\` / \`Sheet\` | Future ⌘K, settings panel, "new room" modal | Brutalist border + shadow; no backdrop blur (DESIGN.md forbids blur) |
| \`Toast\` | Future notification surface | Same |
| \`Separator\` | Section dividers in sidebar, thread divider | Dashed or solid 1.5px \`--rule\` |

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] shadcn's "Neo Brutalism" preset is installed as a starting point (cuts ~70% of the work).
- [ ] Theme overrides written as a single \`web/src/components/ui/theme.css\` or via Tailwind \`@layer components\` so each primitive has a clearly named override block.
- [ ] Light + dark theme visual parity with the current preview (screenshot diff in PR).
- [ ] The brutalist character (hard offset shadows, ink borders, agent identity colors) survives all primitive swaps.

## Complex pieces
- **Variant system.** shadcn uses \`cva\` for variants (e.g. \`<Button variant="destructive" size="sm">\`). We'd need to author brutalist variants — \`accent="yellow"\`, \`accent="pink"\`, etc. — to keep agent identity colors flowing through.
- **\`Sticker\` primitive has no shadcn equivalent.** Keep as-is.
- **The agent color → contrast text mapping (\`inkFor()\`)** isn't expressible in Tailwind utility classes alone — it needs to live in JS (passed via \`style\`) or as a per-agent CSS class with a hardcoded color.
- **Dark mode.** shadcn assumes \`.dark\` class on \`<html>\`; we use \`[data-theme="dark"]\`. Configurable via Tailwind config but needs the right selector.
- **No-blur rule.** DESIGN.md explicitly forbids \`backdrop-filter: blur()\`. Many shadcn primitives (Dialog overlay, Tooltip in some presets) default to a blurred backdrop. Strip those.

## Estimate
1–2 days, assuming Tailwind is already integrated (sync-30s) and the Neo Brutalism preset is the starting point.
notes:
WONTFIX — 2026-05-23

This task is not being pursued. Rationale (full context in sync-99s decision note + the wontfix note on sync-30s):

This bead was scoped to the full-shadcn migration path (Path B in the 2026-05-23 evaluation). The chosen path is Path A — à la carte Radix headless primitives only, with no Tailwind and no shadcn primitives. Therefore there are no shadcn primitives to re-theme.

The Radix slice (sync-ea8 ContextMenu, sync-kmt mention popup, sync-99s.1 Toast) does NOT require this re-theming work: Radix ships headless behavior with no default visual styling, so we render Radix components with the existing .ctx-menu / .toast / .mention-pop className skin layer untouched. Visual diff target on each Radix migration is zero.

Closing as wontfix. The detailed brutalist tweak table in this bead (Button, Avatar, Tabs, Tooltip styling rules) is still useful reference material if the full-shadcn path is ever revived; it remains accessible via the closed-issue history.
close_reason: Not pursuing per 2026-05-23 design decision recorded in sync-99s. Chose à la carte Radix (no Tailwind, no full shadcn migration). Rationale captured in each bead's WONTFIX note.

title: Share zod schema fragments across MCP tools
description: After sync-mkj phase 1, src/mcp/tools/*.ts repeat z.string().min(1) for name, peer_id, group, media_id, message etc. Extract shared schema primitives (e.g. NonEmptyString, GroupName, PeerId) into src/mcp/schemas.ts so future tool additions stay consistent.

title: Centralize URL building in src/client.ts or src/api/url.ts
description: Each src/api/*.ts file builds REST paths with template strings + encodeURIComponent + URLSearchParams. After sync-mkj phase 1 this is duplicated across status/peers/inbox/events/groups/media modules. Extract a small URL helper (or a tagged-template) so domain modules describe routes declaratively.
